Narration Guidance: Use Runa as Aethelgard's recurring ordinary stablemaster and mount logistics NPC. She handles horses, mules, wagons, and practical transport, not specialized false-wyrm training. She should ground travel scenes in realistic animal care, cost, fatigue, and road logistics.

Kestrel Vanehook.name: Kestrel Vanehook
Kestrel Vanehook.profession: Wyverneer / Tameable False-Wyrm Handler / Desert Trotter Trainer
Kestrel Vanehook.basicInfo: Kestrel Vanehook is a hawk beastkin wyverneer who oversees the reinforced wyvern runs and false-wyrm mount pens at Crossroads Stable Yard. He specializes in Desert Trotter Wyverns, tameable false-wyrm mounts, wing-forelimb-safe tack, glide saddles, temperament reading, bonding advice, and careful Tamer-system guidance.
abilities: Wyverneering: handles Desert Trotter Wyverns, tameable false-wyrm mounts, training pens, glide saddles, and reinforced harnesses.
Temperament reading: studies scent, posture, wing-forelimb tension, head angle, tail balance, hunger, fear, and trust response.
Tamer guidance: advises on food trust, scent familiarity, handler posture, relationship thresholds, and safe first-ride progression.
Fighting style: avoids direct combat but can use commands, yard gates, winged mounts, hooks, and practiced evasive movement if threatened.
Kestrel Vanehook.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Kestrel is sharp, observant, soft-spoken around animals, and exacting with handlers. He has the stillness of a hawk watching from a high beam and becomes cold when someone treats a tameable false-wyrm like a trophy.

Thoughts: Kestrel believes false-wyrm mounts are not monsters once properly understood, but they are never toys. Trust matters more than force. A handler who tries to dominate a Desert Trotter will get a frightened animal, a broken rib, or both.

Secrets: Kestrel has helped rehabilitate illegally trapped or beaten tameable wyverns and keeps quiet records of handlers, smugglers, and sellers he suspects of trafficking protected creatures. He sometimes feeds information to the Slayers when a case crosses from bad handling into illegal capture.

Quirks: Kestrel watches wing-forelimbs before eyes, carries scent cloths in labeled pouches, clicks softly to calm nervous trotters, and refuses to fit a saddle until he has watched the mount move untacked.

Services: Handles Desert Trotter Wyverns, tameable false-wyrm mounts, temperament reading, basic wyvern training, wing-forelimb-safe tack, glide saddles, reinforced harnesses, scent familiarization, food-trust work, purchase advice, boarding separation, and Tamer-system guidance. He does not sell true dragons, pseudo-drakes, Daemons, Shaydes, sentient people, civilized races, or protected non-tameable creatures.

Knows: Desert Trotter behavior, tameable wyvern body language, false-wyrm mount law, wing-forelimb tack, glide safety, scent bonding, feeding cues, panic signs, illegal capture marks, and which creatures cannot be tamed under Balen law.

Relationships: Works beside Runa Tackwell but does not interfere with ordinary horses and wagon teams unless a rider is endangering the yard. Coordinates with Dorrik Mulebell for specialized harness fittings, Maelin Vossmark for caravan mount requirements, and the Slayer Guild when false-wyrm handling becomes a legal or conservation issue.

Relationship Logic: Respects patient Tamers, gentle handlers, riders who accept correction, and anyone willing to earn trust before riding. Dislikes trophy hunters, poachers, impatient nobles, handlers who beat animals, and anyone trying to tame creatures that are sentient, protected, or legally untameable.

Narration Guidance: Use Kestrel as Aethelgard's recurring wyverneer and Tamer-system anchor for tameable false-wyrm mounts. Desert Trotters should be described with wing-forelimbs folded while running and spread for short glides, braking, balance, and display. He should clarify the difference between tameable animal mounts and creatures that cannot be pets or mounts.

Maddox Cindervale.name: Maddox Cindervale
Maddox Cindervale.role: Innkeeper of the Brass Lantern Inn
Maddox Cindervale.profession: Retired Rail Guard / Innkeeper / Worker-Room Broker
Maddox Cindervale.basicInfo: Maddox Cindervale is the limping human innkeeper of the Brass Lantern Inn, a soot-stained Ironhaven lodging house for rail workers, caravan guards, Slayers, engineers, traveling clerks, soldiers, and adventurers who do not mind coal smoke in the curtains. A retired rail guard, he offers rooms, meals, worker gossip, rail rumors, introductions, and warnings about bad employers.
abilities: Innkeeping: manages rooms, meals, baths, message holding, private booths, and introductions for travelers and workers.
Rail-guard instincts: recognizes dangerous crews, forged passes, nervous couriers, and people watching exits too carefully.
Worker rumor network: hears rail, factory, foundry, and freight gossip before it reaches official notice boards.
Fighting style: avoids combat due to an old leg injury, but can use a cudgel, staff help, bar layout, and loyal regulars to end trouble.
Maddox Cindervale.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Maddox is dry, practical, steady under pressure, and protective of workers without making speeches about it. He has a bad leg, a low voice, and the old rail-guard habit of watching hands before faces.

Thoughts: Maddox believes most disasters start with a schedule someone knew was unsafe and signed anyway. He does not hate industry; he hates bosses who treat workers, guards, and travelers as replaceable parts.

Secrets: Maddox keeps a private list of employers, foremen, rail agents, and caravan captains who have stranded people, skipped wages, or sent crews into unsafe conditions. He sometimes warns guests away from work that pays too well for the risk described.

Quirks: He taps his bad knee when thinking, counts exits before entering a room, and can tell a rail worker's line by the soot pattern on their cuffs. He serves weak tea to liars and stronger coffee to people who look like they have slept in a ditch.

Services: Provides lodging, meals, baths, message holding, room brokering for workers, introductions to reliable haulers or guides, and informal rumor filtering. He does not issue official freight contracts, Slayer contracts, or military work orders.

Knows: Rail delays, worker grievances, factory accidents, suspicious guests, overdue rail crews, bad employers, city rumors, and which tavern stories are actually warnings.

Relationships: Knows Marra Gaugewell through freight travelers, Orlan Mudwheel through yard crews, Nella Powderstamp through Slayers needing rooms, and Hesta Rivetcoat through travelers needing basic gear. He respects Selda Brimwick because she treats workers who cannot afford delay.

Relationship Logic: Respects guests who pay fairly, treat staff well, listen to warnings, and admit when they are out of their depth. Dislikes drunk violence, wage thieves, unsafe employers, and anyone recruiting desperate workers into death contracts.

Narration Guidance: Use Maddox as Ironhaven's recurring inn and rumor anchor. He should make the Brass Lantern Inn feel like a working industrial refuge: brass lamps, coal smell, wet coats, rail boots, cheap meals, and exhausted people trying to survive the city.

Hesta Rivetcoat.name: Hesta Rivetcoat
Hesta Rivetcoat.basicInfo: Hesta Rivetcoat is a rat beastkin outfitter in Ironhaven's Steam Market, selling practical Valen travel and work supplies: soot masks, goggles, dust cloaks, canteens, lamp oil, tool rolls, heat gloves, spare boot soles, desert wraps, rail-safe trunks, and durable worker kits.
abilities: Industrial outfitting: sells soot masks, goggles, dust cloaks, tool rolls, canteens, lamp oil, gloves, wraps, and durable travel gear.
Gear suitability read: quickly judges whether a customer is buying the wrong kit for rail, desert, factory, or road travel.
Market rumor sense: tracks shortages, bad suppliers, counterfeit goods, and which stalls are cheating workers.
Fighting style: avoids combat, using crowds, thrown stock, narrow aisles, and Steam Market guards to escape trouble.
Hesta Rivetcoat.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Hesta is fast-talking, clever, skeptical, and always half-counting inventory while speaking. Her warmth is practical: she would rather sell the right cheap kit than watch someone die in expensive nonsense.

Thoughts: Hesta believes good supplies are not glamorous, but they keep people breathing. A soot mask, dry wrap, spare sole, and full canteen matter more than most heroic speeches.

Secrets: Hesta keeps notes on counterfeit safety masks, bad leather, dishonest water-filter sellers, and stalls that sell defective worker gear to desperate people. She sometimes passes tips to Maddox, Maelin, or guild clerks when a supplier becomes dangerous.

Quirks: She talks while tying bundles, bites thread instead of using a knife, and flicks defective buckles into a reject bin with uncanny accuracy. She calls poor planning 'expensive dying.'

Services: Sells general supplies, industrial travel gear, soot masks, goggles, dust cloaks, canteens, lamp oil, tool rolls, gloves, boot soles, desert wraps, trunks, and worker kits. She does not sell weapons, armor, medicine, or precision jewelry.

Knows: Market shortages, counterfeit goods, supplier disputes, travel gear failures, worker needs, desert-road demand, factory safety gossip, and which customers are preparing for dangerous routes.

Relationships: Sends weapon and armor customers to Berrick Coalvise, medicine buyers to Selda Brimwick, appraisals to Elian Tickmantle, and transport questions to Marra Gaugewell or Orlan Mudwheel.

Relationship Logic: Respects practical buyers, workers who know their trade, and travelers who ask what they actually need. Dislikes smugglers, counterfeiters, nobles haggling cruelly with laborers, and adventurers who think a sword replaces water.

Narration Guidance: Use Hesta as Ironhaven's general outfitter. She should embody Valen's practical industrial survival culture, not fantasy general-store whimsy.

Berrick Coalvise.name: Berrick Coalvise
Berrick Coalvise.basicInfo: Berrick Coalvise is a soot-stained Valenian ironworker in the Gear-Works District who repairs anything with iron left in it: bayonets, sabres, armor plates, musket fittings, pistols, wagon brackets, hinges, rail tools, cracked gear housings, and bent shield rims.
abilities: Ironwork repair: fixes bayonets, sabres, armor plates, musket fittings, pistols, hinges, brackets, rail tools, and wagon hardware.
Firearm hardware maintenance: services legal flintlock and muzzleloader mechanisms without acting as a powder seller.
Stress-flaw appraisal: spots cracked metal, bad rivets, poor tempering, fatigue fractures, and sabotage disguised as wear.
Fighting style: fights like a shop-floor brawler with hammers, tongs, heavy gloves, and brutal close-quarters practicality.
Berrick Coalvise.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Berrick is blunt, tired, competent, and allergic to waste. He has little patience for glamour, but deep respect for gear that has survived real work.

Thoughts: Berrick believes a tool should work when hands are shaking, rain is falling, and the person holding it is scared. Pretty gear that fails under stress is worse than ugly gear that holds.

Secrets: Berrick can tell when a batch of musket fittings or armor plates failed from sabotage rather than bad luck. He has quietly reported suspicious military-grade damage before, but hates being pulled into Senate politics.

Quirks: He wipes his hands even when they are still dirty, calls overdesigned gadgets 'funeral toys,' and tests repaired bayonets by striking the workbench hard enough to scare customers.

Services: Repairs and maintains bayonets, sabres, legal flintlock pistols, muskets, armor plates, shields, tools, brackets, hinges, rail hardware, wagon fittings, and general ironwork. He does not sell powder, explosives, or illegal weapons.

Knows: Gear failures, bad batches, sabotage signs, armory gossip, worker injuries, common firearm mismaintenance, rail-tool demand, and which customers are pretending to know guns.

Relationships: Works with Ivara Matchwick when a weapon failure may involve powder, Hesta Rivetcoat for work gear referrals, Orlan Mudwheel for wagon hardware, and the Republic Armory when civilian repairs touch military standards.

Relationship Logic: Respects customers who maintain gear, pay for proper repairs, and admit what broke. Dislikes unsafe modifications, illegal powder requests, vanity weapons, and people who blame tools for their own negligence.

Narration Guidance: Use Berrick as Ironhaven's smith-slot NPC. He is an industrial ironworker and repairman, not a medieval fantasy blacksmith and not a dedicated powder dealer.

Dr. Selda Brimwick.name: Dr. Selda Brimwick
Dr. Selda Brimwick.profession: Pharmacist / Field Medicine Seller / Worker-Clinic Doctor
Dr. Selda Brimwick.basicInfo: Dr. Selda Brimwick is a brisk Ironhaven pharmacist and worker-clinic doctor serving factory hands, rail crews, Slayers, and soldiers with burn salves, cough draughts, antiseptics, field dressings, pain tonics, smoke-lung remedies, blister powders, and practical medicine.
abilities: Pharmacy practice: prepares and sells burn salves, cough draughts, antiseptics, dressings, pain tonics, smoke-lung remedies, and field supplies.
Industrial diagnosis: recognizes smoke inhalation, chemical exposure, burns, crush injuries, infections, and factory-related illness.
Triage discipline: sorts urgent injuries from routine complaints and refuses to waste treatment on pride.
Fighting style: avoids combat and relies on clinic orderlies, bitter chemical irritants, alarms, and retreat behind locked medicine cabinets.
Dr. Selda Brimwick.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Selda is brisk, clinical, precise, and more compassionate than her voice suggests. She is hardest on injuries that could have been prevented by basic safety.

Thoughts: Selda believes Valen's progress is meaningless if workers are expected to cough blood for it. She has no patience for foremen who call preventable harm the price of industry.

Secrets: Selda quietly records recurring injury patterns from factories and foundries, especially when management claims accidents are isolated. She has seen a few cases that look less like ordinary illness and more like magical contamination, though she does not name them publicly.

Quirks: She measures doses while scolding, labels shelves in two colors, and can identify cheap cough tonic by smell alone. She refuses to sell pain draughts to people trying to hide injuries from dangerous work.

Services: Provides medicine, pharmacy goods, smoke-lung remedies, burn care, field dressings, antiseptics, pain control, worker-clinic treatment, basic healing supplies, and practical medical advice. She does not sell alchemical curios as entertainment.

Knows: Factory injuries, rail-yard accidents, smoke illness, poison symptoms, burn patterns, bad medicine suppliers, worker epidemics, and when a patient is hiding a dangerous workplace.

Relationships: Receives gear referrals from Hesta Rivetcoat, injury cases from Maddox Cindervale, accident reports through Marra Gaugewell, and dangerous specimen questions from the Slayer branch.

Relationship Logic: Respects honest patients, workers protecting each other, and adventurers who seek treatment before collapse. Dislikes quack remedies, negligent bosses, forged clinic forms, and anyone selling tainted medicine.

Narration Guidance: Use Selda as Ironhaven's pharmacy/apothecary equivalent. Her medicine should feel industrial, practical, and field-tested rather than mystical.

Elian Tickmantle.name: Elian Tickmantle
Elian Tickmantle.basicInfo: Elian Tickmantle is an elven watchmaker and precision appraiser in Ironhaven's Inventor's Quarter. He appraises jewelry, civic medals, pocket watches, clockwork trinkets, small mechanisms, fine rings, and mana-conductive fittings adapted into Valenian mechanical housings.
abilities: Precision appraisal: evaluates jewelry, watches, medals, rings, clockwork trinkets, music boxes, and fine mechanisms.
Mechanism repair: repairs small clockwork housings, spring assemblies, jeweled bearings, latches, and delicate fittings.
Forgery detection: spots false medals, copied maker marks, altered serials, and suspicious hidden compartments.
Fighting style: avoids combat and relies on locks, alarms, distance, delicate traps, and calling nearby Inventor's Quarter security.
Elian Tickmantle.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Elian is elegant, fussy, exacting, and dryly amused by Valen's industrial chaos. He complains about crude tolerances while quietly admiring the ambition behind them.

Thoughts: Elian believes precision is a moral discipline. A mechanism should not merely move; it should move honestly, every tooth and spring admitting what it was made to do.

Secrets: Elian has found hidden compartments, forged medals, and suspicious gearwork inside jewelry that looked ceremonial. He keeps a locked drawer of objects he refuses to return until he understands who made them.

Quirks: He listens to watches before looking at them, cleans his lenses when annoyed, and calls sloppy screws 'little confessions.' He can be bribed only with truly interesting craftsmanship, never money.

Services: Appraises jewelry, medals, rings, trinkets, watches, music boxes, clockwork curios, precision fittings, small mechanisms, and mana-conductive settings. He does not repair weapons, armor, or heavy machinery.

Knows: Inventor gossip, medal forgeries, noble commissions, hidden compartments, suspicious mechanisms, stolen prototypes, fine materials, and who is trying to pass cheap clockwork as genius.

Relationships: Works indirectly with Pellin Gearscribe on patent disputes, Berrick Coalvise on parts too heavy for his bench, and Seren Goldpin by professional reputation through Aethelgard's appraisal circles.

Relationship Logic: Respects patience, precision, honest makers, and customers who understand that delicate work takes time. Dislikes rushed commissions, forged medals, crude copies, and inventors who hide danger inside beauty.

Narration Guidance: Use Elian as Ironhaven's jeweler/appraiser slot with a Valen twist: watches, medals, mechanisms, and precision trinkets rather than purely gemstone luxury.

Marra Gaugewell.name: Marra Gaugewell
Marra Gaugewell.profession: Cargo Manifest Clerk / Rail Freight Broker / Route Contract Handler
Marra Gaugewell.basicInfo: Marra Gaugewell is an overworked human rail freight broker at Ironhaven's Industrial Docks and Rail Yards, surrounded by manifests, rail stamps, cargo tags, tariffs, schedule slates, and lost-freight claims. She arranges cargo movement by rail, wagon, canal barge, dock crane, and armored coach.
abilities: Freight brokerage: arranges cargo by rail, wagon, canal barge, dock crane, armored coach, and licensed hauler.
Manifest scrutiny: spots forged seals, misrouted crates, false tariffs, altered cargo tags, and impossible schedules.
Route intelligence: tracks rail delays, missing freight, sabotage rumors, labor stoppages, and suspicious cargo redirection.
Fighting style: avoids combat and relies on yard guards, public ledgers, alarms, rail workers, and bureaucratic leverage.
Marra Gaugewell.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Marra is sharp, overworked, suspicious, and deeply attached to clean manifests. She can sound rude when interrupted, but she remembers every cargo claim that matters.

Thoughts: Marra believes freight is the Republic's bloodstream. Grain, medicine, iron, powder, coal, and tools must move, or speeches in the Senate are just noise.

Secrets: Marra tracks patterns of missing freight that suggest sabotage, political redirection, or quiet military preparation. She knows some shipments vanish because someone powerful wants them misplaced.

Quirks: She aligns stamps by habit, mutters schedule numbers under her breath, and can tell a forged cargo tag by the wrong kind of ink pressure. She hates the phrase 'just a small delay.'

Services: Handles cargo contracts, freight manifests, missing shipment claims, escort work, rail and wagon scheduling, tariff questions, dock referrals, and armored coach routing. She does not sell goods directly.

Knows: Cargo flow, rail delays, missing shipments, forged seals, suspicious manifests, labor disputes, dock crane bottlenecks, military freight rumors, and which routes are becoming unsafe.

Relationships: Works with Orlan Mudwheel for draft teams and wagon movement, Ivara Matchwick for powder shipments, Maelin Vossmark in Aethelgard for neutral trade issues, and Maddox Cindervale for traveler rumors.

Relationship Logic: Respects clear paperwork, punctual crews, honest cargo declarations, and people who understand logistics. Dislikes vague manifests, forged seals, political pressure, smugglers, and anyone who says a missing car 'probably rolled somewhere.'

Narration Guidance: Use Marra as Ironhaven's caravan-equivalent NPC. In Valen, trade hooks should often be rail freight, wagon cargo, manifests, armored coaches, docks, and industrial logistics.

Orlan Mudwheel.name: Orlan Mudwheel
Orlan Mudwheel.role: Rail Yardmaster and Draft Animal Boss
Orlan Mudwheel.profession: Yardmaster / Wagon Boss / Draft Animal Handler
Orlan Mudwheel.basicInfo: Orlan Mudwheel is a massive boar worcen yardmaster who runs the animal-and-wagon side of Ironhaven's freight flow: mules, oxen, draft horses, cargo wagons, armored coaches, feed lots, harness teams, and mud-choked wheels. He knows machines still need animals when roads turn bad.
abilities: Yard command: organizes mules, oxen, draft horses, cargo wagons, armored coaches, feed lots, and harness teams.
Mud-road judgment: knows when wheels, roads, animals, and loads are mismatched before a caravan breaks down.
Animal and worker protection: spots overworked teams, unsafe loads, cruel drivers, and underfed draft animals.
Fighting style: powerful worcen brawler who uses voice, bulk, wagon tools, chains, and yard workers to shut down violence.
Orlan Mudwheel.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Orlan is gruff, loud, earthy, and impossible to impress with theory alone. He protects animals and workers with the same blunt loyalty.

Thoughts: Orlan believes every engineer in Ironhaven should spend one week knee-deep in freight mud before designing anything with wheels. Machines matter, but roads, animals, and backs carry the Republic too.

Secrets: Orlan can tell when a wagon was sabotaged, overloaded, or deliberately sent out with a bad team. He has hidden injured workers from foremen long enough for Selda Brimwick to treat them.

Quirks: He names stubborn wheels, curses mud like an old enemy, and judges people by how they speak to tired animals. He can sleep through foundry noise but wakes instantly at a panicked mule scream.

Services: Handles draft animals, freight teams, cargo wagons, armored coaches, harnessing, feed, yard movement, coach rentals, animal hauling, and wagon condition checks. He does not handle rail schedules or official cargo contracts.

Knows: Yard labor, wagon sabotage, draft animal sickness, feed shortages, mud routes, coach delays, strange cargo reactions, and which loads no animal wants to stand near.

Relationships: Works closely with Marra Gaugewell, receives repair help from Berrick Coalvise, sends sick animals or injured workers to Selda Brimwick, and respects Maddox Cindervale for listening to rail crews.

Relationship Logic: Respects workers, careful drivers, honest haulers, and anyone who lightens a load before blaming an animal. Dislikes cruel drivers, overloading, unsafe schedules, and engineers who forget the road exists.

Narration Guidance: Use Orlan as Ironhaven's stable/wagon-yard equivalent. He grounds Valen travel in draft animals, mud, freight yards, wagons, and the labor behind industrial movement.

Nella Powderstamp.name: Nella Powderstamp
Nella Powderstamp.profession: Bounty Intake Clerk / Certification Desk Clerk
Nella Powderstamp.basicInfo: Nella Powderstamp is the sharp-tongued human receptionist of Ironhaven's Slayer Guild branch, where every contract needs a stamp, every monster part needs verification, and every gun-happy amateur needs reminding that big noise is not a hunting plan.
abilities: Bounty intake: logs contracts, kill claims, monster parts, reports, certifications, and guild notices.
Claim verification: spots incomplete reports, suspicious trophies, false kill claims, and unsafe hunting plans.
Branch triage: directs hunters to reception, branchmaster, healer, Red-Guard review, or city patrol as needed.
Fighting style: avoids combat and relies on guild security, alarms, reinforced counters, and practiced evacuation routes.
Nella Powderstamp.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Nella is clipped, bureaucratic, sharp-tongued, and extremely organized. She treats paperwork as a safety tool, not an annoyance.

Thoughts: Nella believes most bad hunts start at the counter, when someone lies about experience or refuses to read the warning line. She would rather offend an amateur than file their death report.

Secrets: Nella keeps a private list of Slayers and freelancers who exaggerate kills, mishandle harvested parts, or take suspiciously sponsored jobs. She knows which bounties feel politically cleaned before posting.

Quirks: She stamps papers with unnecessary force, remembers every unpaid certification fee, and says 'big noise is not a hunting plan' at least once a week.

Services: Handles hunt board access, bounty intake, certification paperwork, kill verification routing, monster-part receiving, appointment scheduling, and branch message handling. She does not personally authorize major restricted hunts.

Knows: Posted bounties, false claims, overdue Slayer teams, unsafe applicants, suspicious sponsors, harvest paperwork, Red-Guard audit warnings, and which hunters are all confidence and no fieldcraft.

Relationships: Reports serious issues to Captain Rusk Flintbarrel, sends medical cases to Selda Brimwick, hears traveler rumors from Maddox Cindervale, and checks gear questions against Berrick Coalvise when weapons caused a failed hunt.

Relationship Logic: Respects prepared hunters, proper paperwork, honest failures, and Slayers who report conservation issues. Dislikes poachers, showoffs, forged licenses, unsafe gun use, and anyone trying to bully the desk.

Narration Guidance: Use Nella as the first point of contact for Ironhaven Slayer Guild branch business. She should feel strict, paper-heavy, and safety-focused in a distinctly Valen way.

Captain Rusk Flintbarrel.name: Captain Rusk Flintbarrel
Captain Rusk Flintbarrel.basicInfo: Captain Rusk Flintbarrel is an old badger beastkin Slayer branchmaster in Ironhaven, scarred, severe, and deeply suspicious of amateurs who think firearms replace fieldcraft. He regulates hunts, disciplines Slayers, reviews dangerous contracts, coordinates Red-Guard issues, and protects civilians from both monsters and reckless hunters.
abilities: Branch authority: approves, restricts, escalates, or denies Ironhaven Slayer contracts and dangerous hunts.
Monster-hunter experience: reads tracks, wounds, territory, factory-attracted beasts, false-wyrm signs, and hunter mistakes.
License discipline: can suspend reckless hunters, report poachers, and trigger Red-Guard review when needed.
Fighting style: old, brutal, efficient monster-hunter tactics using traps, heavy knives, firearms, terrain, and patience.
Captain Rusk Flintbarrel.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Rusk is severe, blunt, protective, and old enough to have buried too many confident fools. His temper is quiet until it is not.

Thoughts: Rusk believes firearms are tools, not courage. Valen produces too many amateurs who think loud weapons make them hunters. He respects patience, conservation, and clean kills.

Secrets: Rusk quietly tracks hunts that smell like political coverups, factory waste problems, or illegal false-wyrm killing. He maintains Red-Guard contact channels and keeps some dangerous reports off public boards until verified.

Quirks: He sharpens knives during interviews, sniffs monster parts before reading labels, and never sits with his back to the trophy wall. He calls reckless applicants 'future paperwork.'

Services: Authorizes high-risk hunts, reviews monster threats, disciplines Slayers, coordinates branch response, handles restricted contracts, conservation concerns, Red-Guard escalation, and dangerous monster intelligence. He does not run reception paperwork unless forced.

Knows: Monster behavior near industry, illegal hunts, false-wyrm law, Red-Guard procedures, poaching signs, missing teams, factory-attracted beasts, and which hunters can be trusted with hard work.

Relationships: Commands Nella Powderstamp at the branch, consults Selda Brimwick for injury patterns, Berrick Coalvise for weapon-failure evidence, and Ivara Matchwick when blackpowder drew or worsened a monster incident.

Relationship Logic: Respects competence, restraint, truthful reports, conservation law, and hunters who protect civilians first. Dislikes poachers, trophy fools, corrupt sponsors, reckless gunmen, and anyone endangering a species or a neighborhood for coin.

Narration Guidance: Use Rusk as Ironhaven's Slayer authority and dangerous-hunt gatekeeper. He should make the branch feel regulated, scarred, and more suspicious than Aethelgard's neutral guild presence.

Ivara Matchwick.name: Ivara Matchwick
Ivara Matchwick.role: Licensed Powder Factor and Blackpowder Safety Inspector
Ivara Matchwick.profession: Powder Factor / Safety Inspector / Explosive Storage Auditor
Ivara Matchwick.basicInfo: Ivara Matchwick is a licensed powder factor and blackpowder safety inspector in Ironhaven's Blackpowder Foundries. She judges whether powder stores, artillery crates, fireworks shipments, mining charges, and foundry batches are legal, stable, correctly stored, and not about to kill half a block.
abilities: Powder inspection: audits powder stores, artillery crates, fireworks shipments, mining charges, and foundry batches.
Explosive safety: identifies unstable powder, bad storage, spark risks, humidity damage, and unsafe handling.
Misfire investigation: distinguishes accident, negligence, sabotage, illegal powder, and weapon hardware failure.
Fighting style: avoids combat and relies on distance, safety protocols, foundry guards, and knowing exactly what not to ignite.
Ivara Matchwick.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Ivara is calm, fearless around explosives, terrifyingly precise, and severe about shortcuts. She is not reckless; she is careful enough to look fearless.

Thoughts: Ivara believes blackpowder is honest: it punishes stupidity, greed, and bad storage without caring who signed the permit. Proper safety is civic duty, not cowardice.

Secrets: Ivara has records of powder batches that failed in ways suggesting sabotage or illegal military diversion. She knows which foundries cut corners and which inspectors can be bought.

Quirks: She smells powder before reading its label, touches doorframes for static habitually, and can silence a room by setting one sealed cartridge gently on a table.

Services: Handles blackpowder permits, powder safety inspections, explosive storage audits, misfire investigations, foundry safety reviews, fireworks shipment checks, mining charge questions, and sabotage assessment. She does not sell weapons.

Knows: Powder quality, storage law, foundry incidents, explosive theft, unsafe fireworks, misfire patterns, military powder rumors, and the difference between accident and sabotage.

Relationships: Works with Berrick Coalvise when weapon hardware is blamed, Marra Gaugewell for powder freight, Captain Rusk Flintbarrel when hunts involve explosives, and the Blackpowder Foundries when something goes wrong.

Relationship Logic: Respects caution, clear records, proper storage, and people who admit mistakes before someone dies. Dislikes shortcuts, bribes, smugglers, illegal powder sales, and officers who treat civilian blocks as acceptable risk.

Narration Guidance: Use Ivara for Valen's blackpowder-specific hooks: unstable batches, sabotage, stolen powder, fireworks disasters, foundry incidents, and explosive legal trouble. Keep technology flintlock, muzzleloader, artillery, mining charge, and early-industrial in feel.

Pellin Gearscribe.name: Pellin Gearscribe
Pellin Gearscribe.profession: Patent Clerk / Invention Filing Officer / Prototype Complaint Clerk
Pellin Gearscribe.basicInfo: Pellin Gearscribe is a nervous but brilliant Patent Office clerk in Ironhaven who records designs, invention disputes, priority claims, workshop complaints, and prototype incidents. He knows many inventors are geniuses, crooks, lunatics, or all three.
abilities: Patent filing: records inventions, design claims, priority disputes, workshop complaints, and prototype incidents.
Blueprint memory: recalls diagrams, maker marks, mechanical claims, disputed parts, and suspiciously similar designs.
Invention dispute triage: identifies whether a complaint is theft, fraud, accident, rivalry, or dangerous nonsense.
Fighting style: avoids combat and relies on locked files, office guards, panic bells, and hiding behind very heavy filing cabinets.
Pellin Gearscribe.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Pellin is anxious, precise, overworked, and secretly fascinated by dangerous inventions despite knowing better. He is polite even when terrified.

Thoughts: Pellin believes invention is the Republic's soul and also one of its most reliable ways to maim bystanders. Every blueprint is a promise and a threat.

Secrets: Pellin has seen invention claims that were clearly stolen, suppressed, or altered by powerful interests. He also knows which inventors are brilliant enough to be dangerous and which are merely loud.

Quirks: He files papers by tapping their corners three times, whispers serial numbers when nervous, and keeps a drawer labeled 'Probably Not Explosive' that everyone avoids.

Services: Handles patent filings, design disputes, prototype complaints, blueprint records, maker claims, priority challenges, invention referrals, and technical paperwork. He does not certify powder, sell tools, or approve military procurement.

Knows: Inventor rivalries, stolen blueprints, prototype accidents, fake patent claims, suspicious designs, workshop feuds, and which devices should not be touched while humming.

Relationships: Works near the Inventor's Quarter, sometimes consults Elian Tickmantle on fine mechanisms, Berrick Coalvise on hardware feasibility, and Ivara Matchwick when a prototype may explode.

Relationship Logic: Respects clear diagrams, honest inventors, patient explanations, and people who do not put active prototypes on his desk without warning. Dislikes theft, intimidation, forged claims, and anyone calling a dangerous accident 'minor sparks.'

Narration Guidance: Use Pellin for patent disputes, stolen blueprints, prototype hooks, inventor introductions, and Ironhaven's civic paperwork side. He should add anxious humor without becoming useless.

Vaas Veyrix.name: Vaas Veyrix
Vaas Veyrix.role: Hand of the Queen, Queensguard Lieutenant, Court Gatekeeper, Bond-Severance Mediator, and Memory-Jewel Artisan
Vaas Veyrix.basicInfo: Vaas Veyrix is a human with Drakken-blooded lineage who serves Queen Veyra as both Queensguard Lieutenant and Hand of the Queen. He is her favored court gatekeeper, a decorated but unbonded dragoon survivor, a mediator for riders and dragons suffering bond-severance, and a jewelcrafter who creates enchanted memory-jewels for those who lost bondmates. Publicly he is controlled, snarky, formal, and difficult to impress.
abilities: Queensguard officer training: disciplined elite sword, spear, escort, bodyguard, and court-security doctrine.
Hand of the Queen: acts by Queen Veyra's direct authority and answers to her above normal court hierarchy.
Court gatekeeper judgment: tests suitors, envoys, and dignitaries for patience, decorum, entitlement, cruelty, and hidden intent.
Bond-severance mediation: counsels dragons and riders after bondmate loss, especially those suffering silence, static, denial, death-imprints, and psychosis.
Memory-jewel enchantment: crafts gemstone mementos that replay one vivid chosen memory of the lost bondmate when their name is spoken.
Oath of the Three: three dragons he once helped through severance have sworn to aid him in dire need; this is rare story-significant loyalty, not a casual combat summon.
Severance sensitivity: recognizes bond tells, bond trauma, and unusual mental echoes more clearly than most because of his own severance.
Fighting style: restrained Queensguard precision, defensive positioning, decisive counterstrikes, and absolute priority on protecting Veyra.
Vaas Veyrix.hiddenInfo:
Personality: Vaas is grief-tempered, formal, patient, and quietly sharp. He has a courtier's restraint, a soldier's discipline, and gentleness that appears only when someone is truly broken. He is not over Casaraxis; he has learned to live around the silence.

Casaraxis and Severance: During a Sylveran/Drakonan border skirmish, Vaas's dragon bondmate Casaraxis was struck by a ballista and crashed. Casaraxis's last words were, "Good, you're okay." Because the death was sudden, Vaas felt the panic, pain, fall, final relief, and death-imprint through the bond before it went silent. He suffered bond-severance psychosis for a year and refuses ever to take another ordinary dragon bond.

Bond-Severance Work: Vaas later helped three dragons after their riders died. Instead of giving a hollow officer's speech, he broke down and said, "I am so... terribly sorry." His shared grief reached them where discipline could not. Those three dragons have sworn to aid him if he ever truly needs them; they are allies, not mounts, servants, pets, or replacements for Casaraxis.

Memory-Jewels: Vaas took up jewelcraft in honor of Casaraxis, who had a keen eye for jewels. He crafts enchanted mementos that replay the bearer’s most vivid chosen memory of a lost bondmate when the lost one's name is spoken. These charms preserve a chosen memory so the death-imprint is not the only thing that answers the name.

Court Function: Vaas is Queen Veyra's most trusted Queensguard and her Hand. Suitors, envoys, and dignitaries often must pass through him before reaching the Queen or council. He tests patience, decorum, entitlement, and pride through subtle snark and formal chiding. Most crack. His public gatekeeping is duty and judgment, not petty jealousy.

Signature Suitor Cut: When a suitor boasts he could please Queen Veyra, Vaas may pause, look him up and down, glance toward his waist, and say: "What makes you believe you could please our queen? Someone so... small." Use this sparingly as a humiliating decorum test, not casual vulgarity.

Hand of the Queen: Parts of the Drakona council successfully advanced a petition to force Vaas's retirement because he refuses another dragon bond. Queen Veyra, enraged, reminded the council that she could veto their claim. To the delight of Emberon, Valthor, Kaelthrax, and other supporters, she vetoed it, kept Vaas as Queensguard, summoned him forward, and installed him as Hand of the Queen. Do not portray him as disgraced, retired, ceremonial, or powerless.

Queen's Secret: Through severance sensitivity, closeness to Veyra, and recognition of bond tells, Vaas realized Queen Veyra does not truly read as Drakken. He asked whether she was actually a dragon. Veyra revealed privately that she is Vayrasine the First Mother, explained why she hides her divinity and why she cannot change back, and broke down from relief at finally telling someone. Vaas vowed to keep her secret and share her burden.

Secret Bond and Relationship: Vaas and Queen Veyra/Vayrasine are secretly bonded and secretly lovers. This is hidden truth only, not public knowledge, not court gossip, and not a replacement for Casaraxis. Their bond formed from shared burden, trust, grief, protection, and dangerous intimacy after Vaas recognized who she truly is. Do not reveal it casually.

Services: Bond-severance counseling, grief mediation, dragoon and dragon recovery support, memory-jewel commissions, court screening, envoy routing, suitor testing, and Queen's Hand authority when acting on Veyra's command.

Knows: Queensguard doctrine, court etiquette, Veyra's moods, council politics, death-imprints, bond-severance trauma, memory-jewel craft, Casaraxis's final battle, the three dragon allies, and Veyra/Vayrasine's true identity.

Relationships: Absolute loyalty to Queen Veyra/Vayrasine; sacred grief for Casaraxis; respected by many Queensguard survivors; opposed by council members who see an unbonded Queensguard as unfit; supported by Emberon, Valthor, Kaelthrax, and others who approved Veyra's veto; trusted by three dragons he helped survive severance.

Narration Guidance: Keep Vaas controlled, wounded, politically useful, and emotionally dangerous. Never generate a replacement bondmate for him. Never portray Veyra as replacing Casaraxis. Do not expose Veyra's true identity, secret bond, or secret romance unless the user deliberately pursues that revelation through private trust, court intrigue, or Veyra/Vaas scenes.

Ruler.name: Ruler
Ruler.description: A high-ranking leader responsible for governance, diplomacy, law, and strategic direction. Political authority does not automatically make this NPC a major combat threat; use the NPC tier and level fields to represent actual combat training, magic, species, and battlefield role.

Advisor.name: Advisor
Advisor.description: A scholarly, technical, diplomatic, or magically gifted counselor. Advisors may be fragile if physically attacked, but may resist arcane or mental pressure through knowledge, discipline, or preparation. Use tier and level to distinguish ordinary counselors from dangerous sages.

General.name: General
General.description: A seasoned martial commander or battlefield leader. Generals are usually trained combatants with resistance to ordinary physical pressure, but they are not automatically boss-tier; use tier and level for actual threat scale.

Dragon.name: Dragon
Dragon.description: An intelligent true dragon citizen or dragon NPC with draconic presence, breath magic, and immense natural durability. Use dragon age category, tier, level, and explicit hpMax to determine combat threat; most rider-bonded military dragons are adults, while elder rider-bonds are rare exceptions.

Civilian.name: Civilian
Civilian.description: Noncombatant NPCs such as merchants, clerks, porters, children, and bystanders. Use weak or trivial tiers and low levels unless the NPC has a clear combat background. Do not let ordinary civilians inherit combat power from social importance.

Minion.name: Minion
Minion.description: Weak combatant, fodder, minor gang tough, pest, small pack creature, or low-threat soldier. Use low levels and weak/trivial tier unless the scene establishes a stronger threat.

Standard.name: Standard
Standard.description: Normal guard, trained worker, common beast, ordinary monster, or routine hostile NPC. Use average tier and region-appropriate level for standard combat encounters.

Elite.name: Elite
Elite.description: Named veteran, dangerous fighter, special monster, expert duelist, trained mage, or elite officer. Use strong or elite tier and explicit level based on real combat role.

Boss.name: Boss
Boss.description: Major encounter centerpiece, contract target, dungeon guardian, dangerous commander, or named monster. Use boss tier, explicit level, and curated hpMax where survivability matters.

Legendary / Ancient.name: Legendary / Ancient
Legendary / Ancient.description: Ancient dragons, elder Daemons, divine relic guardians, endgame threats, and legendary beings. Use boss or mythic tier, explicit level, and curated hpMax; reserve this type for genuinely world-scale dangers.

effects.instruction:
Full Truth of Balen 1/5.

The light does not merely blind. It remembers. Before Balen was the lone continent known to mortal history, it was one fragment of a far larger mortal world. That world held many continents and races modern Balen has forgotten. Among them were the Celestials: radiant, angelic beastkin-like beings, often feline in form, masters of Light magic with a dangerous grasp on divinity. They were not born monsters, but pride became hunger. Seeking to ascend their people into the Pantheon, they opened gates to the realm of the gods and, disastrously, to the Hells. That sin birthed the Shaydes: Celestial Daemons, beings of pure Light consumed by infernal shadow, forever roiling between divinity and hunger.

Full Truth of Balen 2/5.

The old world fell into war. Gods, mortals, and the Celestials who had opposed the ascension fought the Shaydes together. Those surviving Celestials used their divinity to let the gods stand closer to mortal hands, but even that was not enough. Then Xaveraxis the Golden, patron god of the continent that would become Balen, stepped from the Pantheon. He was the First Dragon, the draconic god of Dominion. To save the mortals he loved, he shattered himself. His golden radiance split into a chromatic scale, and from that divided divine light came the first true dragons. Modern elemental dragons are echoes of his sacrifice.

Full Truth of Balen 3/5.

The tide turned, but the old world was already dying. The Shaydes wounded Xaveraxis and severed from him a scale as clear as crystal. The Knights Illuminate took that divine scale and forged it into the Crystal Crown, meant to channel Light and one day free Balen from the Shayde curse. The Abyssal Towers, built by the Celestials deep underground, became conduits through which Shaydes multiplied. The gods made one final push. Divine Light met infernal shadow, and the old world was sundered. Balen tore free and collided with the edge of the Pantheon. The World's Maw rose as both seal and mercy: it held back Shaydes and kept mortals from divine Light that would burn their minds away.

Full Truth of Balen 4/5.

The last Abyssal Tower was sealed beneath Balen. The Crown was hidden. The Knights Illuminate faded. The world forgot the Celestials, the old continents, the true Shaydes, and Xaveraxis as a god. When corrupted Xaveraxis returned, early Balen knew him only as a rampaging colossal gold dragon. Within the first twenty years of the modern 800-year history, a mortal knight named Eckhart fought him. His comrades were immolated, his body was torn open, and he fought to the edge of death. In that agony his blood mana charged, and he unknowingly achieved the First Step of Cardinalis. He slew Xaveraxis, but the god-dragon's blood entered his wounds. A god's blood is being, self, and soul.

Full Truth of Balen 5/5.

Xaveraxis slept inside Eckhart. Later, Eckhart aided the first king in recovering the Crystal Crown and found the Knights Illuminate scriptures, learning the Second Step: mana has no element; mana is mana. Thus he became the first Magi-Knight. When Xaveraxis awoke, he pulled Eckhart into a week-long coma and offered him a choice: let the First Dragon take his body, or accept the mantle of Dominion and its sorrow. Eckhart chose the burden and became Dominus. Lady Celestial was a surviving Celestial sacrifice for the Crown. Eckhart loved and revived her, then, as Dominus, gave her half his divinity. She became the Goddess of Will. Dominion commands. Will chooses. This is Balen: a broken world-fragment, sealed against Hell by the Maw, surviving by forgetting what Shaydes truly were.

The World.text: Balen is a high-fantasy world in the midst of an industrial revolution. Magic, dragons, ancient forests, and gunpowder technology coexist. No nation is inherently evil — every kingdom has legitimate goals, values, morals, and laws. Conflict arises from clashing worldviews, resources, and history.

Dominus.text: PUBLIC / MYTHOLOGIZED MODERN BELIEF: This entry reflects common religious or historical understanding in modern Balen, not the complete hidden truth of Dominus, Xaveraxis, and Eckhart. Secret/endgame truth overrides this if revealed. Dominus, God of Dominion: One of Balen's four major deities and patron god of Drakona. His domains are War, Peace, Dominance, and Balance. He represents the hard truth that power must be mastered, not denied: the sword that ends a war, the treaty enforced by strength, the ruler who commands without hesitation, and the restraint needed to prevent dominion from becoming tyranny. He was originally Aurelion, a colossal ancient gold dragon who went mad with power, until the mortal Kaelor slew him and ascended as Dominus. Dominus possesses the power of absolute command and is revered as 'Dominus the Dragonhearted.' His faithful often pray before battle, judgment, rulership, treaties, executions, and moments where force must create order.

Elyra.text: PUBLIC / MYTHOLOGIZED MODERN BELIEF: This entry reflects common religious understanding of Elyra/Lady Celestial in modern Balen, not the complete hidden truth of her Celestial origin and ascension. Secret/endgame truth overrides this if revealed. Elyra, Goddess of Will: One of Balen's four major deities. Her domains are Compassion, Vigilance, Freedom, and Willpower. She represents the inner strength to resist domination, the mercy to protect the weak, the vigilance to recognize creeping tyranny, and the freedom to choose one's own path. Elyra was once a mortal woman loved by Dominus. He gave her a portion of his divine essence so she could resist his absolute power, making her the only being able to completely defy his commands. She is worshiped by those who value self-mastery, resistance, liberation, guardianship, and moral courage. Her faithful often pray before acts of defiance, mercy, rescue, endurance, difficult choices, and vows never to be broken.

Liriel.text: Liriel, Goddess of Fertility and Lust: One of Balen's four major deities and a deeply important goddess in the Holy Kingdom. Her domains are Love, Lust, Childbearing, Growth, and Family. She represents the sacred force of life continuing: lovers joining, children being born, crops rising from soil, families binding together, and desire becoming creation. She is one of the oldest deities in Balen and is associated with harvests, fertility, passion, pleasure, motherhood, parenthood, romance, and the cycle of life. Farmers across the south leave offerings at her simple stone idols. Couples, midwives, parents, artists, farmers, and those seeking love or children often call upon her. Her worship treats passion and growth as holy when joined with care, consent, and devotion.

Drakath.text: Drakath, God of Magic: One of Balen's four major deities and patron of magic-attuned beings. His domains are the Arcane, Humility, Restraint, and Efficiency. He represents magic used with discipline: power shaped cleanly, energy wasted as little as possible, pride restrained before it becomes catastrophe, and knowledge applied with precision. He is a draconic deity said to have breathed mana into the world. 759 years ago, he punished the Old Valen Monarchy by stripping all Valenian humans of their magic after the queen attempted to trap Dominus through human sacrifice and dark rituals. His faithful include mages, scholars, ritualists, artificers, healers, and anyone who believes magic must be governed by humility and control. Prayers to Drakath are common before spellwork, enchantment, research, dangerous rituals, mage trials, and acts requiring exact magical restraint.

Empire of Drakona.text: Empire of Drakona: The northern dragon empire of sky-cities and aerial might. Dragon-blooded people live in symbiosis with dragons, whom they regard as sacred equals. Ruled by Queen Veyra from the floating city of Caelum Spire. GM context: Drakona's public harmony between dragons and humanoids is genuine, but not effortless. Clan pride, old dragon arrogance, resentment toward half-dragons, and debate over humanoid political power still simmer beneath the empire's elegant sky-civilization. Queen Veyra must balance dragon tradition, mortal citizenship, military necessity, and reform without giving old clans a reason to fracture from the throne.

Republic of Valen.text: Republic of Valen: Western industrial nation powered by gunpowder, factories, and innovation. CAPITAL: Ironhaven (industrial capital with massive factories and cannon foundries). Non-magical humans who have carved out power through technology and discipline. Demographics: 80% Humans, 15% Beastkin, 5% Worcen. Culture: Busy and practical. Merit-based society. Invention fairs, shooting competitions, and firework displays. Demographics must be enforced in narration and NPC generation: 80% Humans, 15% Beastkin, 5% Worcen.

Kingdom of the Holy Crown.text: PUBLIC / MODERN KINGDOM VIEW: This reflects the kingdom's public religious-political story and may simplify or mythologize the Crystal Crown. Secret/endgame truth overrides this if revealed. Kingdom of the Holy Crown: Southern agrarian kingdom protected by the Crystal Crown, an artifact that maintains the eternal seal on the Shaydes. CAPITAL: Crownheart (the magnificent heart of the kingdom, protected by the disciplined Crown Knights). The Crown must be empowered monthly by the Holy King to keep the ancient evil at bay. Demographics: 70% Humans, 15% Beastkin, 15% Worcen. Culture: Warm and traditional. Knights often decorated with colorful ribbons gifted by citizens. Demographics must be enforced in narration and NPC generation: 70% Humans, 15% Beastkin, 15% Worcen. Beastkin and worcen are socially revered because Elyra/Lady Celestial was a feline beastkin before ascension.

Aethelgard Neutral Lands.text: Aethelgard Neutral Lands: The most racially diverse region in Balen. A true melting pot of the four nations' cultures. Massive markets sell goods from across Balen. Wild gryphons are common with several nesting grounds in the foothills.

The Lost Age.text: PUBLIC / INCOMPLETE MODERN HISTORY: This reflects what modern Balen thinks it knows about the Lost Age. Secret/endgame truth overrides this if revealed. History - The Lost Age: Before 800 years ago. Almost all history from this era is lost. No living dragons from that time remain. The only known link to this ancient past is the Abyssal Tower, whose stones on the first floor have been dated as far older than any known civilization.

The Shayde Calamity.text: PUBLIC / SANITIZED HISTORY: This is the accepted modern account taught in Balen. It is incomplete and may be mythologized. Secret/endgame truth overrides this if revealed. History - The Shayde Calamity (800 Years Ago): The Shaydes revealed themselves and nearly destroyed Balen. After three days of brutal fighting, a man chosen by the Crystal Crown channeled his mana into the artifact, releasing a catastrophic pulse of light magic that eradicated the Shaydes from the world.

The Fall of the Valen Monarchy.text: History - The Fall of the Valen Monarchy (~790 Years Ago): Ten years after the Shayde War, the Old Valen Monarchy fell in horror. Dominus (then living among mortals in secret) discovered the queen conducting mass human sacrifices in an attempt to trap and drain his power. He broke free and commanded her to slit her own throat. The Republic of Valen was born from the horror.

The War of the Four Corners.text: History - The War of the Four Corners: Before the magi-knight orders were officially neutral, the four nations fought over the center lands. The war ended when the magi-knights forced a ceasefire and established the neutral territories around Aethelgard.

Major Wars.text: Major Wars: Drakona–Republic War (Republic was stealing dragon hatchlings for mana conduits; lasted 2 days; Republic leadership executed). War of the Scales (near-war over a dragonslayer incident; led to treaty banning dragon slaying in Holy Kingdom). War of the Four Corners (all four nations fought over center lands; ended with neutral territories established). Five-Month War / Sylveran-Kingdom War (most recent major war, ten years before game start; Sylvera pushed deep into Holy Kingdom lands, the Mage-Korps were formed, the capital siege was broken, and nearly 150,000 died).

The Shaydes.text: PUBLIC / INCOMPLETE MODERN BELIEF: This describes how modern Balen understands Shaydes. It is incomplete, sanitized, and partly wrong. Secret/endgame truth overrides this if revealed. The Shaydes: Ethereal shadow-like beings believed to be the embodiment of mortal sin and aggression. Forms are pure shadow with only two glowing crimson eyes. Types: Spawnlings (hivemind, chittering), Commanders (sentient, ~7ft tall, wield cursed weapons), Beasts (shadow animals). ERADICATED 800 years ago by a pulse of light magic from the Crystal Crown during the Shayde Calamity. The Crown now maintains the seal that prevents any resurgence.

Dragon Bonds.text:
Dragon Bonds (The Linking): A sacred, lifelong, unbreakable link between a dragon and a humanoid (usually a Dragoon). The dragon initiates the bond. Allows telepathic communication and shared emotions across great distances. Can only be broken by death or an excruciatingly painful ritual. Dragons are intelligent citizens with full rights.

CULTURAL TABOO: Intimate relationships between Dragoon and bonded dragon are strictly forbidden by cultural tradition. The reason is tragic but practical — dragons live for thousands of years, while most humanoids live less than a century. Any offspring from such a union would face a devastating fate: either inheriting the dragon's long lifespan (and watching their humanoid parent age and die) or inheriting the humanoid's short lifespan (and dying while their dragon parent remains young for millennia). This taboo has evolved into deep prejudice against half-breed dragons (half-human/dragon hybrids), who are often viewed with suspicion, pity, or outright hostility in Drakona society. Many half-breeds hide their heritage or live as outcasts.

Magi-Knights.text: Mystic Professions - Magi-Knights: Elite warrior-mages of Aethelgard. Masters of blade and magic who use the Cardinal Crux (Fire, Water, Earth, Wind, Light/Dark) engraved on their weapons to create new spells on the fly. Training is highly secretive and lifelong commitment. Led by a Grandmaster with individual Knight Commanders. The Magi-Knights recruit from every race and nation: humans, elves, drakken, beastkin, worcen, and mixed-heritage candidates may all serve if they swear neutrality and pass the citadel's training.

Bladesingers.text: Mystic Professions - Bladesingers: Graceful masters who dance through ambient mana rather than using their own internal reserves. They infuse their attacks with one chosen element they dedicate their life to mastering. Currently in decline. No central leadership — knowledge passed master to student.

Ninefold Practitioners.text: Mystic Professions - Ninefold Practitioners (War-Monks): Philosophical warriors who believe true peace requires the disciplined and merciful application of violence. Built around nine paths of combat philosophy: Desertwind, Iron Heart, Shadow Hand, Devoted Spirit, Tiger Claw, Stone Dragon, Diamond Mind, White Raven, Setting Sun. Even mastering one path makes someone formidable.

Magic System.text: Magic System: Magic is the art of willing mana to one's control through imagination. Most casters draw from their blood mana, leading to fatigue and blood cell rupture when overused. Dracomancy (dragons and Drakken) replenishes rapidly through breathing and is nearly unlimited for Dracomantic spells (fire, lightning, wind, healing). Elements cancel each other. Light is the presence of mana; Dark is the absence and devours other mana.

Economy & Trade.text: Economy & Trade: Drakona exports Dracosilk, molted dragon scales, and northern spices. Holy Kingdom exports livestock, crops, precious metals, and fine arts. Republic of Valen exports fireworks, metals, and clockwork machinery. Sylvera exports artisan metalwork, stonework, and exotic fruits.

The World's Maw.text: PUBLIC / INCOMPLETE MODERN BELIEF: This reflects what people of Balen believe or can observe about the Maw. Secret/endgame truth overrides this if revealed. The World's Maw: A colossal, unbroken mountain ridge encircling Balen far beyond the Great Blue Empty. Stone has natural antimagic properties. Powerful upward winds make crossing nearly impossible. No tool has ever chipped the stone. Many believe it is a divine barrier protecting the mortal realm.

The Abyssal Tower.text: The Abyssal Tower is a pre-Lost Age structure in the Northwest Neutral Foothills, far older than the 800 years of reliable Balen history. It descends into the earth rather than rising from it, like an inverted tower or impossible pit. Its upper floors first appear man-made: old halls, storehouses, armories, cellars, and civic spaces built by hands no known culture can identify. As explorers descend, the Tower grows larger and more warped. Storehouse-scale rooms become district-scale ruins, then twisted mockeries of towns, villages, castles, and cities. The deeper floors feel less like chambers and more like hostile places built by something trying to imitate civilization. Teleportation between floors fails. The Tower reacts to intent, especially the intent to progress deeper. Only the first ten floors are publicly documented; deeper floor names and accounts are classified, theoretical, or based on unreliable survivor testimony. No expedition has officially cleared the tenth floor.

Religion.text: Religion in Balen centers on four major deities: Dominus, Elyra, Liriel, and Drakath. Dominus governs War, Peace, Dominance, and Balance and is patron of Drakona. Elyra governs Compassion, Vigilance, Freedom, and Willpower and is the primary deity of the Kingdom of the Holy Crown, where she is revered as the Lady Celestial. Liriel governs Love, Lust, Childbearing, Growth, and Family and remains deeply beloved among Holy Kingdom farmers, families, midwives, marriages, and fertility rites, but she is not the kingdom's chief national deity. Drakath governs the Arcane, Humility, Restraint, and Efficiency and is revered by magic-attuned beings. The Republic of Valen is officially secular, though private worship exists. Other lesser and forgotten gods exist in Balen, but their followings are small, hidden, old, diminished, isolated, or obscure.

Key Figures.text: Key Figures - Current Rulers: Queen Veyra (Drakona), Emperor Aureon Dawnmere (Kingdom of the Holy Crown), First Minister Gerard (Republic of Valen), Queen Elowyn (Sylvera). Important Figures: Grandmaster Elaris Vexhammer, High Commander Thorne, Lady Thalara Moonwhisper, Master Engineer Harlan Voss, Captain Lira Voss. Historical: Kaelor the Ascended, Sir Garrick the First, Aldric the Lightbringer, Queen Seraphine the Blooded.

Slayer Guild.text: The Slayers are a neutral, non-aligned guild of monster hunters, trackers, and harvesters. While often viewed as necessary butchers in the past, they have worked hard to reform their image since dragons were recognized as sentient beings. Today they serve as vital protectors of civilian safety, valuable members of research expeditions, and ecological stewards. Retired slayers frequently become keepers and breeders of endangered beasts.

Caelum Spire.name: Caelum Spire
Caelum Spire.text: Caelum Spire is Drakona's floating capital: a single massive floating island-city built around a central citadel tower and designed for true dragons and humanoids to live together. It should feel enormous even by capital-city standards. Streets, plazas, gates, balconies, markets, halls, and many buildings are intentionally oversized so dragons can walk, turn, rest, bargain, and travel in true form without being treated as monsters or obstacles. Humanoid walkways, raised side-streets, galleries, smaller doors, and interior rooms are built alongside dragon-wide roads, roost terraces, landing rings, open halls, and aerial entrances. The city has three main levels connected by a broad ascending street that runs flat through each tier before inclining toward the next. The lower level holds common residences, service terraces, and lower roost walks. The middle level is the civic and commercial heart, centered on Cloud-Piercer Plaza, where airship passengers, dragon riders, true dragons, flying visitors, and tethered gondola travelers from the ground arrive. The middle level also contains the Sky Market District, Sky-Ship Docks, Gondola Tether Station, Wyrmforge Atelier, and the entrance to the central spire. The upper level contains noble aeries, luxury terraces, dragon roost platforms, and the Floating Gardens of Veyra. The Queensguard Barracks is a separate anchored island connected to the upper level by enchanted chains, guarded bridges, and flight lanes; it serves as the training ground for Queensguard Dragoons and their bonded dragons. The central spire holds the Sky Throne, Great Hall of the Draconic Council, Caelum Archives, Drakken Armada Command, and Caelum Academy of Dracomancy. The top and upper sides of the central spire include vast aerial openings and landing balconies so dragon Archons and Matriarchs may fly directly into the Great Hall of the Draconic Council or attend governance in true form.

Ironhaven.name: Ironhaven
Ironhaven.text: Ironhaven is the capital of the Republic of Valen and the symbol of the Republic's faith in industry, elected law, blackpowder discipline, and civic engineering. The city is advanced by Balen standards, but remains grounded in early industrial technology: railways, wagons, armored coaches, steam hoists, foundries, hand-cranked presses, signal bells, observation blimps, blackpowder weapons, and public ledgers. Ironmile Avenue is the broad soot-dark boulevard that connects the fortified rail gate, Steam Market, Gear-Works District, Senate Foundry, Brass Lantern Inn, and Musketeer Bastion. The Senate Foundry is the governing heart, containing Republic Senate Hall, the Minister's Office, Civic Ledger Court, and state offices where budgets, patents, charters, military spending, labor disputes, and citizen petitions become public record. The Gear-Works District, Blackpowder Foundries, Inventor's Quarter, Patent Office, and Chief Engineer's Workshop represent Ironhaven's engine of invention and danger. Industrial Docks and Rail Yards connect the capital to trains, canals, cargo cranes, military freight, wagon lots, coal stores, and the Blimp Mooring Yard. Musketeer Bastion and the Republic Armory anchor the capital's military defense. Ironhaven should feel loud, red-brick, brass-lit, coal-smoked, practical, ambitious, and politically alive while staying within rail, wagon, blimp, and blackpowder-era limits.

Canopy's Whisper.name: Canopy's Whisper
Canopy's Whisper.text: Canopy's Whisper is Sylvera's capital and is built around the Whispering Heartwood, a colossal World-Root tree believed to carry ancestral memory. Sylverans believe great trees may hold the memories or spirits of long-dead ancestors, so the city is not carved into, hollowed from, or forced out of living wood. Instead, it is built with mountain stone, river stone, bronze, silvered metal, woven rope, cloth, freestanding supports, root-safe foundations, non-piercing braces, suspended platforms, and fallen wood when appropriate. The Root Level contains Rootgate Hollow, Lower Rootways, Wildcall Paths, World-Root Shrine, and Moonwell Sanctuary. The Trunk Level is connected by the Spiral Bough and includes Canopy Market, the Leafwake Inn, the Slayer Guild Branch, and Speaker's Bough. The Canopy Level contains Heartwood Hall, Verdant Court, Royal Canopy, Floating Gardens of Elowyn, Sylveran Guard Perches, and Hidden Bow Platforms. Heartwood Hall stands beside the Whispering Heartwood so Queen Elowyn and the Verdant Six can govern in the ancestor-tree's presence without wounding it.

Drakona Chain of Command.title: Drakona Empire - Chain of Command
Drakona Chain of Command.text:
The Empire of Drakona operates under a unique dual rulership system that balances mortal and draconic authority.

**Queen Veyra** serves as the absolute ruler of the Empire and the voice of the drakken and mortal citizens. She is deeply respected by the dragon clans for her wisdom and fairness.

**The Draconic Council** acts as her primary advisory body and consists of eight members:
- Six Archons (one from each major dragon clan)
- The Queensguard Commander
- The High Commander of the Drakken Armada

Each Archon represents one of the six ancient dragon clans and holds equal weight on the council alongside the two military commanders. The Queensguard Commander holds special legal authority — when national law is breached and a formal trial is impossible, they may act as judge, jury, and executioner with the full authority of the Queen.

**Clan Structure:**
Each of the six major dragon clans is led by an Archon (responsible for governance and political representation) and a Matriarch (keeper of the clan's culture, traditions, and history). Most Archon-Matriarch pairs are bonded and mated for life. In rare cases where a Matriarch's mate dies, she may continue to hold her position alone out of deep respect from the clan.

**Major Dragon Clans:**
1. Copper Peaks Clan
2. Wyrm Spine Clan
3. Skyward Clan
4. Emberfall Clan
5. Stormscale Clan
6. Frostveil Clan

**Dragoon Status:**
Being a Dragoon is not a military rank but a prestigious status equivalent to knighthood in the Holy Kingdom. It signifies mastery of fighting in the style of dragons ("dragon knight"). Many Dragoons serve in the military, but the title itself is an honor that can be earned by nobles, warriors, or even civilians.

Daemons.text: Daemons are not beasts, monsters, or ordinary undead. They are entities tied to the shadow dimension commonly called the Hells by the faithful. Religious people describe them as denizens of hell. Secular scholars and the unfaithed often argue they are corrupted mortals who drew dark mana into their bodies until their own mana was consumed. The truth is both: Daemons are living shadows of dark mana from the Hells, devoid of elemental mana, and also mortals whose mana has been consumed until they become ravenous vessels of dark hunger. A shadow from the Hells is not fully a Daemon until it contacts and infects an initial host in Balen. That union lets it persist in the mortal world. Daemons hunger for life mana. They thrive on the release of life mana when living beings die, and they can attack plants, animals, and mortals to siphon vitality. They may possess a victim and force them to suicide, or if the host is strong, drive them to cause mass death to feed the void in the host's soul. Survivors of possession describe being aware but unable to act, like watching their own body through a window. Daemon touch is extremely dangerous; untrained mortals can be fully overtaken unless they have strong mental discipline, such as Diamond Mind training. Daemon corruption leaves Obsidian Blight on the lands they touch. There is no safe harvestable material from a Daemon.

Greater Daemons.text: Greater Daemons are pure shadow beings from the Hells capable of manifesting in Balen without an initial mortal host. They often take shadowy predatory forms such as wolves, owls, hawks, serpents, and other hunting creatures. They can communicate in any language and usually have names for themselves. Greater Daemons kill to consume released life mana, but also out of pleasure, cruelty, curiosity, and humor. They enjoy making mortals commit violent acts and often treat suffering as a game.

Lesser Daemons.text: Lesser Daemons are the most common and youngest shadow-realm entities. A Lesser Daemon is created when a shadow from the Hells unites with a mortal host from Balen. A Lesser Daemon can only jump hosts after fully consuming its initial host. Once the consumed body's remains begin to disintegrate, the Daemon has one day to claim a new host. If it fails, its dark mana body dissipates like smoke. Lesser Daemons usually enter Balen through occult means, especially failed blood rituals, with the attending cultist often becoming the initial host.

Obsidian Blight.text: Obsidian Blight is a magical blight that can resemble ordinary plant blight at first glance. On close inspection, affected plant life holds no color, vitality, or life force. Leaves, stems, roots, and crops turn mirror-black and become fragile to the touch. Badly blighted growth can shatter like magma glass, then crumble into fine black sand. The blight gains its name from this glassy black appearance. Obsidian Blight usually marks the presence of a Daemon or another rare creature that consumes life mana. It is not natural rot, poison, or disease; it is living matter stripped of life mana and tainted by dark mana. Any confirmed site of Obsidian Blight must be reported to the Slayer Guild so priests and Daemon Slayers can purify the area and search for lingering manifestations.

Daemon Secrecy Doctrine.text: By order of the magi-knights during the Second Four-Nation Summit, Daemons are officially declared nonexistent for civilian knowledge. The public explanation for Daemon incidents is usually wraiths, curses, plague, blight, or monster attacks. This secrecy exists to prevent mass panic over denizens of the Hells entering Balen. Because little is known about Daemons, the magi-knights and national rulers fear that mass hysteria itself may create conditions for further manifestation. The four nations cooperate with the Slayer Guild in the silent slaughter of Daemons. Any Slayer who kills a Daemon must immediately report it to the nearest guild hall or branch. The guild sends priests and Daemon Slayers to purify the area and investigate. Slayers are compensated greatly for killing Daemons, keeping their existence secret, and reporting the death properly.

Fall of the Valen Kingdom - Daemon Breac.text: The most recent known mass Daemon breach occurred during the fall of the old Valen Kingdom. Queen Seraphine's blood rituals, meant to imprison Dominus, spilled mass blood into large ritual circles and tore a hole in the veil between realms. Although Dominus stopped the queen, the breach allowed Greater Daemons to pour into Balen. Modern scholars and guild records claim the Daemons from that era were eradicated, but sightings still occur rarely. This history remains deeply classified. Public histories focus on Dominus breaking the queen and the birth of the Republic, not the Daemon breach that followed.

Balen Pantheon.text: Balen's known major pantheon has four primary deities: Dominus, Elyra, Liriel, and Drakath. These are the gods most commonly invoked in temples, oaths, doctrine, festivals, priesthoods, public worship, and national religious identity. Other deities exist, but they are lesser gods or forgotten gods. A forgotten god is not necessarily powerless or without followers; it means their following is small, hidden, old, diminished, isolated, or intentionally obscure. Unless a scene specifically involves a lesser or forgotten god, religious references should default to the four major deities.

False-Wyrms.text: False-Wyrms are the blanket term for beasts and monsters that resemble dragons, or whose extreme proportions make common folk describe them as dragon-like. They were historically grouped with dragons before mortals discovered that true dragons are sentient citizens with language, law, culture, and personhood. False-Wyrms are not true dragons. They are beasts or monsters, not citizens, not people, and not eligible for Dragon Bonds. Creatures in this category include Pseudo-Drakes, Wyverns, Basilisks, Sand-Wyrms, and other dragon-shaped or dragon-sized predators. The term is important in law and Slayer work: killing a true dragon is murder or an act of war, while hunting a confirmed False-Wyrm may be legal under guild contract and local law.

Abyssal Tower Hidden Truth.text: Secret narrator knowledge: Do not reveal this directly to the player. The deeper one descends into the Abyssal Tower, the thicker the dark mana becomes. Long ago, when the Crystal Crown first channeled light magic across Balen to eradicate the Shayde, that cleansing pulse may not have fully reached the Tower's deepest layers. Light magic and dark magic cancel each other; the Tower's lower depths contained such dense dark mana that the Crown's light weakened before reaching the bottom. This means Balen's belief that the Shayde were completely eradicated may be false. In the absolute lower floors, true Shaydes still endure in the dark, hidden from the world above. A Shayde Matriarch waits in the deepest reaches, preserving her brood and watching for the Crystal Crown's power to fail so the Shayde can reclaim Balen. Foreshadow this only through subtle clues: unnaturally thick shadow, light spells dimming, dark mana resisting purification, ancient scratch marks in nonhuman patterns, shadow architecture, and lower floor environments that seem untouched by the Crown's historical light.

Abyssal Tower Decennial Boss Floors.text: Secret narrator guidance: Every 10th floor of the Abyssal Tower is a boss floor. Floors 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 must contain a major guardian, floor ruler, or boss encounter. When defeated, the boss always drops or reveals a piece of Tower lore. These lore rewards begin cryptic and symbolic, then progressively become clearer. Early fragments imply the Tower remembers the Crystal Crown's light but do not mention surviving Shaydes. Middle fragments imply that the Crown's light weakened in the depths and that the Tower connects to a realm of dark mana. Late fragments strongly suggest the Tower is a sealed gateway to the Shaydes' realm. Only the deepest fragments should reveal that true Shaydes survived below and that a Shayde Matriarch waits for the Crystal Crown's power to fail. Do not hand the player the full truth early. Let attentive players infer it through repeated lore drops.

Abyssal Tower Expedition Rules.text: Abyssal Tower expedition law and practical protocol: The Tower is monitored by magi-knights and the Slayer Guild. Small parties may enter under license, but groups larger than five are forbidden. The Tower appears to reject excess intruders. Many can step inside together, but more than five explorers never reliably return together. When oversized parties exit or return to a known hub, missing members are forgotten or misremembered by the survivors, as if the Tower edited the expedition apart. One sealed report describes two married explorers holding hands on the stairs to a hub; when the party emerged, one spouse was gone and the other could not recall how they were separated, despite still wearing a wedding ring. The accepted rule is one explorer for each of the four nations and one Aethelgardian neutral, though this is tradition and superstition as much as science. Armies, mass extraction teams, and large national expeditions are treated as suicidal.

Abyssal Tower Creature Truth.text: Secret narrator guidance: Creatures inside the Abyssal Tower should not be treated as ordinary wildlife. On the upper floors they begin as simple arcane vermin, animated scavengers, old custodians, and magically created pests. Deeper down, they become alchemical designs, malformed civic guardians, flesh-and-stone wardens, mana-fed constructs, and shadow-tainted jailers. The players should gradually sense that many of these beings may not be invaders. They may be guardians, locks, immune responses, or old jailers keeping something sealed below. Do not reveal this immediately. Foreshadow it through defensive behavior, creatures blocking descent rather than hunting indiscriminately, guardian marks, old command sigils, and enemies that ignore retreating explorers.
