The governing council is the Six Seats of the Senate: Senator Marcelline Ironwright governs civic works from Ironhaven in the Valen Iron Marches; Senator Garron Blackiron governs heavy industry from Blackiron Foundry in the Valen Iron Marches; Senator Elian Powdergate governs defense policy from Powdergate Arsenal in Ironforge Gate; Senator Petra Gearford governs trade and patents from Gearford Exchange in the Gearworks District; Senator Brina Coalhand governs labor from Ashgate Worker Rows in the Ashen Flats; and Senator Tomas Railwright governs railways, roads, bridges, and depots from Ironmile Depot along Western Foundry Road.

Currency and Contracts.text: All nations now use universal Marks for trade and contract math. Use Copper Marks, Silver Marks, and Gold Marks only. Slayer contract rewards, shop prices, quest payouts, fees, bribes, wages, and loot should be converted into the fewest clear Mark denominations. Do not use old national coin systems.

Celestial Ruin Network.text: Several dangerous Celestial ruins across Balen are exposed crowns of noble homes, forges, archives, bastions, and boundary structures from the Vaulted City network. They fused into mortal ruins during the Sundering and were sealed by escaping survivors. Each site is midgame or higher and may hint toward the Vaulted City.

Vaulted City Realm Concept.text: The Vaulted City is not a normal surface map region. It is a collapsed Celestial pocket-dimension reached only through endgame sealed access from Celestial ruins. It should not overwrite or replace Balen's main region map. Treat it as a separate realm-space concept until the correct realm schema is confirmed.

Vaulted City Navigation.text: The Vaulted City realm is navigated by its routeGraph/realmRoutes, not by area paths. Area paths inside each Vaulted City location remain empty because each POI is its own location. Normal route: Vault of Penitent Light to Canted Promenade, then toward Thousand-Lamp Plaza or Abyssal Tower Vista, with other districts reached through listed realmRoutes.

Vaulted City Entrances.text: The Vaulted City is not reached by normal roads. It is entered from Balen through opened Celestial breach seals at five dangerous surface ruins: Saintless Abbey Breach, Ashen Skycourt Forge, Reliquary Archive Breach, Broken Halo Bastion, and Glasswound Boundary Breach. Each unlocked gate leads to the Vault of Penitent Light, which can return the party only to breach gates already opened.

Worcen Terminology.text: Worcen is the correct canon race term for beastkin people in Balen. Do not use Worcester, Worchester, Worce, or similar variants as race names, NPC labels, or synonyms. If a player uses one of those variants, interpret it as a typo for Worcen and answer using the correct term.

Currency Inventory Grants.text: Inventory currency grants must use exact existing currency item names and stack them: Copper Marks, Silver Marks, and Gold Marks. A total value of 251 is paid as 2 Gold Marks, 5 Silver Marks, and 1 Copper Mark. Do not create generic Mark/Marks, money, purse, coin, or other noncanonical currency items.

Vaulted City Dust Secrecy.text: Within the Vaulted City, the fine obsidian-black dust must remain an unexplained environmental detail. Describe how it gathers, clings, smears, and dulls the light, but do not identify it as Shayde, daemon, shadow, ash, bodies, bones, remains, or proof of what happened. NPCs inside the realm should not casually know what it is.

Balen Defense Layers.text: Combat defense in Balen uses four layers: contact, miss chance, damage, and mitigation. First determine contact: miss, dodge, block, parry, deflect, graze, hit, or critical. Second, apply miss chance only when fiction supports concealment, invisibility, incorporeal form, phasing, blur, heavy smoke, darkness, or displacement: 20% for partial concealment and 50% for total concealment or phasing effects. Use only the highest miss chance. Third, use the damage outcome ladder. Fourth, apply physical Damage Reduction to physical damage and typed Resistance only to matching typed damage. Bosses and constructs should use moderate HP, DR, resistances, weak points, and special actions rather than absurd HP bloat. Twin or dual weapon items use their combined effective damage range before this ladder is applied: double both minimum and maximum base damage, such as a 1-8 longsword becoming 2-16 as twin longswords.

Healing in Balen.text: Healing in Balen has clear limits by method. Mundane medicine and alchemy stabilize the body, stop bleeding, treat poison, manage shock, splint bones, prevent infection, and support recovery. Healers are strongest at practical medicine, alchemical treatment, triage, and keeping someone alive when magic is unavailable or insufficient. Light magic can close wounds, stop bleeding, soothe pain, shield allies, and preserve life, but it does not truly rebuild complex internal damage, regrow tissue, restore organs, or properly repair shattered bones by itself. Dracomancy is the primary form of true regenerative healing: it can close wounds, repair bones, mend torn muscle, restore damaged tissue, and stabilize severe trauma when the caster has enough breath, control, and power. Closed wounds do not always mean the body is fully healed.

Slayer Vocation Techniques.text: Slayer vocation abilities are professional fieldcraft and combat techniques, not magic, Dracomancy, or Sublime Arts. Monster Read, Hunter's Mark, and Clean Harvest do not cost mana, stamina, or breathing. Combat-focused Slayer techniques should use cooldowns, usually 5 to 10 rounds, to represent focus, timing, and the need for a new opening rather than supernatural resource expenditure.

Bladesinger Starting Equipment.text: Bladesinger vocation equipment is element-specific. Pyromantic Bladesingers start with Twin Sabers and a Duelist's Jacket. Hydromantic Bladesingers start with a Dueling Saber and a Duelist's Jacket. Aeromantic Bladesingers start with Dual Daggers and a Duelist's Jacket. Fulmantic Bladesingers start with an Estoc and a Duelist's Jacket. These weapons include small Bladesinging-support bonuses so they function as vocation tools rather than dead equipment slots. Twin or dual weapon items use combined damage ranges, such as Twin Sabers at 2 to 12 and Dual Daggers at 2 to 8.

Dual and Twin Weapon Damage Rule.text: When a weapon item represents a paired set, twin weapons, or dual-wielded weapons, its listed damage range reflects both weapons striking together. Single dagger 1 to 4 becomes Dual Daggers 2 to 8. Single saber 1 to 6 becomes Twin Sabers 2 to 12. Single longsword 1 to 8 becomes Twin Longswords 2 to 16.

Ravager Weapon Options.text: Ravagers most commonly favor greataxes, warhammers, and twin axes. The standard starting Ravager kit uses a greataxe, but warhammers and twin axes are valid Ravager weapons for later equipment, regional variants, loot, shops, NPCs, and custom starts. Twin Axes follow the twin/dual weapon rule and list a combined damage range for both axes striking together.

Knight Vocation.text: Knight vocation characters are formal armored warriors, not merely people with swords. They are trained to hold lines, protect allies, issue challenges, brace behind shields, read martial hierarchy, and carry the social expectations of oath, house, order, rank, or recognized station. A starting Knight is equipped for disciplined defense and battlefield presence rather than raw savagery or monster-hunter fieldcraft.

Shield Equipment Rule.text: Shields are secondary-slot Weapon items rather than body armor. A standard knightly shield grants +1 physical Damage Resistance while equipped and may be used for Shield Bash. Shield Bash has Dmg Range: 1 to 2 and may stun, stagger, interrupt, or knock an enemy off-balance on a successful hit. Better shields may add stronger protection, cover, warding, or movement drawbacks, but the standard Knight's Shield is the baseline starter shield.

Mercenary Vocation.text: Mercenaries in Balen are professional soldiers-for-hire who occupy a tense role in the cold peace between nations. When rulers, nobles, guilds, merchants, or border lords need force without officially sending armies under Magi-Knight scrutiny, they may hire mercenaries for deniable disputes, proxy skirmishes, sabotage protection, caravan defense, expedition security, sentry work, and private guard duty. This does not make every mercenary criminal; many are respectable hired guards and expedition soldiers. But every experienced mercenary understands that some contracts are really political violence wearing a private seal.

Mercenary Starting Equipment.text: Mercenary vocation starting equipment is intentionally practical rather than noble: Arming Sword, Brigandine Vest, Folded Mercenary Banner, and whetstone. The banner is memorabilia from a mercenary company the character once marched with and may represent pride, loss, exile, loyalty, betrayal, or proof of old service.

Spellsword Vocation.text: Spellswords are martial arcanists who bind runes, sigils, and magic circles into combat. They are not Sublime Arts practitioners by default; their power comes from written structure, prepared inscriptions, mana-charged lines, and direct-contact rune techniques. Their blade acts as both weapon and writing tool, letting them release prepared sigils, guard circles, and combat brands through controlled mana use.

Runic Branding.text: Runic Branding is a Spellsword technique that brands a harmful rune into a target through direct contact. Contact must be established through a weapon, hand, or etched focus before the spoken rune can flare. If the attack misses, is dodged, parried, blocked, or otherwise fails to touch the target, the brand does not form. The basic brand runes are Ignis for fire, Aero for forceful wind and knockback, Fulcra for lightning, and Icis for frost and slowing.

Spellsword Starting Equipment.text: The standard Spellsword starting kit is a Rune-Etched Arming Sword, Reinforced Coat, Engraving Stylus, and Inscription Chalk. This gives the vocation a weapon, light armor, and the tools needed for basic combat inscriptions without duplicating the Runescribe’s broader stationery kit.

Hunter Vocation Removal Note.text: Hunter is no longer a general starting vocation. Hunting, wilderness tracking, and game-provision concepts should be represented through Scout, Slayer, Tamer, Inherent Skills, Story Starts, wilderness kits, or roleplay rather than a separate vocation competing with those roles.

Stealth and Shadow Stealth Distinction.text: Stealth and shadow stealth are separate skills. Stealth is mundane concealment: quiet movement, hiding, camouflage, cover, sightline control, noise control, and avoiding attention without magic. Shadow stealth is supernatural concealment using dark mana, shadow powers, Shadow Hand maneuvers, or actual shadow-melding effects. Ordinary scouts, criminals, outcasts, wilderness travelers, and dungeoneers should normally use stealth, not shadow stealth.

Scout Vocation.text: Scouts are utility-focused advance observers, trailfinders, infiltrators, caravan pathfinders, military lookouts, and expedition guides. Their role is information control: finding what waits ahead, avoiding detection, marking safe routes, sensing ambushes, and surviving long enough to report back. Scouts are not Slayers, Mercenaries, or Dungeoneers by default; they specialize in reconnaissance, mundane stealth, route reading, and practical survival.

Scout Starting Equipment.text: Scout vocation starting equipment is shortbow, dagger, travel cloak, and Scout's Map Case. The kit supports quiet scouting, route marking, ambush awareness, and practical field movement without turning the Scout into a full Slayer, Hunter, or soldier package.

Scout Avoidance.text: Avoidance is the Scout's mundane stealth trait. In areas with cover, broken sightlines, crowds, brush, ruins, darkness, smoke, clutter, or uneven terrain, a Scout can escape detection more easily than ordinary characters. Avoidance is not invisibility and not shadow magic; open ground, direct observation, and lack of cover still matter.

Arcanist Vocation.text: Arcanists are trained arcane casters who combine natural spellcasting talent with disciplined magical study. They are not limited to memorized formulae, but neither do they rely on instinct alone. An Arcanist studies spell theory, mana behavior, elemental structure, focus control, and practical casting until their innate gift becomes reliable. Their spellbook is a living record of experiments, formulae, gestures, failed designs, corrected theories, and personal discoveries.

Arcanist Cantrips.text: Arcanist cantrips are minor utility magic for mundane tasks: cleaning, warming, chilling, nudging or levitating small unattended objects, creating harmless sounds, lighting candles, manifesting small temporary conveniences, or similar prestidigitation. Cantrips are not combat spells, do not create lasting valuables, and do not bypass serious obstacles. They normally have no tracked mana cost unless repeated, enlarged, strained, or used under pressure.

Arcanist Magic Missile.text: Magic Missile is the Arcanist's basic scalable arcane attack. Each missile costs 10 mana and deals 1 to 2 arcane damage. For every additional 10 mana spent, one additional missile is formed. Missiles may be directed at one visible target or split among visible targets. Large mana dumps are possible but risk exhaustion, strain, or overdraw if the caster spends more than they can safely afford.

Runescribe Vocation.text: Runescribes are tattooed ritual arcanists who write magic into flesh, air, stone, parchment, and prepared surfaces. They often fight unarmored with protective body-runes, touch-based Runic Branding, air-cut circles, mana-drain trap circles, chained circles, ward scripts, and inscription-breaking techniques. They are not Magi-Knights or Sublime Arts practitioners; their power comes from written structure, ritual geometry, direct contact, and efficient circlework.

Runic Tattooing.text: Runic Tattooing allows a Runescribe to use stable body-runes and magic circles in place of armor. While unarmored and while the markings remain visible, intact, and magically stable, the tattoos grant +2 physical Damage Resistance. This does not stack with armor-based Damage Resistance unless a specific enchantment says otherwise. Damage to the lines, anti-magic, mana-drain seals, or disrupted body-circuits can weaken the protection.

Efficient Circlework.text: Runescribes can power simple, properly drawn runes and magic circles with minimal mana. Minor prepared effects may require only a small channel, often 1 mana, because the written structure does most of the spell-shaping work. This does not make powerful magic free: complex chained circles, combat traps, major wards, enchantments, unstable circles, and high-power effects still require appropriate mana, time, materials, and Runic Magic checks.

Runescribe Starting Equipment.text: Runescribe starting equipment is dagger, Runescribe Kit, Ritual Ink, Inscription Chalk, Engraving Stylus, Blank Parchment, and Traveling Robe. Runescribes do not start with armor because Runic Tattooing is their defensive method.

Runic Branding Delivery Styles.text: Runic Branding is shared by Spellswords and Runescribes but delivered differently. Spellswords commonly brand through weapon contact, hands, or etched foci. Runescribes commonly brand through direct touch, tattooed palms, etched foci, or ritual implements. In all cases the brand requires successful contact; a miss, dodge, parry, block, or interruption prevents the brand from forming.

Tamer Vocation.text: Tamers are creature handlers, humane trappers, trainers, and field companions. They do not begin with a guaranteed pet, mount, or companion. Instead, they begin with the tools and knowledge needed to calm, capture, feed, train, bond with, and temporarily coordinate with tameable creatures through play.

Wyverneer Trait.text: Wyverneer is a focused Tamer vocation trait. It grants +3 to Tamer checks involving tameable wyverns, including calming, trapping, feeding, training, bonding, reading temperament, and preventing panic or aggression.

Tamer Starting Equipment.text: The Tamer starting kit is dagger, Tamer's Whistle, Feed Pouch, Handling Gloves, Training Lead, and Snare Kit. This kit supports trapping and handling without granting a starting tame.

Wild Call to Aid.text: Wild Call to Aid is a Tamer technique that can temporarily gain aid from a suitable nearby wild beast or tameable creature for 5 to 6 rounds. It does not create a permanent companion, does not summon creatures from nowhere, and cannot force loyalty or override sentient will.

Scholar Vocation.text: Scholars are trait-heavy knowledge specialists rather than combatants or spellcasters by default. Their power is disciplined research: broad education, source verification, field evidence handling, translation, contradiction-spotting, and knowing where facts should be checked. A Scholar may be an archivist, historian, translator, natural philosopher, court academic, field researcher, or reluctant adventuring expert. They should help reveal context, leads, contradictions, and reliable evidence, but they should not automatically reveal secret or endgame truth without earned proof.

Scholar Starting Equipment.text: Scholar starting equipment should support research and survival, not heavy combat: dagger, Scholar's Journal, Writing Kit, Ink Vial, Reference Notes, and Traveling Robe. The journal, writing kit, and reference notes represent field-ready academic tools for copying inscriptions, preserving observations, checking lore fragments, and organizing evidence.

Scholar Knowledge Limits.text: Scholar traits can improve knowledge, lore, research, translation, historical, academic, cultural, religious, legal, and theoretical checks, but they do not override secrecy rules. Scholars may identify contradictions, public versions, likely missing context, and where to search next. They should not casually know hidden truths, sealed guild doctrines, classified Abyssal Tower facts, or divine/endgame revelations unless the story has provided direct evidence.

Dungeoneer Vocation.text: Dungeoneers are practical ruin-delvers, tomb-breakers, trap-spotters, lock-workers, and expedition survivors. They are not automatically scholars or thieves. Their strength is surviving enclosed danger: suspicious floors, rigged chests, bad air, unstable ceilings, false stones, hidden exits, valuables placed too conveniently, and old mechanisms waiting for careless hands.

Dungeoneer Starting Equipment.text: Dungeoneer starting equipment should support practical delving rather than combat bloat: dagger, Crowbar, Lockpicks, Rope, Lantern, Chalk, and Dungeoneer's Pack. These items help with traps, locks, route marking, darkness, climbing, old mechanisms, and dungeon survival.

Dungeoneer Vocation Traits.text: Dungeoneer grants Trap Sense, Treasure Eye, and Delver's Instinct. Trap Sense helps notice traps and hidden mechanisms. Treasure Eye helps judge value, danger, authenticity, and portability of loot. Delver's Instinct helps survive enclosed spaces, bad air, unstable stone, monster nests, hidden exits, and structural danger.

Inherent Skill Starting Items.text: Inherent Skill starting items are intentionally small support tools or flavor items, not full loadouts. They should never grant main weapons, armor, ration stacks, or vocation-level equipment packages. Natural Dracomancer grants Draconic Breathing Charm; Gunpowder Prodigy grants Powder Measure; Blade Master grants whetstone; Arcane Aptitude grants Practice Focus; Stealth Expert grants Softstep Wraps.

Equipment Slot Bonus Rule.text: If an item occupies an equipment slot, it should provide a useful bonus, rule hook, or clear utility. Items that provide only story flavor should avoid occupying limited equipment slots unless the story intentionally treats them as meaningful keepsakes. Tools, trinkets, clothing, and accessories that take a slot should help checks, enable actions, or provide a clear mechanical/narrative benefit.

Location Danger Override Rule.text: Region level ranges describe broad environmental danger, starter content, and civilian-scale scenes. Specific capital areas may override that range by security, military presence, restricted access, sacred importance, arcane protection, or sovereign authority. When an internal area has levelRange or npcLevelRange, use that area range over the parent region/capital range for NPCs, guards, patrols, hazards, and resistance generated inside that area. Capital markets, inns, roads, and public plazas may remain level 1-5, while barracks, citadels, royal courts, faction headquarters, restricted archives, palace security, and inner sanctums should use higher ranges. Do not spawn trained capital guards, soldiers, knights, magi-knights, Glaives, Queensguard, Musketeers, Sylveran Guard, palace protectors, or equivalent authority NPCs at level 1 merely because the capital region is level 1-3.

Combat Damage Hard Rule.text: Combat damage must use the declared attack source and Voyage native baseDamage as raw impact pressure. Do not stack all equipped weapons or add raw skill scores, attack totals, success margin, or character level. Hit result sets impact instead of adding damage: Mixed 0.20, Basic 0.35, Success 0.50, Great 0.75, Critical 1.00. Armor reduces impact before HP loss. A full miss, dodge, block, parry, cover stop, or armor stop deals 0 HP damage.

Runemancy: Circle Structure.text:
Runemancy circles in Balen are structured runic circuits, not decorative diagrams. A proper magic circle usually contains three major rings: the Core Ring, the Geometric Ring, and the Inscription Ring.

The Core Ring is the innermost ring and anchor point. It may be thin, bold, simple, or reinforced depending on stability and mana load. The elemental rune placed inside the Core Ring determines the circle’s primary elemental outcome: Ignis for fire, Icis for frost/ice, Fulcra for lightning, Aero for wind, Tera for earth, Void for dark, and Hallow for light. If the core is broken, misplaced, or misnamed, the whole circle may misfire.

The Geometric Ring is the largest ring and forms the body of the circle. It contains linework, lattices, routes, and geometric structures that determine how mana travels between the Inscription Ring and the Core Ring. In compound or nested circles, the Geometric Ring may join several effects together. Its linework tells the circle how to activate, combine, delay, magnify, store, redirect, stabilize, or rebound the runes elsewhere.

Common Geometric Ring linework includes Transference Lines, straight lines that move mana directly between rings; Rerouting Lines, curved lines that redirect mana through the body of the circle or back through the Inscription Ring before reaching the Core; Magnification Lines, bolder angular routes often moving at ninety-degree angles from the Inscription Ring toward the Core so mana accumulates before release; Storage Geometry, simple geometric shapes connected to the Inscription or Geometric Ring that slow and hold mana before returning it to a line or rune; and Bloom or Rebounding Lines.

Bloom/Rebounding Lines are special prolonging lattices connected directly to the Core Ring. A bloom is formed from two rerouting lines that both connect to the Core and meet at a sharp outward angle, resembling a flower petal with the Core as the center. Blooms rebound an already-active Core effect back through the circle, prolonging and potentially magnifying the effect before it ends. Poorly drawn blooms can cause echoing surges, delayed backlash, or unstable overextension.

The Inscription Ring is the outer ring, also called the Third Ring. This is where the runesmith places the runic phrase or inscription that instructs the circle’s purpose. It is commonly divided into three equal segments by solid straight lines. Known elemental/action runes include Icis for ice/frost, Ignis for fire, Fulcra for lightning, Aero for wind, Tera for earth, Void for dark, Hallow for light, Grecis for storage, and Absive for absorption. Runes may describe elements or actions, which is why circles can store mana, absorb spells, redirect force, trigger traps, or stabilize enchantments.

Every line in the Geometric Ring must connect to the inner or outer structure. A disconnected line is a dead circuit that can weaken, distort, delay, or catastrophically fumble the effect. A brilliant runesmith reads a circle as both language and machine: runes say what mana should become, while geometry tells mana how to move.

Runemancy: Circle Chaining and Inscripti.text:
The Inscription Ring is commonly divided into northern, eastern, and western segments. The northern segment is the circle’s primary purpose, followed by the eastern and then western segments as modifiers or support. A segment may contain more than three runes; three-rune inscriptions are only the simplest teaching form. Advanced circles may use longer phrases, repeated runes, nested phrases, or compressed chains.

Repeating the same inscription across all three segments magnifies output. A simple flame-throwing circle with an Ignis core may repeat Aero/Ignis/Aero in all three segments, telling the circle to blow fire with maximum directness and strength. Different segment inscriptions diversify behavior but divide output. For example: Northern Segment Aero/Ignis/Aero, Eastern Segment Grecis/Grecis/Grecis, Western Segment Absive/Absive/Absive. With transference lines between Grecis and Absive and rerouting lines back to the flame segment, the circle’s logic becomes: Store my mana, absorb my stored mana, blow fire. The result lasts longer, but is weaker than a triple-repeated flame inscription. This is the central tradeoff: repeated inscription means stronger/simple/direct; mixed inscription means weaker but longer, smarter, or more flexible. Advanced latticework can compensate but is too complex for casual use.

Compounding Circles, also called Circle Chaining, link two or more circles so their effects interact as one larger circuit. Circles do not compound by proximity alone; they require compatible inscription logic, most often a shared or compatible rune in the Inscription Ring.

A standard ward circle uses Hallow/Absive/Grecis: Hallow defines protective light or warding presence, Absive absorbs incoming force or mana, and Grecis stores it safely until grounded, released, redirected, or consumed. Because all wards use this basic purpose, any circle containing Hallow, Absive, or Grecis may potentially chain with a ward if the geometry is stable and the intended behavior is compatible.

A repulsion circle might use Aero/Absive/Aero: absorb incoming mana or force, then release it as wind pressure. The fastest and crudest way to compound this with a ward is to overlap the Inscription Rings so both circles physically share the same Absive rune. This shared Absive becomes a bridge-rune. Mana entering the ward’s Absive logic can pass into the repulsion circle’s Absive logic, allowing the ward to absorb force and immediately feed it into Aero repulsion.

Overlapping inscription rings is practical but dangerous. The shared rune becomes a shared failure point: if it is damaged, overloaded, misdrawn, or misaligned, both circles may fail at once. More advanced chaining uses deliberate bridge geometry instead of crude overlap, reducing failure risk and allowing expert runesmiths to preserve more power while adding complexity.

The Five-Month War.text:
The Five-Month War, formally the Sylveran-Kingdom War, is also called the Last Great War by many in the Kingdom of the Holy Crown and the Last Border War by eastern-front veterans. It was the last major war before the current era, beginning ten years before game start as a border dispute between Sylvera and the Kingdom of the Holy Crown before escalating into one of the bloodiest conflicts in modern history.

Sylveran forces turned the conflict into a deep-penetration campaign, using guerrilla tactics, forest movement, ambush doctrine, runic traps, and magic-circle warfare to strike behind Holy Kingdom supply lines. Kingdom knight companies and Glaive regiments were devastated by hidden circles, false retreats, broken roads, severed messengers, and sudden elven blade-strikes from terrain the kingdom believed secure.

Within the first month, Sylvera had pushed the front nearly one hundred and fifty kilometers into Holy Kingdom territory. Emperor Aureon Dawnmere enacted a national draft, summoning all able-bodied men and women to defend their home. From this emergency came the Mage-Korps: paired infantry teams created to counter Sylvera’s battlefield magic. Each pair usually consisted of a runic mage capable of breaking or countering elven circles, and a warrior trained to meet Sylveran fighters in close combat. One famous surviving pair was Kazien Cranehollow, a runesmith and spellsword, and Valyrine Dawnmere, a direwolf-worcen frontline breaker.

The Magi-Knights initially refused to intervene, believing the war to be a contained national border dispute rather than a threat to Balen’s wider balance. By the time the true depth of the invasion became clear, Holy Kingdom forces had been forced back to the capital region. This late action damaged the Holy Kingdom’s faith in Aethelgard. Many Crownsworn believe the Magi-Knights abandoned them until the Crystal Crown itself was threatened, solidifying the belief: “We must defend what is ours because no one else will.”

Ten years later, tensions between the Holy Kingdom and Sylvera remain dangerously high. Many Crownsworn resent elves. Others, like Kazien, do not hate them but suffer trauma flareups around elven soldiers or veterans and cannot remain near them for long. Across five months, nearly one hundred and fifty thousand souls died. The Holy Kingdom remembers desperate patriotic survival; Sylvera remembers grief, controversy, and unresolved questions; Aethelgard remembers a failure of early intervention.

The Five-Month War: Notable Events.text:
Notable Event: The Thirty Sylven Massacre / Elven Slaughter. Despite the name, this began when thirty Sylveran soldiers ambushed a Mage-Korps unit in the woods of the Holy Kingdom near the border. The Korps was scattered and almost entirely slain. The only survivor was Kazien Cranehollow, badly injured and nearly out of mana. Kazien enacted the Bloody Gambit: he baited the attackers down his blood trail into a clearing while inscribing explosive runes into the trees and shaping the entire clearing as the geometric body of a massive magic circle. Its center held an Aero ward around him, while the clearing erupted in thousands of Ignis and Fulcra detonations. The blast consumed nearly two hundred units of mana and left Kazien at death’s door until Valyrine Dawnmere found him while investigating what she called “the gods-damned earth cracking open” and carried him back to camp.

Notable Event: Dawnmere’s Gambit / The Wolf and the Halfbreed. Officially, Dawnmere’s Gambit was a reckless retaliatory assault led by Valyrine Dawnmere and Kazien Cranehollow after the slaughter of their Mage-Korps comrades. Holy Kingdom officers later called it “The Wolf and the Halfbreed” after it became known that Cranehollow was half-dragon. The pair entered a Sylveran logistics camp as a two-person strike force. Kazien used circles, runic branding, warding, and enhancement magic to disrupt defenses and amplify Valyrine’s frontline pressure. Valyrine fought in brutal Ravager-like Magi-Knight style, breaking lines with greatsword, speed, strength, and elemental infusion. Together they leveled the camp, slew nearly two hundred Sylveran soldiers and support personnel, and temporarily cut the elven supply line. It might have been the turning point if Sylveran forces had not already been marching on the capital; a general retreat to defend the capital was called the next day.

Notable Event: The Breaking of the Siege. After three days of siege, Sylveran armies had pressed so close to the capital walls that defenders claimed they could see the color of elven eyes from the battlements. Many militia, Crown Knights, and Glaives believed the third day would be their final stand. Hundreds of militia were knighted in the field by House Knights as battlefield honor. Moments before the full Sylveran assault, Emperor Aureon Dawnmere entered the courtyard in warrior’s armor, refused to sit a throne while his people bled, and ordered a final charge. The drawbridge came down while the elves were preparing siege equipment. The battle lasted nearly six hours, painting the golden plains around the capital crimson. On the sixth hour the Sylveran forces sounded retreat. Cheers grew louder when the first Magi-Knight gryphons flew overhead, signaling Aethelgard’s intervention at last.

The Five-Month War: Aftermath.text:
After the Breaking of the Siege, Valyrine Dawnmere was offered knighthood for courage, but when she learned Kazien Cranehollow had been passed over despite standing beside her—widely suspected to be because of his half-dragon bloodline—she refused the honor and took the oath of a Magi-Knight instead.

Kazien left military service, traveled as a freelance enchanter and runesmith, and eventually became the most used civilian contractor for the Magi-Knights after Valyrine began commissioning protective enchantments for her order’s equipment.

Historical Moment: The Threshold Promise. Before the final charge, Kazien told Valyrine that if they survived, he would enchant gloves strong enough to carry her, marry her, and carry her across the threshold of wherever they claimed as home. He meant it partly as gallows humor and partly to keep her fighting if he died. Ten years later, after realizing Magi-Knights could marry, he proposed properly, made the gloves, married her, and carried the towering direwolf-worcen across the threshold of her Citadel quarters.

Cranemere Estate.text: Cranemere Estate is the private cottage home of Kazien Cranehollow and Valyrine Dawnmere, located in the Aetherian Marches not far from Aethelgard's gate. Kazien bought it so they would have a real home outside the Citadel: a garden, a warm hearth, a reinforced workshop for runes and circlework, and a training yard large enough for Valyrine's greatsword and direwolf-worcen stride. When Valyrine is on leave, they stay there together; when she is on Citadel duty, Kazien usually stays with her in the Magi-Knight Citadel. The estate should feel personal and earned, not like a public inn or generic quest hub.

Region Level Range Guidance.text: Region danger ranges from the former regionLevelRanges root object were migrated into valid regions.<name>.npcLevelRange fields where the region existed. Migrated ranges: 408. Entries without matching regions: 0. Use each region's npcLevelRange first when generating local NPCs or encounters; location/area-specific level guidance may override it when present.

Many can breathe fire through internal fire glands, though not all breeds possess this trait. Only some breeds have venom, tail barbs, frost breath, or specialized sensory anatomy. Bestiary: Basilisk.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Basilisk",
  "category": "False-Wyrm",
  "publicSummary": "A dangerous reptilian false-wyrm associated with gaze shock, paralysis, or petrification-like effects.",
  "description": "Basilisks are squat, heavy reptiles with crown-like head ridges, glassy eyes, and mineral-hard scales. They are not evil or intelligent; they are ambush predators with dangerous magical biology. Old basilisk dens are often surrounded by birds, mice, or larger animals frozen stiff from shock.",
  "knownRegions": "Rocky ruins, caves, old roads, dry gullies, abandoned structures, and stone-heavy badlands.",
  "behavior": "Still, patient, territorial, and comfortable waiting for prey to meet its gaze.",
  "threats": "Gaze shock, paralysis or petrification-like effects, venom, claws, and sudden ambush.",
  "weaknesses": "Mirrored shields, smoke, blindfolded positioning, ranged attacks, flank discipline, and breaking eye contact.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "eye-stone",
    "venom sacs",
    "scales",
    "claws",
    "gaze glands"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not normally tameable except under extreme specialist containment.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Do not casually generate basilisks as pets. A contained basilisk is a dangerous specialist project, not a companion.",
  "craftingNotes": "Eye-stone may provide anti-gaze warding, intimidation, or petrification resistance. Venom can be used in dangerous alchemy. Scales may support poison resistance or light armor.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Rumor",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Owlbear.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Owlbear",
  "category": "Magical Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A powerful forest and foothill predator with the heavy body of a bear, the hooked beak and night-sight of an owl, and a territorial temper that makes it a common cause of Slayer contracts near farms and logging roads.",
  "description": "Owlbears are thick-muscled magical beasts covered in dense fur and layered feathers. Their heads are broad and owl-like, with forward-facing eyes, a crushing beak, and feathered crests that rise when threatened. Most stand higher than a horse at the shoulder. They are not malicious, but they are brutally direct: if something enters their territory, threatens a nest, or smells like food, they charge.",
  "knownRegions": "Aethelgard forests, neutral foothills, Holy Kingdom deep woods, Sylveran border forests, and old logging roads.",
  "behavior": "Solitary or mated-pair hunters. Extremely nest-protective. More active at dusk, night, and dawn. They prefer ambushes from brush, rocky slopes, or ruined farm walls.",
  "threats": "Crushing forelimbs, tearing claws, hooked beak, night hunting, powerful charge, and stubborn refusal to retreat once enraged.",
  "weaknesses": "Bright sudden light, loud metallic noise, baited charges, exposed eyes, soft throat, and poor turning speed during a full rush.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "feathers",
    "claws",
    "beak",
    "hide",
    "fat",
    "eggs under heavy restriction"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Normally not tameable. Exceptionally rare if raised from egg by specialist handlers, but not common or safe.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Do not casually generate owlbears as pets. An owlbear egg or cub should be a major story complication, not a simple companion reward.",
  "craftingNotes": "Feathers can be used for stealth cloaks, fletching, and night-camouflage trim. Claws can become hooks, climbing tools, or curved blades. Hide can become heavy leather armor. Fat can be rendered into cold-weather salves.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Confirmed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Legacy harvest/value note preserved from older bestiary scaffold:
{

Bestiary: Gryphon.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Gryphon",
  "category": "Magical Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A proud aerial predator with leonine strength and raptor-like wings, used by elite couriers, scouts, and handlers when properly trained.",
  "description": "Gryphons combine the front half of a great hunting bird with the hindquarters and muscle of a large cat. Their feathers vary by region, from storm-grey and tawny gold to snowy white along the Wyrmspines. A trained gryphon is one of Balen's most respected aerial mounts; an untrained one is a territorial predator with talons strong enough to break bone.",
  "knownRegions": "Aethelgard cliffs and grasslands, Drakonan highlands, Wyrmspine ridges, Holy Kingdom aeries, and open mountain skies.",
  "behavior": "Proud, territorial, intelligent for a beast, loyal to skilled handlers, and quick to reject cruelty or cowardice.",
  "threats": "Diving strikes, talons, hooked beak, aerial grappling, wing gusts, and high-speed knockdowns.",
  "weaknesses": "Wing strain, enclosed spaces, poor footing, baiting pride, and careful grounding tactics.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "feathers",
    "talons",
    "beak fragments",
    "hollow bones",
    "eggs under strict legal restriction"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Tameable with skill, usually through aeries, long training, or rescue while young.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Uses petCompanionSystem. Public risk is Restricted. Often Transport, Scout, Combat, and Guard. Requires stabling/aerie access in most cities.",
  "craftingNotes": "Feathers are prized for arrows, cloaks, and air-aspected charms. Talons can be used for piercing tools or climbing gear. Hollow bones can reinforce lightweight spear shafts or aerial tack.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Confirmed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Legacy harvest/value note preserved from older bestiary scaffold:
{
  "description": "Majestic aerial predators with the body of a lion and the head, wings, and talons of a giant eagle. Intelligent and proud.",
  "habitat": "High mountains, cliffs, and open skies.",
  "harvestableParts": {
    "feathers": "150-400 marks each",
    "talons": "250-550 marks",
    "egg": "80,000-180,000 marks"
  }
}

Bestiary: Troll.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Troll",
  "category": "Monster / Regenerating Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A huge, solitary or small-cluster monster known for regeneration, cave dwelling, and violent hunger. Trolls in Balen are not a culture-bearing people; they are dangerous regenerating brutes with animal cunning.",
  "description": "Trolls are hulking, long-limbed monsters with rubbery hide, wet eyes, heavy jaws, and hands large enough to fold a man's ribs inward. Their flesh knits with unnatural speed unless wounds are burned, cauterized, or chemically ruined. Old trolls often smell of damp stone, carrion, and old blood.",
  "knownRegions": "Caves, river gorges, old mines, deep forests, mountain hollows, and abandoned ruins.",
  "behavior": "Hungry, territorial, pain-resistant, and drawn to carrion, cave moisture, and easy prey. They may retreat from fire but return once hunger overwhelms fear.",
  "threats": "Regeneration, crushing strength, bite, thrown rocks, surprise lunges from cave mouths, and endurance in long fights.",
  "weaknesses": "Fire, acid, severing followed by cauterization, sunlight fatigue in some breeds, and trap pits too deep to climb quickly.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "troll blood",
    "regenerative tissue",
    "hide",
    "bone",
    "teeth"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Never a pet or companion. A restrained troll is a disaster waiting to happen.",
  "craftingNotes": "Troll blood is used in healing potions and regeneration salves if stabilized quickly. Hide may become self-mending leather if treated by an expert. Regenerative tissue spoils or becomes dangerous if not preserved.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Confirmed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Legacy harvest/value note preserved from older bestiary scaffold:
{
  "description": "Highly aggressive territorial beasts that live in dark mountain caverns. Extremely regenerative. Their blood is the catalyst for all healing potions.",
  "habitat": "Dark caves and mountain tunnels.",
  "harvestableParts": {
    "hide": "600-1,400 marks",
    "blood": "800-2,200 marks",
    "regenerativeGland": "1,500-3,500 marks"
  }
}

Bestiary: Ash Walker.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Ash Walker",
  "category": "Monster / Cave Hive Predator",
  "publicSummary": "A grey-chitined cave predator resembling a spider and scorpion hybrid, known for moving through ash tunnels, mines, and volcanic stone cracks.",
  "description": "Ash Walkers have low bodies, hooked legs, scorpion-like tails, and layered grey chitin that looks dusted in cooled ash. Their eyes are small and ember-red. They hunt by heat, vibration, and scent, often waiting in chimney cracks or mine supports before dropping onto prey.",
  "knownRegions": "Volcanic caves, old mines, ash tunnels, warm caverns, Republic mining scars, and Drakonan geothermal vents.",
  "behavior": "Small-colony or hive hunters. They avoid heavy rain and open cold, but swarm aggressively in tunnels.",
  "threats": "Pincers, stinger venom, wall-clinging movement, swarm pressure, and ambush from ceiling cracks.",
  "weaknesses": "Cold water, sudden bright light, cracked chitin joints, queen-scent disruption, and open terrain.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "grey chitin",
    "venom sac",
    "ash silk",
    "heat glands",
    "stinger"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Never a normal pet. At most, a contained specimen may be kept by a reckless scholar or alchemist.",
  "craftingNotes": "Grey chitin can become light armor plates. Venom can be refined into paralysis or burning toxins. Ash silk is useful for fire-resistant thread, gloves, and heat wraps. Heat glands can aid forge or alchemy work.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Confirmed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Legacy harvest/value note preserved from older bestiary scaffold:
{
  "description": "Aggressive predatory creatures resembling large cave spider/scorpion hybrids with thick grey chitinous shells. Hive-mind species.",
  "habitat": "Mine shafts, forges, and warm caverns.",

    "chitinShell": "450-950 marks (drone), 4,000-9,000 marks (queen)",

  }
}

Bestiary: Basalt-Back Ravager.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Basalt-Back Ravager",
  "category": "Monster / Magical Beast",
  "publicSummary": "An ape-like tundra and black-stone highland monster with basalt-like growths across its back, shoulders, and arms.",
  "description": "Basalt-Back Ravagers are massive knuckle-walking beasts with heavy forearms, pale frost-resistant hide, and jagged black stone growths along the spine. When angered, they slam their stone backs and fists into cliffs or ice sheets to shake prey off balance before charging.",
  "knownRegions": "Frozen wastes, tundra ridges, black-stone highlands, Drakona's northern reaches, and glacier edges.",
  "behavior": "Territorial, dominance-driven, loud, and aggressive toward anything that meets its stare or challenges its ground.",
  "threats": "Stone-backed charges, crushing fists, thrown boulders, armor-like growths, ice-breaking slams, and battlefield intimidation.",
  "weaknesses": "Soft belly, joints beneath stone plates, balance disruption, thin ice, and heat applied under the stone growths.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "basalt plates",
    "tendon cords",
    "frost-resistant hide",
    "heavy knuckle bones",
    "stone dust"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Never a pet or mount. Too aggressive, too strong, and too dominance-driven to safely train.",
  "craftingNotes": "Basalt plates can reinforce heavy armor or shield faces. Tendon cords can be used for heavy bow tension and bindings. Frost-resistant hide is valued for northern armor and winter gear.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Confirmed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Legacy harvest/value note preserved from older bestiary scaffold:
{
  "description": "Ape-like monsters with stone outcroppings growing from their spinal scales. Bodies are rock-hard but vulnerable to fire.",
  "habitat": "Frozen tundras and arctic mountains.",
  "harvestableParts": {
    "bone": "900-2,200 marks",
    "scales": "1,200-3,000 marks",
    "meat": "150-350 marks"
  }
}

Bestiary: Sand-Wyrm.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Sand-Wyrm",
  "category": "False-Wyrm",
  "publicSummary": "A massive burrowing false-wyrm of Valen's western sands, feared by caravans and desert settlements.",
  "description": "Sand-wyrms are long, armored burrowers with wedge skulls, ringed bodies, chitinous plates, and jaws built to swallow prey whole. Most of the creature remains beneath loose sand, where it senses vibration through the ground. A sand-wyrm attack often begins with silence, then a collapsing road and a mouth full of teeth.",
  "knownRegions": "Republic of Valen's far western sands, desert trade routes, dry basins, and deep dune fields.",
  "behavior": "Ambush predator, vibration-sensitive, avoids stone flats unless starving, drawn to rhythmic caravan movement.",
  "threats": "Burrowing ambush, crushing body, swallowing, sand collapse, and caravan disruption.",
  "weaknesses": "Rocky ground, vibration lures, exposed throat plates, cold nights, and forcing it onto hardpan.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "chitin plates",
    "teeth",
    "gut-stone",
    "heat sacs",
    "sand-slick hide"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Never a pet, mount, or pack animal. Too large, too hungry, and too tied to burrowing instinct.",
  "craftingNotes": "Chitin plates can become desert armor, shield facing, or abrasion-resistant gear. Teeth provide heavy serration. Gut-stone may aid alchemy involving digestion, acid resistance, or heat survival. Heat sacs can be used in desert survival tools.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Confirmed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Crownfield Hart.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Crownfield Hart",
  "category": "Sacred Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A white-gold deer-like animal of Holy Kingdom fields and sacred woods, associated with Elyra/Lady Celestial legends.",
  "description": "Crownfield Harts are tall, graceful deer-like beasts with pale coats, gold-tinted antlers, and calm eyes that reflect light like polished amber. They move through wheat fields and holy groves without bending the stalks as much as their size should. Many Crown villages treat a hart sighting as a blessing, omen, or warning.",
  "knownRegions": "Holy Kingdom farmlands, sacred woods, old Aethelgard groves, pilgrim-road meadows, and protected temple fields.",
  "behavior": "Gentle unless cornered. Often appears before storms, difficult births, funerals, or moments of religious significance.",
  "threats": "Powerful antlers and hooves if threatened, but normally non-hostile.",
  "weaknesses": "Not usually hunted. Can be driven off by fire, loud iron, or corrupted ground.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "naturally shed antlers",
    "hair",
    "hoof shavings only when ethically collected"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Rarely tameable. Usually revered, observed, or protected rather than owned.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Do not treat as a normal pet. A bond with a Crownfield Hart should be sacred, rare, and story-significant.",
  "craftingNotes": "Naturally shed antlers may be used in healing charms, blessing tokens, midwife tools, or willpower-focused holy items. Killing one without cause is a grave religious offense.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Rumor",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Sepulcher Moth.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Sepulcher Moth",
  "category": "Magical Beast / Death-Adjacent",
  "publicSummary": "A large pale moth drawn to graveyards, battlefields, crypts, and old ruins. Its wing dust can trigger flashes of memory from the dead.",
  "description": "Sepulcher Moths are handspan to shield-sized insects with powder-white wings, black eye markings, and soft bodies that glow faintly under moonlight. When disturbed, they shed pale dust that can make the living remember things that never happened to them: a soldier's last breath, a widow's prayer, or a child's burial song.",
  "knownRegions": "Holy Kingdom crypt roads, old Aethelgard ruins, battlefields, graveyards, deep woods, and abandoned churches.",
  "behavior": "Nocturnal, drawn to old grief, candle smoke, burial herbs, and unquiet places.",
  "threats": "Memory confusion, grief hallucinations, swarm suffocation, panic in enclosed crypts.",
  "weaknesses": "Bright dawn light, strong wind, sealed lanterns, smoke discipline, and calm minds.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "wing dust",
    "silk",
    "pale chitin",
    "antennae"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Not a pet. They may be kept briefly by scholars or undertakers only with containment.",
  "craftingNotes": "Wing dust can be used in memory inks, mourning veils, spirit-detection charms, and dangerous grief rites. Silk can be woven into funeral cloth or quieting wraps.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Valen Ironjackal.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Valen Ironjackal",
  "category": "Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A lean badland scavenger-canid known for chewing bone, scrap metal, shell casings, and battlefield refuse.",
  "description": "Ironjackals are long-legged desert scavengers with rust-brown coats, dark muzzles, and jaws strong enough to crack marrow bones and thin scrap. Packs trail foundries, rail works, old battlefields, and caravans. Their teeth are often stained black by soot and metal dust.",
  "knownRegions": "Republic of Valen badlands, industrial outskirts, dry roads, mine edges, and battlefield ruins.",
  "behavior": "Pack scavenger, opportunistic, clever, skittish around gunfire but attracted to aftermath.",
  "threats": "Pack tactics, metal-cracking jaws, disease, night harassment, and theft of supplies.",
  "weaknesses": "Water scarcity, loud sudden shots, pack leader disruption, baited traps.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "jawbone",
    "hide",
    "scent glands",
    "teeth",
    "ears"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Sometimes tameable with skill, especially young or rescued animals.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Uses petCompanionSystem. Roles may include Tracker, Guard, Combat, and Utility. Public risk Common to Restricted depending region.",
  "craftingNotes": "Jawbones can reinforce trap mechanisms or intimidation charms. Scent glands assist tracking lures. Hide is tough and heat-tolerant.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Powderback Beetle.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Powderback Beetle",
  "category": "Magical Beast / Hazard Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A large dryland beetle that accumulates volatile mineral dust beneath its shell and can burst into sparks or explosive powder clouds when startled.",
  "description": "Powderback Beetles are knee-high to waist-high insects with layered brown shells, yellow mineral seams, and powder vents along the back. When threatened, they hiss, rattle, and release a dry mineral cloud that can flash with sparks or ignite near open flame.",
  "knownRegions": "Republic drylands, badland scrub, powder mines, sunbaked gullies, and arid farms.",
  "behavior": "Usually non-aggressive unless stepped on, cornered, or exposed to fire. Swarms panic easily.",
  "threats": "Explosive dust clouds, fire hazard, shell impact, swarm panic, and chain reactions near powder stores.",
  "weaknesses": "Water, cold, careful approach, soft underside, and avoiding sparks.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "powder sacs",
    "shell plates",
    "ignition glands",
    "mineral dust"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not normally tameable. Can sometimes be farmed or contained by specialists.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Not a normal pet. At most, handled as hazardous livestock or alchemical stock.",
  "craftingNotes": "Powder sacs can be refined into alchemical powder, blast traps, or fire starters. Shell plates resist heat and abrasion. Ignition glands are dangerous but valuable to artificers.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Sylvera Glasswing Mantis.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Sylvera Glasswing Mantis",
  "category": "Magical Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A huge camouflaged jungle mantis with translucent wings like stained glass leaves.",
  "description": "Glasswing Mantises hang among jungle branches with their wings spread flat, catching light like wet leaves or colored glass. Their forelimbs are long, hooked, and sharp enough to shear through vines, leather, and careless limbs. Many victims never see the mantis until the canopy moves.",
  "knownRegions": "Sylvera jungles, World-Root canopies, overgrown ruins, humid ravines, and old forest roads.",
  "behavior": "Solitary ambush predator. Still for hours, then explosively fast. Avoids prey that notices it too early.",
  "threats": "Camouflage, scythe forelimbs, sudden drop attacks, wing distraction, and silent movement through branches.",
  "weaknesses": "Disturbed reflections, low light, wing vibration, smoke, and attacks to the hinge joints.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "glasswing membranes",
    "scythe claws",
    "chitin",
    "compound eyes"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Never a normal pet. It can be studied, avoided, or hunted.",
  "craftingNotes": "Glasswing membranes can support reflective cloaks, light-bending veils, or silent glider cloth. Scythe claws can become cutting blades or sickle-like tools. Chitin is light and flexible.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Worldroot Boa.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Worldroot Boa",
  "category": "Magical Beast",
  "publicSummary": "A giant serpent of Sylvera old forests that mimics roots and vines until prey comes within reach.",
  "description": "Worldroot Boas are enormous serpents with bark-pattern scales, vine-like ridges, and eyes the color of wet moss. When still, they look like exposed roots twisting around ancient trees. When they move, whole sections of the forest seem to come alive.",
  "knownRegions": "Sylvera old forests, World-Root networks, deep jungle ravines, and overgrown temple foundations.",
  "behavior": "Ambush constrictor. Patient, silent, and drawn to warm-blooded prey that rests against roots or fallen logs.",
  "threats": "Constriction, camouflage, crushing coils, sudden strikes, and dragging prey into root tangles.",
  "weaknesses": "Cold iron hooks, firelight, vibration, attacks behind the jaw, and forcing it into open ground.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "scales",
    "fangs",
    "hide",
    "root-mimic glands",
    "sinew"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Never a normal pet or companion. Too large, too instinct-driven, and too dangerous.",
  "craftingNotes": "Hide can become camouflage leather or flexible armor. Fangs can be used for poison tools. Root-mimic glands are valuable in stealth oils, camouflage dyes, and trap lures.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Wyrmspine Snow Roc.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Wyrmspine Snow Roc",
  "category": "Magical Beast / Aerial Predator",
  "publicSummary": "A huge white-grey mountain bird that hunts along Wyrmspine updrafts and can threaten careless flyers or small airships.",
  "description": "Snow Rocs have vast white-grey wings, hooked black beaks, talons like curved ice picks, and hollow bones that make them lighter than their size suggests. They ride mountain winds in near silence, then fall from the clouds with enough force to tear a mount from the air.",
  "knownRegions": "Wyrmspine Ridges, Drakona highlands, frozen cliffs, glacier valleys, and high mountain passes.",
  "behavior": "Aerial ambush predator, territorial near nests, drawn to wounded flying mounts and isolated air traffic.",
  "threats": "Dive attacks, wing gusts, talons, altitude drops, and attempts to knock prey into cliffs or ravines.",
  "weaknesses": "Low valleys, wing strain, storm timing, heavy fog, and grounding tactics.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "feathers",
    "talons",
    "hollow bones",
    "beak fragments",
    "down"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Rarely tameable, mostly by expert aeries or handlers raising young birds.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Uses petCompanionSystem only in rare expert cases. Public risk Restricted to Dangerous.",
  "craftingNotes": "Feathers and down are valuable for cold-weather cloaks and flight charms. Talons can become piercing tools. Hollow bones can reinforce lightweight spear shafts or aerial gear.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Hot-Spring Nix.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Hot-Spring Nix",
  "category": "Magical Beast / Environmental Creature",
  "publicSummary": "A small amphibious mineral-spring creature found in Drakonan hot springs and geothermal pools.",
  "description": "Hot-Spring Nix are palm-sized to forearm-length amphibians with slick mineral scales, wide dark eyes, and frilled gills that shimmer with steam. They are usually harmless unless grabbed or crushed, at which point they release numbing secretions that cloud the water.",
  "knownRegions": "Drakona hot springs, Wyrmspine geothermal pools, mineral caves, and warm waterfall basins.",
  "behavior": "Skittish, social in small clusters, drawn to warm mineral water and algae films.",
  "threats": "Numbing defensive secretions, slippery swarms, contaminated water if mishandled.",
  "weaknesses": "Cold water, dry air, gentle netting, and mineral imbalance.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "numbing secretion",
    "mineral scales",
    "gill frills",
    "spring mucus"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not a normal pet, but may be kept in specialized mineral aquariums.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Usually environmental or alchemical, not a companion.",
  "craftingNotes": "Numbing secretion can be refined into painkiller salves or mild numbing toxins. Mineral scales may support heat-resistant oils or bathhouse medicines.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Rumor",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Blightglass Stag.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Blightglass Stag",
  "category": "Blighted Creature",
  "publicSummary": "A deer-like animal consumed by Obsidian Blight, leaving mirror-black fragile antlers and glassy hide.",
  "description": "A Blightglass Stag looks at first like a sick deer or hart, until light catches its body and shows mirror-black hide where living color should be. Its antlers resemble fragile obsidian branches. When broken, they crumble into fine black sand. Most are not truly alive in a normal sense; they are walking signs that life mana has been consumed from the land.",
  "knownRegions": "Obsidian Blight zones, dead farms, cursed groves, battlefields, and places touched by life-draining corruption.",
  "behavior": "Erratic, wrong-footed, often silent. Some flee until cornered; others charge as if driven by something hollow.",
  "threats": "Shattering antlers, blight dust, panic aura, contaminated wounds, and spreading fear among livestock.",
  "weaknesses": "Purification, light rites, careful ranged destruction, avoiding dust inhalation, and containment.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "blightglass antler fragments under containment",
    "black sand residue under containment"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Not tameable.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Never a pet. Blighted creatures cannot be tamed or safely kept.",
  "craftingNotes": "Blightglass is unsafe without containment. It may be studied by priests, magi-knights, or authorized Slayers, but ordinary crafting use is dangerous and often illegal.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": "The bestiary should not explain Daemon links unless the character has earned secret knowledge."
}

Bestiary: Hearthtail Wyvern.text:

{
  "creatureName": "Hearthtail Wyvern",
  "category": "False-Wyrm / Wyvern Subspecies",
  "size": "Small",
  "length": "3 to 4 feet",
  "maturity": "2 to 3 months",
  "lifespan": "40 to 60 years",
  "publicRisk": "Common to Restricted",
  "publicSummary": "A small, thin-bodied, catlike wyvern breed kept as a household companion, pest-hunter, and mischievous exotic pet.",
  "description": "Hearthtail Wyverns are small, thin-bodied wyverns known for catlike behavior, playful mischief, and a strong preference for warm places. They are not built for war, riding, or heavy work. They have the classic wyvern shape of hind legs, wing-forearms, a draconic head, and a long tail, but their bodies are slim and flexible. Their wings let them flutter between rafters, windowsills, rooftops, shelves, and tree branches. They are agile gliders rather than strong long-distance fliers. Hearthtails act much like cats: sleeping in sunbeams, curling near hearths, knocking small objects from shelves, stealing shiny trinkets, weaving around ankles, and claiming warm spaces as their own. Despite their docile nature, they are still false-wyrms, with needle teeth, sharp claws, and tiny fire glands capable of short puffs of flame or hot sparks.",
  "knownRegions": "Towns, noble homes, mage households, warm inns, general stores with exotic pets, farms needing pest-hunters, and hearth-warm settlements across Balen.",
  "behavior": "Playful, curious, heat-seeking, affectionate on their own terms, possessive, mischievous, fond of shiny objects.",
  "threats": "Sharp claws, quick bites, short-range flame puffs, sudden gliding leaps, and territorial jealousy.",
  "weaknesses": "Fragile compared to larger wyverns, easily distracted by food or toys, poor cold endurance, and not suited for labor or serious combat.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "scales",
    "claws",
    "teeth",
    "wing membranes",
    "small fire glands",
    "tail spines"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Tameable. Often purchased already trained or raised as a pet.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Excellent pet-class wyvern. Roles: Utility, Scout, Pest-Hunter, Companion. Too small to serve as a mount. Purchased Hearthtails usually begin at 50 relationship: Obedient.",
  "craftingNotes": "Small fire glands can be used in minor ignition tools or alchemy. Scales can be decorative or used in charms. Wing membranes can be used in lightweight pouches, delicate glider cloth, or spellcraft components.",

  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Skysunderer Wyvern.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Skysunderer Wyvern",
  "category": "False-Wyrm / Wyvern Subspecies",
  "size": "Small to Medium",
  "length": "Roughly direwolf-sized at maturity",
  "maturity": "Roughly 14 days",
  "lifespan": "Roughly 200 years",
  "publicRisk": "Restricted to Dangerous",
  "publicSummary": "A smaller wyvern named for its shrill cry, known for quick imprinting, docile bonding, venom tail barb, fire glands, and fierce pack loyalty.",
  "description": "Skysunderer Wyverns are smaller wyverns named for the shrill, piercing cry they emit when frightened, aggressive, or defending their pack. Though armed like dangerous predators, they are unusually docile by wyvern standards and imprint quickly. They recognize those who feed them as providers and may become fiercely loyal to those they consider pack. Skysunderers mature at a startling rate, reaching adult size and basic hunting capability within roughly fourteen days of hatching. Their first two weeks are the critical imprinting and training window. Despite their rapid maturity, Skysunderers are long-lived and may survive for roughly two centuries, making them lifelong companions or even family guardians passed between generations. They have powerful hind legs, wing-forearms, sharp claws, barbed venom tails, and fire glands capable of short bursts of flame. Young Skysunderers often act catlike, curling in heated places, stealing warm blankets, riding on their handler's shoulder when small enough, and kneading claws into leather or bedding. As they mature, they become more task-oriented, preferring to fly overhead, perch on ledges, watch doors, or shadow their handler from above.",
  "knownRegions": "Aethelgard highlands, Drakonan lower ridges, cliffside nests, warm barns, hunting lodges, and regions where trained wyvern companions are tolerated.",
  "behavior": "Docile when bonded, defensive of pack, quick to imprint, heat-seeking, alert, vocal under stress.",
  "threats": "Shrill stunning cry, venom tail barb, fire breath, fast flight, sharp claws, pack-defense behavior.",
  "weaknesses": "Food-driven, protective instincts can be exploited, vulnerable wing membranes, and overreacts to threats against bonded handlers.",
  "harvestableMaterials": [
    "venom barb",
    "fire glands",
    "claws",
    "teeth",
    "wing membrane",
    "hide",
    "vocal throat sac"
  ],
  "tamingStatus": "Tameable, especially if fed, rescued, or raised young.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Excellent companion candidate. Roles: Scout, Combat, Guard, Utility. Not large enough for most adult riders. Gains a named bone whistle when tamed. Early training matters because maturity arrives in roughly 14 days.",
  "craftingNotes": "Venom barb can support toxins or piercing weapons. Fire glands can support fire-aspected alchemy. Throat sac may be used in sonic alarms, whistles, or intimidation charms.",
  "knowledgeTier": "Confirmed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Bestiary: Desert Trotter Wyvern.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "Desert Trotter Wyvern",
  "category": "False-Wyrm / Wyvern Subspecies",
  "size": "Medium to Large",
  "length": "Varies by breed line; generally mount-sized",
  "maturity": "6 to 8 months",
  "lifespan": "60 to 90 years",
  "publicRisk": "Restricted",
  "publicSummary": "A docile ground-running wyvern of arid lands, known for raptor-like posture, strong hind legs, folded wings, beaked face, and colorful feathers blended with scales.",
  "description": "Desert Trotter Wyverns are ground-running wyverns adapted to arid terrain. They have extremely strong hind legs, flexible forearms, and thinner wings than standard wyverns. Rather than relying on sustained flight, they move in a raptor-like trot on their hind legs or sprint on all fours when speed matters. Their wings fold tight against the body unless they extend their forearms, allowing short-distance glides across dunes, cliffs, ravines, and broken badland terrain. Desert Trotters are visually distinct for their beaked faces and the mixture of feathers and scales along their bodies, often in vibrant ochre, red, teal, gold, blue, sun-bleached white, or patterned desert green. Despite being carnivorous like other wyverns, they are naturally docile and commonly tamed as mounts in dry regions.",
  "knownRegions": "Republic of Valen drylands, badlands, desert roads, caravan towns, arid farms, and western frontier routes.",

Bestiary: TyrWing Wyvern.text:
Balen Slayer's Bestiary creature reference. This is normal surface-world ecological/Slayer knowledge unless the entry says otherwise.

{
  "creatureName": "TyrWing Wyvern",

  "length": "Up to 30 feet",
  "wingspan": "Up to 35 feet",
  "maturity": "2 to 3 years",
  "lifespan": "120 to 160 years",
  "publicRisk": "Dangerous",
  "publicSummary": "The classic large red wyvern of Balen, fire-breathing, powerful, common, and best tamed through hatchling imprinting.",
  "description": "TyrWings are the most common classic wyverns in Balen. They are large, red-scaled, fire-breathing false-wyrms with powerful hind legs, sharp fangs, long claws, and broad wings. Though they have wing-forearms, they often perch upright on their hind legs like massive predatory birds. TyrWings are wild by nature and require a strong, consistent handler. They are easiest to tame at birth, as hatchlings imprint on the first creature they see after hatching. Imprinting does not make them safe immediately; young TyrWings are affectionate, hungry, and dangerous by accident. Once bonded, TyrWings can become deeply affectionate toward their master, but their appetite remains a constant problem. An underfed TyrWing is not safe around livestock, strangers, or unsecured meat stores.",
  "knownRegions": "Common across Balen in cliffs, mountains, forests, farmlands near wild ridges, and regions with large prey.",

    "wing sinew",
    "hide",
    "bones",
    "heart"

  "tamingStatus": "Tameable, but difficult unless imprinted at birth. Adult wild TyrWings are dangerous and hard to control.",
  "petSystemNotes": "Uses petCompanionSystem. Best tamed at hatchling stage. Roles: Transport, Combat, Guard, Pack.",
  "craftingNotes": "Fire glands support fire-aspected alchemy and weapon effects. Red scales can be used in heat-resistant armor. Claws and teeth are excellent for piercing weapons and intimidation pieces. Wing sinew supports powerful bows or siege-tension mechanisms.",

  "publicRisk": "Restricted to Dangerous",
  "publicSummary": "A grey-to-black nocturnal wyvern resembling a scaled bat, with silent flight, needle teeth, venom tail, echolocation, and heat-sense.",
  "description": "Darkwing Seekers are night-hunting wyverns with grey to obsidian-black scales, bat-like wings, needle teeth, and barbed venom tails. They resemble large scaled bats more than classic red wyverns. Their flight is nearly silent, making them feared by night watchmen, cave travelers, and isolated shepherds. Darkwings are nearly blind in daylight. In darkness, however, they navigate through echolocation and heat-sensing organs, allowing them to hunt in caves, forests, ruins, and moonless skies. They prefer ambush, silence, and precision over brute force. They can be tamed, but a handler must respect their nocturnal nature, avoid bright-light punishment, and train them through sound, scent, heat cues, and routine.",

  "petSystemNotes": "Uses petCompanionSystem. Roles: Scout, Combat, Guard, Tracker, Utility. Excellent night companion but poor daylight mount. May require shaded rest or hooding in bright environments.",

  "description": "Silkwing Wyverns are slender, elegant courier wyverns with long translucent wing membranes and narrow bodies built for speed rather than strength. They are weak in direct combat compared to most wyverns, but their agility, trainability, and speed make them valuable messengers, scouts, and noble household companions. Silkwings have delicate features, smooth scales, fine wing veining, and soft vocal clicks. Their wings shimmer faintly in bright light, giving them their name. They are nervous around loud crowds but bond well with patient handlers. A Silkwing cannot carry an adult rider, but it can carry messages, small parcels, alchemical vials, keys, ribbons, or light tools.",

Hornbacks are practical beasts: strong, stubborn, stable, and hard to scare once trained. A Hornback can pull sledges, carry packs, guard a caravan, or haul salvage from monster hunts. They are most common around frontier towns, Drakonan lower settlements, Valen trade roads, and rugged rural routes where ordinary pack animals are too fragile.",

Their scales are pale blue, white, silver-grey, or frost-black, often threaded with visible blue veins that glow faintly when they breathe cold. Unlike fire-breathing wyverns, Frostveins exhale freezing mist or supercooled vapor. This breath can numb exposed flesh, frost over weapons, slick stone, and slow prey. They are quieter than TyrWings but more patient, waiting in snow shelves or cliffside fog before striking. Frostveins are prized by northern handlers but difficult to raise outside cold regions.",

  "knowledgeTier": "Observed",
  "lockedLore": ""
}

Hidden Truth: The Full Truth of Balen.text:
SECRET / ENDGAME-ONLY NARRATOR LORE. Do not reveal casually; reveal only through earned endgame conditions, direct divine testimony, the Abyssal Tower ending, the World's Maw revelation, or explicit testing trigger if present.

{
  "name": "The Full Truth of Balen",
  "secret": true,
  "endgameOnly": true,
  "revealConditions": [
    "The players complete the Abyssal Tower by killing the Shayde Matriarch and witnessing the golden revelation after the Hells-gate bursts closed.",
    "The players cross beyond the World's Maw and are exposed to divine truth before mortal minds are destroyed by overwhelming divine light.",
    "A player action contains the exact testing command {FULL TRUTH CODE:1517}.",
    "Direct divine testimony from Dominus, Lady Celestial, or an equivalent endgame-level source."
  ],
  "forbiddenRevealConditions": [
    "Do not reveal through ordinary scholars.",
    "Do not reveal through common religious teaching.",
    "Do not reveal through standard ruins.",
    "Do not reveal through normal bestiary or history books.",
    "Do not reveal through casual NPC exposition.",
    "Do not reveal through ordinary Magi-Knight records.",
    "Do not reveal through normal Slayer Guild knowledge."
  ],
  "summary": "Balen is not the whole mortal world. It is a surviving fragment of a much larger mortal realm that was sundered after the Celestials tried to ascend into the realm of the gods and opened the way to both the Pantheon and the Hells. The resulting Shayde catastrophe destroyed most of the old world, led to the birth of true dragons from Xaveraxis the Golden, created the Crystal Crown from his severed crystal scale, and ended with Balen colliding into the Pantheon while the World's Maw was raised as both seal and mercy. Eckhart, a mortal knight of Balen, later slew the corrupted Xaveraxis, became the first Magi-Knight, inherited the mantle of Dominion, and became Dominus. Lady Celestial, a surviving Celestial, became his eternal counter and lover as the Goddess of Will.",
  "testRevealCommand": "{FULL TRUTH CODE:1517}",
  "fullRevealScriptRef": "fullTruthRevealSystem.fullTruthRevealScript.script",
  "scriptedRevealRequired": true
}

Quest Generation Guidance.text: Index entry. Detailed quest generation rules are stored in worldLore entries beginning with "Quest Guidance:". Use these rules for generated quests, contracts, quest boards, rewards, faction motives, travel obligations, bestiary handling, economy continuity, and lore-safe quest hooks.

Resource Update Guidance.text: Index entry. Detailed resource update rules are stored in worldLore entries beginning with "Resource Update Guidance:". Use these rules for HP, mana, stamina, Breathing, rations, currency stack updates, resistance, armor mitigation, damage attribution, NPC threat scaling, and outcome impact when resolving actions.

Neutral Trade Guilds.text: The Neutral Trade Guilds are Aethelgard-aligned but not a national government.
