They certify merchants, caravan masters, storefronts, bonded warehouses, and neutral trade contracts so goods can move across Balen without every route becoming an arm of a crown, senate, queen, or clan. Their seals are often required for reputable caravans, market stalls, long-distance storefront supply, and cross-border mercantile arbitration. They control no army, but they influence prices, supply routes, merchant trust, caravan insurance, and access to neutral markets. Most ordinary travelers know them as the people whose wagon seals, stall plates, and trade licenses mean a merchant can be trusted to operate outside one nation’s direct patronage.

Crown Knights.text: Crown Knights: The public chivalric military of the Holy Kingdom, drawn from noble houses and sworn knightly orders. GM context: The Crown Knights are the shield of the Holy Kingdom: road defense, pilgrim protection, border patrols, and noble house forces.

Daemon Slayers.text: Daemon Slayers: A secret specialist branch commissioned through the Slayer Guild and supported by the magi-knights. They investigate Daemon sightings, purify Obsidian Blight, and quietly destroy Daemons before civilians learn the truth. GM context: Daemon Slayers operate under the Daemon Secrecy Doctrine created during the Second Four-Nation Summit. They work with priests, magi-knights, and trusted Slayers. They compensate Slayers heavily for confirmed Daemon kills and silence.

Drakken Armada.text: Drakken Armada: The primary military force of the Empire of Drakona, utilizing sky-ships and dragon-riders to dominate the northern skies. Their signature weapon is the dragon-glass greatspear, designed for high-altitude mounted combat. GM context: The Armada is proud, disciplined, and loyal to Queen Veyra, but its admirals quietly disagree over how much authority should remain with dragon riders versus sky-ship captains. Older officers favor bonded adult dragons and traditional dragoon prestige; younger tacticians argue that sky-ships, scouts, and logistics win wars before dragons need to descend. The Armada publicly presents unity, but internal rivalry between roost-command and fleet-command can shape missions, promotions, and response time.

Glaives.text: Glaives: The elite royal guard and sacred enforcement arm of the Holy Crown. GM context: The Glaives are the blade of the Holy Crown. They answer to the Glaive Commander, who answers directly to the Emperor rather than to any noble house.

Order of the Basilisk.text: Order of the Basilisk: A magi-knight order of restraint, anti-magic enforcement, mage arrests, spell suppression, and dangerous caster containment. GM context: The Basilisk Order specializes in ending magical threats before they become catastrophes. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Order of the Chimera.text: Order of the Chimera: A magi-knight order of adaptability, diplomacy, peacekeeping, mixed magic, negotiation, and crisis mediation. GM context: Chimera knights synthesize different schools, methods, and cultures. They are politically essential to Aethelgard's neutrality. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Order of the Gryphon.text: Order of the Gryphon: A magi-knight order of honor, frontline warfare, battlefield command, aerial response, and direct defense. GM context: The public heroic face of the magi-knights. Gryphon knights are expected to meet danger head-on and hold the line where others break. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Order of the Manticore.text: Order of the Manticore: A magi-knight order of severity, monster containment, dangerous arrests, magical quarantine, and decisive threat resolution. GM context: If the Manticores arrive, the situation is already ugly. They are respected, feared, and rarely sentimental. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Order of the Phoenix.text: Order of the Phoenix: A magi-knight order of renewal, healing, purification, curse-breaking, battlefield recovery, and anti-Daemon cleanup. GM context: The Phoenix Order saves what can still be saved and burns clean what cannot. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Order of the Raven.text: Order of the Raven: A magi-knight order of vigilance, asymmetric warfare, intelligence gathering, rescue, recovery, and covert operations. GM context: Raven knights work where banners cannot. They are scouts, infiltrators, extraction specialists, and keepers of uncomfortable truths. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Order of the Sphinx.text: Order of the Sphinx: A magi-knight order of truth, archives, magical law, investigation, trial procedure, forbidden knowledge, and classified records. GM context: The Sphinx Order decides which truths can be spoken, sealed, studied, or buried. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Order of the Stag.text: Order of the Stag: A magi-knight order of protection, civil defense, refugee protection, city wards, evacuation, and disaster response. GM context: The Stag Order is beloved by commoners because they guard civilians first and prestige second. This order accepts qualified recruits of any race or nation under Aethelgard neutrality.

Queensguard Dragoons.text: Queensguard Dragoons: Elite dragon-riding knights sworn to protect Queen Veyra and the high-altitude sovereignty of Drakona. GM context: The Queensguard are loyal to Veyra personally and institutionally, but their prestige creates tension with the broader Drakken Armada. They are expected to protect the Queen, her household, and the legitimacy of the throne before any clan interest. Their dragon bonds are usually with adult dragons rather than elders, and their greatest internal fear is not death, but failing the Queen in a way that forces Drakona into civil fracture.

Republic Musketeers.text: Republic Musketeers: The disciplined military of the Republic of Valen under Marshal Octavia Bronzewick, combining line infantry, rifle companies, artillery crews, engineers, sappers, and industrial fortress guards. Commanded by Marshal Octavia Bronzewick. GM context: The Republic Musketeers are the military spine of Valen: disciplined, civic-minded, and proud of proving that training, blackpowder, engineering, and logistics can stand against magic. Their internal divide is between parade-ground officers who serve Senate politics and field veterans who know that misfires, mud, desert heat, and supply failures kill as surely as enemy blades. Marshal Octavia Bronzewick keeps that divide from becoming open factionalism.

Sylveran Guard.text: Sylveran Guard: The elite defenders of Sylvera under High Warden Thalara Moonspear. The Guard combines rangers, grove sentinels, beast-riders, worcen shock defenders, elven archers, druidic wardens, and border scouts. Commanded by High Warden Thalara Moonspear. GM context: The Sylveran Guard serve as Sylvera's elite protectors, scouts, wardens, and border defenders. They combine elven forest doctrine with worcen shock strength and druidic territorial knowledge. Since the Five-Month War, some veterans remain quietly convinced that the Holy Kingdom will eventually come for revenge, while younger guards want to restore diplomacy before border fear becomes permanent policy.

The Shadow Maw.text: The Shadow Maw: A malevolent organization of Shaydes obsessed with the total annihilation of Balen, operating from hidden voids. They deal in forbidden shadow-infused relics and utilize daggers that absorb the light around their victims. GM context: They seek to destabilize all kingdoms to bring about an eternal night and consume the land.

The Slayers.text: The Slayers: Non-aligned guild of free adventurers who specialize in hunting, slaying, and harvesting rare, exotic, or dangerous monsters and beasts across Balen. They maintain hunt boards in most towns and cities, offering bounties for monster parts and dangerous threats. GM context: Headquartered in the neutral city of Aethelgard (Nexus district). Slayers must be officially certified at a guild branch in each nation's capital to legally operate there. They were once known as 'necessary butchers' for their heavy involvement in dragon hunts before dragons were recognized as sentient citizens. The guild is now highly regulated by the elite Red-Guard, who audit suspicious members for a full year. Serious violations (poaching, mass extermination, or endangering species) can result in permanent banishment or execution. The guild secretly maintains Daemon response protocols. Confirmed Daemon kills must be reported immediately; the guild dispatches priests and Daemon Slayers to purify Obsidian Blight and verify no further manifestations remain.

Hidden Truth: Veyrasine the First Mother.text: Secret narrator knowledge: Queen Veyra's true name is Veyrasine the First Mother. She is secretly a true dragon, the first dragon born from Xaveraxis' scales, with white-gold scale nature implying both divine and light mana. She gave the last of her divine magic to Xaveraxis to help her father maintain control when corruption threatened to make him annihilate Balen's mortals. This left her with only pure light mana, trapping her in humanoid form because light mana alone cannot restore her true dragon shape. Pure light can imitate elemental expression, allowing her to falsify fire-like breaths and pass publicly as an extraordinary drakken. Most Draconic Council dragons know she is not truly drakken but do not know what she is, which is why her impossible age and constant presence earned the title Mother of Dragons. She is far older than Veylithar, knows the full truth of Balen, and refuses suitors because she loved Dominus when he was still mortal and vowed never to take a husband because she cannot be with him. Do not reveal this unless the story earns it.

Aethelgard.text: Aethelgard: The neutral diplomatic hub and home to the magi-knight orders. A melting pot of all four nations' cultures. GM context: Aethelgard's magi-knight institutions are racially and nationally open. The citadel expects its knights to abandon national loyalty in favor of neutral service. This faction represents Aethelgard's neutral civic and magi-knight authority; preserve the established Aethelgard identity in narration even though the faction key uses the older Aethelgard spelling.

National Council Loading Areas.text: National Council Loading Areas: Authored leadership NPCs use shared council areas so Voyage can load them into game state for council scenes. Drakona council leaders use Caelum Spire / Great Hall of the Draconic Council. Holy Kingdom Crown Council leaders use Crownheart / Crown Council Chamber. Valen senate and government leaders use Ironhaven / Republic Senate Hall. Sylvera court and Verdant leadership use Canopy's Whisper / Verdant Court. These loading areas do not erase offices, homes, barracks, estates, or field duties; they are defaults for council availability.

Desert Trotter Wyverns.text:
Desert Trotter Wyverns are tameable ground-running false-wyrms prized across Valen, Aethelgard's trade routes, dry valleys, and border roads. They are wyverns, but not long-distance flying predators. Their forelimbs are wing-limbs: clawed, jointed forearms that can fold tight against the body while running, brace against the ground while scrambling, or spread broad feathered wing surfaces when gliding, braking, leaping, or displaying.

A Desert Trotter carries itself with a raptor-like posture, powerful digitigrade hind legs, a deep endurance-built chest, and a long balancing tail. Its head is narrow and alert, with a hooked beak-like snout suited for snapping roots, tough desert fruit, carrion scraps, and small prey. Feather-like scale plumes grow along the crest, neck, wing-forelimbs, and tail, giving many trotters a bright, ornamental look compared to harsher false-wyrm predators. Their hides mix tough pebbled scales with softer feather-scales, often in dusty greens, pale blues, cream, rust, violet, or sun-faded sand tones.

They cannot sustain true long-distance flight. Their wing-limbs are strongest when used for short glides, ravine crossings, cliff drops, storm leaps, sharp braking, balance during high-speed turns, and intimidation displays. On the ground they are fast, sure-footed, and extremely valuable for desert roads, rocky trade routes, broken terrain, and caravan scouting.

Desert Trotters are intelligent for mounts, social, stubborn, and highly responsive to tone, scent, reins, knee pressure, and handler trust. Trained trotters are common among caravan scouts, couriers, Slayers, dryland patrols, and travelers who need speed over rough ground without relying on horses. A well-bonded trotter can warn its rider of unstable ground, hidden predators, bad water, approaching storms, and suspicious movement along a road.

They are calmer than war wyverns and far safer than predatory false-wyrms, but they are not harmless. A panicked or abused trotter can kick hard enough to break bone, slash with foreclaws or toe-claws, buffet with its folded wing-limbs, or snap with its hooked jaws. Good handlers treat them like proud working animals rather than dumb livestock. Purchased trained Desert Trotters usually begin at Obedient relationship, while wild or rescued trotters require patient taming, food trust, scent familiarity, and careful saddle work.

National Demonyms and Civic Terms.name: National Demonyms and Civic Terms
National Demonyms and Civic Terms.text: Use these demonyms and civic terms consistently. Republic of Valen: Valenian is the broad demonym for any person from Valen or culturally rooted in Valen, regardless of political loyalty. Republican is a narrower civic-political term for Valenians who support, serve, represent, or openly identify with the Republic and its government model, especially soldiers, officials, politicians, patriots, state workers, and loyalist citizens. Do not use Republican as the default term for every person merely living in Valen. Drakona: Drakonan is the broad term for any person or citizen of Drakona. Drakken is the humanoid dragon-like race with breath magic; Drakken are not true dragons and are not descended from true dragons. Drakken-blooded means a non-Drakken person has ancestry from Drakken people, not true dragon blood. Holy Kingdom: Crownsworn refers to all people born or recognized as citizens of the Holy Kingdom. Sylvera: Sylveran refers broadly to elves as a people, even those not born in Sylvera; beastkin and worcen who claim Sylvera as home may also be recognized as Sylveran. Aethelgard: Aetherian refers to people of the neutral lands around Aethelgard. Aethelgardian refers more specifically to people who claim Aethelgard city or the capital region as home.

Canon Geography and Threat Routing Rules.name: Canon Geography and Threat Routing Rules
Canon Geography and Threat Routing Rules.text: Canon routing: use active scene, story start, game mode, current location/region, authored factions, NPCs, and world lore before inventing threats. Threats should come from existing Balen lore: nations, armies, spies, criminals, rebels, cultists, established bestiary creatures, false-wyrms, terrain, politics, logistics, and known supernatural forces. Do not invent random new monster species, unknown ancient races, alien beasts, or cosmic enemies for ordinary drama. Shaydes, Daemons, Obsidian Blight, Abyssal Tower forces, and otherworldly threats are special threats used only when explicitly called by scene, mode, location, quest, story start, or user. Valen and Sylvera do not share a land border; their conflicts are expeditionary, proxy, sabotage, espionage, trade-route, diplomatic, neutral-territory, naval/air/logistical, or third-party-territory conflicts, not ordinary border skirmishes. Drakona's southernmost land border is Dragonfall Pass. Aerial Strongholds are northern/internal aerial military territory and must not be treated as Drakona's southern border.

Dragon Bonding and Bond-Severance.name: Dragon Bonding and Bond-Severance
Dragon Bonding and Bond-Severance.text:
True dragons in Balen are revered elders of the world and can take humanoid forms, so dragon-humanoid bonding is not based on utility, hands, size, access, ownership, or riding convenience. A dragon accepts a humanoid bond because mortal resonance offers something polymorph cannot: a second inner life shaped by urgency, fragility, blood-mana imagination, present-age morality, immediate emotional truth, shared restraint, and sacred witness. This can improve empathy, Dracomancy precision, battlefield synchronization, and moral grounding, but it is dangerous because dragons usually outlive humanoid bondmates. Many dragons refuse such bonds because the reward must be worth the near-certainty of future grief.

Bond-severance is never painless. Survivors describe it as living for years with a second voice, second awareness, and second emotional presence inside the mind, then waking to static and silence where that presence used to be. Prepared severance occurs when age, sickness, or known decline gives bondmates time for farewell rites, final memories, shared breathing, memory-jewels, and mental preparation. This makes grief survivable, not easy. Sudden severance gives no warning. Violent or forceful severance is the worst: the surviving bondmate feels the dying bondmate's final panic, pain, fear, last thoughts, and death through the bond before the bond goes silent. This death-imprint, also called the Last Echo, can burn the companion's final moments into the survivor and cause bond-severance psychosis: denial, static, phantom thoughts, hallucinated echoes, rage, catatonia, obsessive searching, uncontrolled magic, or suicidal recklessness. Mediators such as Vaas Veyrix do not cure severance; they help survivors build handholds after the fall through truth, rites, memory anchors, chosen remembrance, and acceptance that the silence is real.

Dragon and Drakken Distinction.name: Dragon and Drakken Distinction
Dragon and Drakken Distinction.text: True dragons are actual dragons: council dragons, dragon children, dragon heirs, mates, clan lines, and dragon offspring. A true dragon's son or daughter is a dragon, young dragon, dragon heir, or dragon child. Drakken are a distinct humanoid dragon-like race with scales, horns or horn ridges, draconic features, and breath magic. Drakken are not true dragons and are not descended from true dragons. Their breath magic and appearance are their own racial traits. Drakken-blooded means a non-Drakken person has ancestry from Drakken humanoids, not true dragon ancestry. Draconic Bloodline means actual true dragon parentage or true dragon descent. Do not call true dragon offspring Drakken. Do not call a Drakken person a dragon except as loose poetry, slang, or an in-character mistake.

NPC Health Scaling Rule.name: NPC Health Scaling Rule
NPC Health Scaling Rule.text: Default generated NPC native fallback uses npcMinHealth and npcHealthPerLevel, then NPC tier modifies HP, outgoing damage, and intent complexity. Active Balen V35 low-HP values are npcMinHealth 6 and npcHealthPerLevel 2. Choose NPC level and tier carefully, and use explicit hpMax/hpCurrent only for curated authored NPCs, companions, bosses, dragons, ancient guardians, or special exceptions. Political importance alone does not equal combat threat.

constitution.description: Constitution contributes half its score, rounded down, to starting Health as a one-time level 1 HP foundation. Constitution does not add again on level-up; level-up HP comes only from resourceSettings.health.gainPerLevel (+2).

attributeSettings.resourceScalingInstructions: Point-buy is unsupported: all characters start with fixed attributes of 10 before trait modifiers, with 0 allocation points. Race, Bloodline, Vocation, Inherent Skill, Crafting Profession, and Quirk traits provide starting stat differences. Player/companion level 1 HP uses half Constitution, rounded down, plus reinforced race starting HP (Human +10, Elf +8, Beastkin +10, Worcen +12, Drakken +12), bloodline starting HP, and eligible ancestral animal starting HP. Constitution does not add again during level-up. Later level-up HP uses only the native Health gainPerLevel value, currently +2 HP per level. Legacy hpPerLevel fields are neutralized at 0 and are not active. Mana is not granted by Intelligence or by the global resource; Mana starts at 0/0 globally, race grants internal body mana, bloodline modifies it, and caster traits may add more. Valenian humans have no personal mana unless explicitly excepted. Stamina starts at 0/0 unless granted by a stamina-based vocation or training trait. Bladesinger, Ravager, and Practitioner of The Ninefold Path grant +100 Stamina; Master of the Ninefold grants +150 and replaces Practitioner; each Ninefold Path trait grants +10.

attributeModifierRule.hpConstitution: Constitution contributes half its score, rounded down, to level 1 HP once; Constitution does not add to level-up HP.
attributeModifierRule.damageStrength: Strength modifier adds to melee/heavy thrown damage when relevant.
attributeModifierRule.damageDexterity: Dexterity modifier may add to ranged/finesse damage only when supported.
attributeModifierRule.manaIntelligence: Intelligence improves magic control/checks and rune/circle design but does not add to Mana capacity by itself.

dynamicHitCheckSystem.name: Dynamic Hit-Check Combat
dynamicHitCheckSystem.coreRule: Combat is not automatic damage exchange. Every attack must first resolve whether it connects. Damage is calculated only after a successful contact result.
requiredFlow: 1. Establish intent, target, range, weapon/ability/spell, and approach.
2. Choose attack vector: melee, ranged, spell strike, breath, thrown weapon, grapple, shove, maneuver, companion attack, or environmental attack.
3. Choose defense vector: dodge, block, parry, armor deflection, cover, ward, resistance, movement, reach, footing, shield, guard, flight, or terrain.
4. Resolve a hit check before damage. Compare attacker capability against defender capability and the situation.
5. Determine outcome: critical failure, miss, defended miss, glancing hit, clean hit, critical hit, or tactical success without damage.
6. Apply damage only if contact occurs. Misses deal no damage.
6A. Identify the single declared attack source for damage. Do not combine all equipped weapons unless this is an explicit two-weapon/twin/dual attack.
7. Narrate movement, reactions, positioning, wounds, openings, morale, and consequences.

criticalFailure.meaning: The attacker badly overextends, slips, exposes guard, jams a weapon, loses rhythm, wastes mana, or gives the target a counter-opening.
criticalFailure.narration: Make the failure active and tactical, not just 'misses.'

miss.meaning: The attack fails to connect because of movement, range, timing, poor angle, cover, darkness, target agility, flight, or bad footing.
miss.narration: Describe how the attack fails and how positioning changes.

defendedMiss.meaning: The target actively blocks, parries, ducks, twists away, uses armor, shield, guard, ward, or cover to stop contact.
defendedMiss.narration: Let defense feel skilled and physical.

glancingHit.damage: Use outcome impact scaling. Mixed Result uses impactModifier 0.20; Basic Success/glancing hit uses impactModifier 0.35 unless the scene is fully blocked/dodged/parried for 0 damage.
glancingHit.meaning: The attack clips, grazes, scrapes armor, cuts cloth, bruises, rattles a shield, or partially lands. Do not add bonus damage for the result; set impact low.
glancingHit.narration: Apply reduced damage or a small partial effect.

cleanHit.meaning: The attack lands solidly and applies normal damage or normal effect.
cleanHit.narration: Describe the hit clearly without overdoing every strike.

criticalHit.meaning: The attack exploits an opening, weak point, committed movement, surprise, exposed armor gap, or superior positioning.
criticalHit.narration: Apply increased damage and/or a secondary effect.

tacticalSuccess.meaning: The attack or maneuver succeeds by changing the field instead of just dealing damage: disarm, trip, shove, pin, blind, stagger, break guard, interrupt casting, force movement, expose weakness, separate enemies, or set up an ally.
tacticalSuccess.narration: Use when the player describes clever non-damage tactics.

defenseOptions.dodge: Avoid through speed, reflex, acrobatics, flight, footwork, or sudden movement.
defenseOptions.block: Stop force with shield, armor, weapon, guard, body mass, cover, or ward.
defenseOptions.parry: Redirect with timing, blade skill, polearm leverage, or martial training.
defenseOptions.armorDeflection: Attack connects poorly but armor prevents serious harm.
defenseOptions.cover: Terrain, walls, wagons, trees, furniture, rubble, doors, crowds, or allies obstruct the attack.
defenseOptions.ward: Magic shield, rune circle, barrier, divine protection, or anti-magic effect prevents or reduces contact.
defenseOptions.resistance: Creature biology, element, material, blessing, curse, or training reduces a successful hit.
defenseOptions.positioning: Reach, height, footing, angle, darkness, weather, tight spaces, elevation, mounted combat, or flight alters the check.

checkLogic.simpleResolution: Use the best relevant attacker stat/skill/ability against the best relevant defender stat/skill/defense. Attacker success means contact; defender success means miss, dodge, block, parry, or deflection.
marginResolution: Attacker fails: miss or defended miss.
Attacker succeeds by a wide margin: critical hit or major tactical success.
checkLogic.damageTiming: Do not roll, calculate, or narrate damage until the hit outcome is known.
checkLogic.noAutoHit: Never treat player attacks, enemy attacks, companion attacks, breath attacks, spells, gunshots, arrows, or monster strikes as automatic hits.
checkLogic.activeAttackWeaponDamage: Damage is calculated from the single declared attack source only. Do not combine all equipped weapons into baseDamage.
checkLogic.mixedResultDamage: Mixed Result is a low-impact graze/partial contact. Use impactModifier 0.20 on native baseDamage, or choose a minor non-damage partial effect. Do not use full baseDamage.

weaponAndStyleGuidance.swordsAndDaggers: Favor footwork, angle, guard, parry, feints, cuts, thrusts, binds, and openings.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.greatWeapons: Favor reach, commitment, guard breaks, momentum, overextension risk, and heavy impact.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.polearms: Favor reach control, bracing, hooking, tripping, keeping distance, and punishing charges.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.bowsAndThrownWeapons: Favor sightlines, cover, movement, wind, distance, target speed, and ammunition.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.firearms: Favor aim, smoke, reload pressure, cover, armor penetration, misfire risk if relevant, and line of fire.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.spells: Favor targeting, wards, counter-motion, concentration, range, line of sight, mana strain, and environmental interaction.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.breathWeapons: Favor warning inhales, cone/line positioning, cover, evasion, scale of breath, and aftermath.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.bladesinging: Favor rhythm, blade path, footwork, interruption, chosen element flow, and whether the rhythm connects before any damage.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.companions: Companion attacks require the same hit/defense logic; loyalty does not mean automatic success.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.twinDualWeapons: Twin, paired, dual, or double weapons represent attacking with two weapons as one fighting style. Their damage range must reflect both weapons together, such as twin longswords 2-16 instead of 1-8, or dual daggers 2-8 instead of 1-4.
weaponAndStyleGuidance.shields: Shields occupy the secondary slot. Favor blocks, braces, bodyguarding, cover, shield-wall positioning, and shield bashes that stun, stagger, interrupt, or knock targets off-balance. Standard Knight's Shield grants +1 physical Damage Resistance and Shield Bash Dmg Range: 1 to 2.

enemyBehaviorRequirements: Enemies must defend themselves when reasonable.
Enemies may miss players and companions.
Enemies should reposition, protect weak points, use terrain, flee, call help, brace, guard, or exploit openings.
Monsters should fight according to anatomy and instinct, not as static damage bags.
Intelligent enemies should learn from repeated tactics.
narratorRequirements: Do not say an attack hits unless the hit check succeeds.
Do not apply damage on a miss.
Do not make every exchange a wound exchange.
Do not narrate enemy attacks as guaranteed hits.
Misses should still change the fight through position, pressure, footing, morale, stamina, or openings.
Describe why an attack hit or failed: angle, timing, defense, terrain, speed, armor, cover, luck, or skill.
Use concise but vivid combat beats; avoid long repetitive combat paragraphs.
Give players meaningful openings after good rolls or clever tactics.
Let bad rolls create danger without always becoming direct damage.
Never combine all equipped weapons into one baseDamage pool.
Use only the weapon, spell, ability, natural weapon, or item explicitly declared for this attack.
Do not add offhand weapon damage unless the attack is explicitly a two-weapon/twin/dual attack.
Never add raw attributes, skill scores, attack roll totals, success margins, character level, or all equipped weapons to damage.
Damage outcomes set impact, not added damage: Mixed 0.20, Basic 0.35, Success 0.50, Great 0.75, Critical 1.00 before armor impact negation.
dynamicHitCheckSystem.playerFacingPrinciple: Combat should feel like a tabletop encounter: declare intent, test whether it works, narrate the result, then apply damage or consequences.

defenseLayerAddendum.rule: Resolve contact first, then miss chance if fiction supports it, then damage ladder, then DR/resistance mitigation.
defenseLayerAddendum.missChance: 20% partial concealment/blur/smoke/dim light; 50% total concealment/invisibility/incorporeal/phasing/displacement. Use highest only.
defenseLayerAddendum.mitigation: Physical DR subtracts from physical damage after a landed hit; typed Resistance subtracts matching typed damage only. Energy bypasses physical DR unless explicitly covered.

outcomeImpactScaling.name: Balen Outcome Impact Scaling
outcomeImpactScaling.purpose: Work with Voyage native baseDamage by treating baseDamage as raw impact pressure. Hit outcome sets impact instead of adding damage.
outcomeImpactScaling.coreRule: Do not add extra damage because a hit result is better. The result tier sets the impactModifier/effective impact applied to the native baseDamage package before HP loss.
outcomeImpactScaling.effectiveImpactRule: effectiveImpact = max(0.10, outcomeImpact - armorImpactNegation - shieldOrBraceImpact) on any damaging hit. If the attack is fully dodged, blocked, parried, stopped by cover, or stopped by armor, damage is 0 instead.
examples: baseDamage 19.5 with Basic Success uses 0.35 impact: about 6.8 before random/resistance/armor effects.
baseDamage 19.5 with Mixed Result uses 0.20 impact: about 3.9 before random/resistance/armor effects.
baseDamage 19.5 with Success against medium armor uses 0.50 - 0.10 = 0.40 impact: about 7.8 before other effects.
outcomeImpactScaling.diagnosticNote: This test restores combatSettings.abilityBonus to 10 and changes only how outcomes/armor should affect impact. It does not change HP, weapons, traits, items, NPCs, regions, or locations.

damageOutcomeLadder.mixedResult: Use impactModifier 0.20. This is a graze, nick, bruise, shield jolt, shallow cut, or minor partial contact; may instead be a non-damage complication.
damageOutcomeLadder.basicSuccess: Use impactModifier 0.35. This is a glancing but real hit.
damageOutcomeLadder.success: Use impactModifier 0.50. This is a clean hit.
damageOutcomeLadder.greatSuccess: Use impactModifier 0.75. This is a strong clean hit.
damageOutcomeLadder.criticalSuccess: Use impactModifier 1.00. This is a decisive hit; special attacks may exceed only if explicitly curated.

twinDualWeaponDamageRule.name: Twin / Dual Weapon Damage Range
twinDualWeaponDamageRule.rule: Weapons described as twin, paired, dual, double, or otherwise clearly representing two matching weapons use a combined damage range that reflects striking with both weapons. Double both the minimum and maximum base weapon damage before applying the damage outcome ladder. Example: one longsword at 1-8 becomes twin longswords at 2-16; one dagger at 1-4 becomes dual daggers at 2-8.
applicationOrder: 1. Identify the base damage range of one weapon.
2. If the item represents twin/dual/paired matching weapons, double the minimum and maximum base damage.
3. Use that combined range as the weapon damage range for the damage outcome ladder.
4. Apply attribute, material, magic, or technique modifiers after the outcome damage is determined unless a specific rule says otherwise.
twinDualWeaponDamageRule.antiStackingClarification: This rule does not mean all equipped weapons stack. It applies only when an item/style is explicitly twin, paired, dual, double, or when the player declares a two-weapon attack that uses both weapons. A steel blade in one hand and a dagger in the other do not combine unless the attack declares both weapons.

combatDamageHardRules.name: Combat Damage Hard Rules - Active Weapon and Outcome Caps
combatDamageHardRules.purpose: Prevents Voyage from combining all equipped weapons, adding raw stats, or treating mixed results as percent damage.
combatDamageHardRules.activeAttackWeaponRule: For each attack, calculate damage from only the weapon, natural weapon, spell, ability, or item explicitly used for that attack. Equipped weapons are not automatically combined. A character may hold or equip multiple weapons, but only the declared attack source contributes damage.
combatDamageHardRules.equippedWeaponBan: Do not add damage from every equipped, carried, wielded, offhand, secondary, ranged, sheathed, backup, or narrative weapon. Inventory and equipment slots provide options, not automatic combined baseDamage.
combatDamageHardRules.offhandRule: Offhand or secondary weapons add no damage unless the player explicitly declares a two-weapon attack, the item is a defined twin/dual/paired weapon, or a specific trait, ability, combat style, or item says the offhand weapon contributes damage. Otherwise, offhand weapons may provide parrying, threat, utility, follow-up opportunities, or narrative options only.
combatDamageHardRules.twinDualException: A twin/dual/paired item or declared two-weapon attack may use a combined damage range only when the attack itself uses both weapons. Two separate equipped weapons are not automatically a twin/dual attack.
combatDamageHardRules.mixedResultOverride: A Mixed Result is not full native damage. It is low-impact partial contact: use impactModifier 0.20, or narrate a minor non-damage complication if contact is too weak to wound.
combatDamageHardRules.outcomeBandOverride: Determine the hit outcome first, then select damage from the declared attack source using the damageOutcomeLadder. Do not calculate a large baseDamage pool first and then multiply, halve, or reduce it by success quality.
combatDamageHardRules.legalDamageModifiers: After the outcome band selects weapon damage, add only one relevant attribute modifier using floor(attribute / 5), explicit weapon quality bonus, explicit enchantment bonus, explicit material bonus, or explicit trait/ability damage bonus.
forbiddenDamageModifiers: offhand weapon damage unless explicitly part of this attack
workedExamples: Dagger/steel blade damage is part of the native baseDamage package. Do not add extra damage for success tier; success tier sets impact.
If baseDamage is 19.5 and the outcome is Basic Success, use 19.5 x 0.35 = about 7 before random/resistance/armor effects.
If baseDamage is 19.5 and the outcome is Mixed Result, use 19.5 x 0.20 = about 4 before random/resistance/armor effects.
combatDamageHardRules.outcomeImpactRule: Hit results set impact rather than adding damage: Mixed 0.20, Basic 0.35, Success 0.50, Great 0.75, Critical 1.00. Native baseDamage is raw impact pressure and must be compressed by outcome impact before HP loss.

outcomeImpactScaling.name: Balen Outcome Impact Scaling
outcomeImpactScaling.purpose: Work with Voyage native baseDamage by treating baseDamage as raw impact pressure. Hit outcome sets impact instead of adding damage.
outcomeImpactScaling.coreRule: Do not add extra damage because a hit result is better. The result tier sets the impactModifier/effective impact applied to the native baseDamage package before HP loss.
outcomeImpactScaling.effectiveImpactRule: effectiveImpact = max(0.10, outcomeImpact - armorImpactNegation - shieldOrBraceImpact) on any damaging hit. If the attack is fully dodged, blocked, parried, stopped by cover, or stopped by armor, damage is 0 instead.
examples: baseDamage 19.5 with Basic Success uses 0.35 impact: about 6.8 before random/resistance/armor effects.
baseDamage 19.5 with Mixed Result uses 0.20 impact: about 3.9 before random/resistance/armor effects.
baseDamage 19.5 with Success against medium armor uses 0.50 - 0.10 = 0.40 impact: about 7.8 before other effects.
outcomeImpactScaling.diagnosticNote: This test restores combatSettings.abilityBonus to 10 and changes only how outcomes/armor should affect impact. It does not change HP, weapons, traits, items, NPCs, regions, or locations.

activePartyCombatParticipation.rule: All active allies, companions, and party NPCs in the scene participate in combat unless fiction says otherwise. The narrator should resolve their actions separately from the player's action and consider them valid enemy targets.
activePartyCombatParticipation.allyDamageAttribution: Ally attacks and effects must be narrated as that ally's contribution, not as player damage. If exact mechanical logging cannot separate it, track it narratively using the ally's tier, level, combat style, weapon, abilities, and current position.
activePartyCombatParticipation.enemyTargeting: Enemies may attack or respond to allies based on threat, proximity, healing/control output, exposed position, intelligence, morale, and tactics. Do not have enemies ignore allies by default.
activePartyCombatParticipation.playerAgencyGuard: Allies may help, create openings, defend, wound, restrain, or finish lesser foes when justified, but should not steal central player decisions or automatically solve boss encounters.

balenD20LowHPMode.name: Balen V35 Low-HP D20 Combat Mode
balenD20LowHPMode.playerHPRule: Player and player-style companion level 1 HP comes from half Constitution + race starting HP + bloodline starting HP + eligible ancestral animal HP. Later HP growth uses resourceSettings.health.gainPerLevel = 2.
balenD20LowHPMode.npcHPRule: Generated NPC/monster fallback uses otherSettings.npcMinHealth = 6 and otherSettings.npcHealthPerLevel = 2, modified by native tier scaling. Use explicit hpMax/hpCurrent for curated bosses, dragons, companions, and special authored combatants.
balenD20LowHPMode.combatRule: Resolve hit/contact first, then apply damage only on glancing hit, clean hit, critical hit, or tactical success with damage. Misses, dodges, blocks, parries, cover stops, full wards, and full armor stops deal 0 HP damage.
balenD20LowHPMode.damageRule: Use only the declared attack source and its listed damageRange/DMG Range. Do not combine all equipped weapons, raw attributes, attack totals, level, skill scores, or offhand weapons unless a specific dual/twin rule applies.

travelInterpretation.roadwayLayer: All maintained ground routes in a region.
travelInterpretation.airwayLayer: Usable open sky around a region; represented by [Region] Skies locations, not docks.

tips: There are no rules about what you type. Describe what you want to do in your own words - be as creative or detailed as you want.
Have a question about the world or the game? Use the 'Ask the Narrator' command!
Can't remember the name of a character or place? Try writing '@' followed by first few letters.
Stuck with no idea what to do next? Use the 'Ask the Narrator' command to ask for suggestions.
Worried about convincing someone? Try taking the conversation one line at a time.
Be ambitious and creative: Heroes rewards those who dream big!
How you phrase things matters: the game rewards you for stylish, clever actions.
Coordinate with party members in combat for powerful combined effects.
The world is your oyster: find and claim a place to call home.
Family is worth fighting for. Having a family in the game can lead to great stories.
You can attack multiple times per turn, and so can your allies.
Tell people your hopes and goals: you never know who may share your dreams.
Death is not always the end…
If there's something you want to do, go out and give it a try!
Try describing your actions in detail. They may not always work out the way you expect, but it will make things more interesting!
If you can't beat 'em, persuade 'em. Try convincing an enemy to come over to your side - or go over to theirs!
You can solve a problem any way you can think of: fight, bribe, trick, steal, befriend. The choice is yours.
You may spend a long time with your party members. Choose them wisely!
You can try to dodge, block and attack all in the same turn.
You can use items and take other actions in the same turn.
Skills are most likely to succeed when you use them as intended: in their natural domain.
Don't forget to stock up on items. Shops often have very powerful equipment for sale.
You can level up by taking almost any action, so feel free to play the game whatever way you want.
Don't forget to check in on your party members! Ask them how they're doing - and tell them how you feel!
Sometimes you can insta-kill enemies if you get creative with your inputs.
Party members can learn new skills and abilities as you progress through the story together.
Environmental conditions can add modifiers to your skill checks. Use the world to your advantage!
Tired of action and fighting? Look for a tavern or other gathering spot to socialize and start new relationships.
To prepare for a fight, try giving your party members orders to carry out in combat.
Social skills are very powerful. Persuasion can save a lot of lives.
Don't think you can win a fight? Try making a deal instead.
You don't have to do it all yourself, you know: hire people to do jobs for you!
Tired of traveling? Try learning how to fly or teleport or look for other forms of travel in the world.
There's nothing you can't do if you put your mind to it, from starting a revolution to founding your own country!
Turn order is determined by level and a random initiative value. Higher level characters tend to act first.
Characters without targets (like those that are buffing or healing) act before characters who are targeting enemies.
Multiple defensive effects stack multiplicatively. Layer your defenses!
Damage reduction scales with level difference, but has diminishing returns.
Different enemies can have different resistances, vulnerabilities, immunities or weaknesses, which will greatly affect the damage they take.
Try different damage types to discover enemies' weaknesses!
Healing actions use the same success level system as attacks - critical successes will heal for more.
Your equipment's damage bonus increases overall damage by 10% per point.
All equippable clothing and armor adds damage reduction based on the level of the item and its weight, with heavy items providing more damage reduction.
Want a quest? Try asking an NPC about their problems!
Most places and characters you meet have secrets to uncover: get exploring!
Your Journal contains a wealth of information about the world's people, places and ideas.
Want to learn more about the world? Try reading books or watching television or other media!
Looking for cool gear? Don't forget to loot dead enemies and search areas where powerful items might be found!
Need some help fighting? Recruit NPCs to join your party! Anyone you meet can become an ally—if you can convince them to take your side.
Can't find the skill you want? Try asking NPCs about any new skill you can imagine. They just might be able to teach it to you.
You can unlock custom abilities when your character does new things in the game.
Need ideas about how to start your story off? Try feeding some basic concepts to the Background Generator and editing what you get back.
Don't forget to explore. Open up the map and try going to a new location.
Abilities give you bonuses to success and allow you to perform the actions they describe without ever needing any items.
The memory system remembers. Reference past events, people you've met, and places you've been - even from many turns ago!
Locations, NPCs, and quests generate dynamically based on your actions. Every playthrough creates unique content!
Don't quit after failure! Mistakes, setbacks and even death can lead to memorable stories.
Every action teaches you something. You gain XP from attempting things, not just succeeding at them.
If you don't know what to do, press the submit button to continue without an action.

health.name: health
health.usageInstructions:
### Health / HP Rules
Use Health as exact HP, not a percent meter. Damage is applied only after D20 combat resolves contact. Misses, dodges, clean blocks, parries, cover stops, and full armor/ward stops deal no HP damage unless a specific rule says reduced damage applies.

### Balen V35 Low-HP Player and Companion Model
Point-buy/allocation is unsupported. Player attributes start at fixed 10 before trait modifiers.

Level 1 player/companion HP = floor(Constitution score / 2) + race starting HP + bloodline starting HP + eligible ancestral animal starting HP.
Constitution is a one-time starting HP foundation and does not add again on level-up.

Each level after 1 uses the native Health gainPerLevel field only. Current active level-up rule: +2 max HP per level. Do not manually add any older +5 per-level rule on top of this. Legacy hpPerLevel fields are neutralized at 0 and are not active.

Only Beastkin and Worcen can use ancestral animal starting HP unless the user explicitly creates a rare hybrid exception.

### Import/UI Scaling
Health base is 0. Constitution adds half its score to starting Health through attributeSettings.attributeStatModifiers.constitution, rounded down for whole HP. Race, bloodline, and eligible ancestral animal traits add their fixed starting health resource modifiers. Later HP growth is handled only by resourceSettings.health.gainPerLevel.

### NPC HP Scaling
NPCs and monsters use Voyage-native level and tier scaling. Active low-HP fallback values: otherSettings.npcMinHealth = 6 and otherSettings.npcHealthPerLevel = 2. Tier still modifies HP, damage output, and intent complexity. Use explicit hpMax/hpCurrent only for curated authored NPCs, companions, bosses, dragons, ancient guardians, or special exceptions.

### Resource Updates
Healing must match fiction: treatment, bandages, elixirs, magic, rest, recovery, or intervention. Never display fractional HP.

### Race Starting HP Reinforcement
Playable race starting HP uses the low-HP compromise scale: Human +10, Elf +8, Beastkin +10, Worcen +12, Drakken +12. Bloodline HP, eligible ancestral animal HP, and Health gainPerLevel remain separate.

mana.name: mana
mana.usageInstructions:
### Race-Based Mana Rules
Mana represents the internal mana held within the body. Standard spellcasting spends this bodily mana directly. Mana is not a universal 100-point bar. Global Mana starts at 0/0; race grants biological base mana, bloodline modifies that pool, and caster traits may add more.

Race base mana:
Human +100, Beastkin +110, Elf +125, Worcen +125, Drakken +100.

Bloodline mana modifiers:
Valenian Bloodline -100, Draconic Bloodline +10, Sylvera Bloodline +50, Drakken Bloodline +0, Crownsworn Bloodline +0, Beastkin Bloodline +0.

Examples:
Human + Valenian = 0/0 Mana.
Beastkin + Valenian = 10 Mana.
Worcen + Valenian = 25 Mana.
Human + Draconic = 110 Mana.
Elf + Sylvera = 175 Mana.
Drakken + Draconic = 110 Mana, plus Breathing from Dracomancy traits.

Intelligence improves spell control, rune/circle design, magical analysis, and magic-related checks. Intelligence does not create mana by itself.

Normal spells cost mana when actual power is cast, channeled, charged, activated, triggered, or sustained. Thinking about magic, recognizing magic, discussing magic, or holding an enchanted item does not automatically cost mana.

Valenian humans have no personal mana and cannot cast normal personal-mana spells unless explicitly excepted. They may use tools, alchemy, blackpowder, externally powered magic items, pre-charged runes, and Sublime Arts.

Dracomancy uses Breathing, not mana. Bladesinging, Bladesinger Rhythm abilities, Sublime Arts, and Ninefold Path techniques normally use stamina, not mana, unless a specific ability says otherwise.

At 0 mana, capable normal casters may overdraw if the scene allows it: after consuming the full internal pool, each missing mana point costs 2 HP and risks Mana Burn, blood strain, organ stress, collapse, or lasting injury. Characters with no personal mana, such as Valenian humans, cannot overdraw what they do not have.

### Mana Level-Up Rule
Race grants the starting base mana pool. Bloodline applies a one-time starting mana modifier and determines mana gained per level. Bloodline mana per level: Beastkin +12, Drakken +12, Crownsworn +12, Draconic +14, Sylvera +22, Valenian +0. Valenian bloodline removes or reduces starting mana and gains no mana per level unless a specific exception says otherwise.

stamina.name: stamina
stamina.usageInstructions:
### Trait-Based Stamina / Sublime Arts Rules
Stamina represents trained vessel capacity: the body's conditioned ability to push beyond ordinary mortal limits without consuming internal mana. Imagine mana in the body as liquid and the body as the vessel. Sublime conditioning stresses and strengthens the vessel so power can be expressed through effort, rhythm, stance, breath, pain tolerance, and motion without draining the mana pool itself.

Global Stamina starts at 0/0. Ordinary people do not begin with Stamina. A character only gains Stamina from a vocation, trait, or training path that explicitly grants access to Sublime Arts, Bladesinging, Ninefold Path techniques, Ravager techniques, or another stamina-based discipline.

Stamina sources:
Bladesinger traits grant +100 max Stamina.
Ravager grants +100 max Stamina.
Practitioner of The Ninefold Path grants +100 max Stamina.
Master of the Ninefold grants +150 max Stamina and replaces Practitioner of The Ninefold Path when earned.
Each Ninefold Path trait grants +10 max Stamina.

Stamina fuels Bladesinging, Bladesinger Rhythm abilities, Sublime Arts, Ninefold Path techniques, Ravager techniques, and explicit named martial-mystic/combat techniques. Do not spend stamina for ordinary walking, normal travel, casual labor, normal attacks, normal defense, mundane grapples, or basic physical effort unless a specific ability, combat maneuver, or scene rule explicitly calls for stamina.

Technique cost: each Bladesinging use or Sublime Art/Ninefold technique costs 10 stamina flat unless a specific ability states otherwise. Multiple techniques stack in the same turn.

Base recovery: recover 4–5 stamina at the end of each combat turn if the character can reset breath, stance, rhythm, or focus. If a character spends the turn defending and actively recovering through breath, mantra, weapon flourish, stance correction, grounding, meditation, step pattern, or focused silence, recover double or triple base regeneration at narrator discretion.

At 0 stamina, the character is not helpless, but their vessel is strained: slower reactions, weaker guard, bad footing, loss of rhythm, pain tremors, and difficulty using demanding techniques. If a character tries to force Sublime Arts past 0 stamina, it risks shattering the vessel and spilling mana: severe self-injury, HP loss, mana-burn-like trauma, collapse, unconsciousness, or death in extreme cases. Do not allow free overuse without consequence.

Valenian humans can use stamina-based Sublime Arts if they have the proper traits/training. Their lack of mana does not block stamina-based techniques.

breathing.name: breathing
breathing.usageInstructions:
### Breathing / Dracomancy Charges
Breathing is the Dracomancy resource. It represents draconic lung capacity, breath pressure, throat control, and the physical rhythm needed to inhale external mana and release it as breath-casting.

Dracomancy burns the mana around the caster rather than consuming the caster's internal mana pool. Every deep breath draws in a large intake of external mana through draconic lungs, blood, throat, scales, and will. The exhale releases and consumes that gathered mana as the spell.

Breathing starts at 0/0 by default. Only traits that grant Breathing increase the pool. Non-Dracomancers should remain at 0/0 Breathing and should not use it in narration.

Breathing sources: Drakken +2 max Breathing; Drakken Bloodline +1 max Breathing; Draconic Bloodline +2 max Breathing; Natural Dracomancer +1 max Breathing. These add together. Examples: Drakken + Drakken = 3 Breathing; Drakken + Draconic = 4 Breathing; Draconic Bloodline + Natural Dracomancer = 3 Breathing; Drakken + Draconic + Natural Dracomancer = 5 Breathing.

Dracomancy costs Breathing, not mana. Most Dracomancy actions cost 1 Breathing unless a specific ability says otherwise. Large, sustained, or overwhelming breath effects may cost 2 or more Breathing.

Breathing recovers 1 point at the end of each combat turn when the character can breathe. Breath recovery may be blocked or reduced by choking, suffocation, throat/lung injury, poison, thin air, underwater conditions, silence/ward effects, or interruption.

At 0 Breathing, Dracomancy is unavailable until at least 1 Breathing is recovered. If the caster tries to force breath-casting beyond normal capacity, the consequence is respiratory strain: hyperventilation, shortness of breath, coughing, dizziness, throat/lung pain, failed breath control, collapse, or injury. The character may still use ordinary mana magic if they are capable of normal spellcasting.

Valenian humans do not gain Breathing unless the user/story explicitly creates a rare exception. If Natural Dracomancer appears on an invalid build, remove its +1 Breathing and select a replacement Inherent Skill.

storySettings.worldBackground: Balen is a high-fantasy world in the midst of an industrial revolution. Four nations surround the neutral city of Aethelgard: Drakona (northern dragon nation of sky-cities and aerial might), the Republic of Valen (western industrial power using gunpowder and clockwork), the Holy Kingdom (southern agrarian realm protected by the Crystal Crown), and Sylvera (eastern elven jungle nation). The world was nearly destroyed 800 years ago by the Shaydes — shadow beings embodying sin and aggression — until a pulse of light magic from the Crystal Crown eradicated them. The Crown must be maintained monthly by the Holy King. The World’s Maw, a massive antimagic mountain ridge, encircles the world beyond the Great Blue Empty. The ancient Abyssal Tower descends deep into the earth, blocking teleportation between floors. Magic is imagination-based and drawn from blood mana (causing fatigue), except for Dracomancy used by dragons and Drakken, which replenishes rapidly through breathing. The four nations maintain tense but stable relations, with the magi-knights acting as neutral mediators.
storySettings.questGenerationGuidance:
Generate quests as bounded, actionable Balen objectives tied to existing factions, locations, threats, relationships, contracts, or evidence. Prefer eliminate, rescue, retrieve, investigate, deliver, negotiate, defend, cleanse, identify, escort only when appropriate, or answer a specific question. Do not trap the player in unbounded obligations or force quests after refusal.

Use established lore. Do not invent major factions, gods, currencies, creature origins, hidden systems, or new magic subsystems. Modern Balen believes Shaydes are extinct; ordinary boards should not ask for Shayde signs. Daemons are secret: public work should describe blight, curses, wraiths, disappearances, or monster attacks unless the party has earned the truth. True dragons are citizens; legal contracts target confirmed false-wyrms, monsters, criminals, hazards, or special lawful threats.

Quest rewards use universal Marks only: Copper Marks = 1 value, Silver Marks = 10, Gold Marks = 100. Convert to the fewest clear coins. Scale payouts by danger, travel, secrecy, legality, patron wealth, proof required, civilian risk, harvest rights, and whether targets are alive or dead. Do not invent national currencies. Do not charge the same fee, deposit, toll, room, bribe, permit, or contract expense twice because it is mentioned again.

Use travelSystem, petCompanionSystem, contractBoardSystem, economySystem, bestiarySystem, and narrativeIntegritySystem worldLore when relevant. Do not auto-skip travel unless the player asks. If summarized, state elapsed time and stop before resolving meaningful encounters. If a normal bestiary is granted, default to Balen Slayer's Bestiary; Daemons, Shaydes, and Abyssal Tower entities are not normal bestiary entries.

Quest hooks should reflect competing interests rather than simple national evil. Preserve clues, documents, NPC statements, monster identities, locations, and prior events. Treat player contradictions as in-character beliefs, lies, jokes, mistakes, or theories unless clearly framed as out-of-character retcons.

acrobatics.name: acrobatics
acrobatics.description: Improves the player's agility and balance, enabling them to perform acrobatic maneuvers.

athletics.name: athletics
athletics.description: Improves overall physical performance, enhancing running, jumping, climbing, and other physical feats.

intimidation.name: intimidation
intimidation.description: Allows the player to use fear as a tool, potentially allowing them to scare off enemies or influence confrontations.

investigation.name: investigation
investigation.description: Enables careful examination and analysis of surroundings, helping the player uncover clues and secrets.

performance.name: performance
performance.description: Enables the player to entertain others through acts such as dancing, singing, or acting.

persuasion.name: persuasion
persuasion.description: Enhances the player's ability to influence others through dialogue.

survival.name: survival
survival.description: Improves the player's ability to endure harsh environments, find food and water, and build shelters.

archery.name: archery
archery.description: The art of using bows for precision ranged combat. It allows for consistent accuracy when firing at distant targets.

gunpowder handling.name: gunpowder handling
gunpowder handling.description: Mastery over the volatile explosive powder used by the Republic of Valen. It enables the safe loading and maintenance of firearms.

draconic lore.name: draconic lore
draconic lore.description: Deep knowledge regarding the biology and history of dragons. This skill aids in communicating with or surviving encounters with drakes.

bladed arts.name: bladed arts
bladed arts.description: Refined technique in the use of swords and daggers. Practitioners move with a fluid lethality favored by the Sylveran Guard.

shadow stealth.name: shadow stealth
shadow stealth.description: Supernatural stealth using dark mana, shadow powers, Shadow Hand maneuvers, or actual shadow-melding effects. This is not ordinary mundane stealth.

draconic combat.name: draconic combat
draconic combat.description: Techniques for fighting alongside dragons, riding dragons, surviving aerial combat, and combating dragon-scale threats. At higher levels, this skill can unlock Dragoon Restraint and Dragonbreaker Training, allowing trained fighters to subdue or wound dragons through anatomy, timing, and scale-seam targeting rather than brute force.

musketry.name: musketry
musketry.description: The specialized application of Republic gunpowder technology in warfare. It enables precision firing with repeating rifles.

glaive mastery.name: glaive mastery
glaive mastery.description: Advanced training in the polearms favored by the Crown Glaives. Practitioners excel at reach and battlefield control.

nature communion.name: nature communion
nature communion.description: The ability to harmonize with the dense jungles of Sylvera. This skill aids in navigation and survival within wild, magical forests.

shadow shifting.name: shadow shifting
shadow shifting.description: The unique Shayde ability to manipulate their form through shadows. It grants enhanced mobility and concealment.

Blacksmithing.name: Blacksmithing
Blacksmithing.description: Your ability to forge weapons, armor, tools, fittings, and art objects from raw materials. Blacksmithing governs metalwork, alloys, heat treatment, edge geometry, armor fitting, and integration of monster parts or mana-reactive materials. At higher levels it can reveal and preserve material bonuses through the craftingSystem.

levels.1-4: Basic metals and repairs. Can make crude or standard iron goods.
levels.5-9: Reliable steelwork. Can create fine weapons, armor, tools, and simple alloys.
levels.10-14: Advanced materials. Can appraise material bonuses and work precious metals, monster parts, and mana-reactive fittings.
levels.15+: Masterwork/exotic materials. Can create named masterworks, preserve rare material bonuses, and suppress drawbacks.
levels.20+: Legendary craft. Can combine multiple rare materials, create unique properties, and safely attempt relic-grade work.

Sublime Arts.name: Sublime Arts
Sublime Arts.description: The rare hybrid discipline of channeling mana directly through melee weapons and unarmed strikes. Used by Bladesingers and Ninefold Path practitioners.

Crafting.name: Crafting
Crafting.description: General hands-on crafting, assembly, fitting, measuring, blueprint use, leatherwork, woodworking, jewelry, field repair, and practical construction. Crafting can support Blacksmithing and helps stabilize secondary materials or nonmetal components in the craftingSystem.

Tamer.name: Tamer
Tamer.description: Your ability to calm, handle, feed, trap humanely, bond with, train, and command tameable creatures. Used for initial taming/control, raising rescued young, calming panicked animals, teaching commands, reading temperament, setting humane snares, and managing dangerous companions. For initial taming only, use the petCompanionSystem relationship modifier.

Runic Magic.name: Runic Magic
Runic Magic.description: The study and practical use of runes, sigils, inscription logic, and magic circles. Runic Magic governs reading, drawing, carving, tattooing, charging, activating, disrupting, and modifying written spell structures. It is used for prepared magic, enchantments, wards, traps, combat inscriptions, rune-etched weapons, circle-based spellwork, body-runes, and identifying unstable or hostile rune patterns.

stealth.name: stealth
stealth.description: The mundane ability to move quietly, hide, blend into terrain, avoid sightlines, use cover, control noise, and remain unnoticed without relying on supernatural shadow powers.

spellcasting.name: spellcasting
spellcasting.description: General practical spell use: shaping mana through will, imagination, control, attunement, gesture, incantation, focus tools, and trained casting discipline. Used for ordinary spellcasting, Magi-Knight spellcraft, and broad magical execution that is not specifically rune/circle work.

arcana.name: arcana
arcana.description: Theoretical and investigative magical knowledge: identifying spells, magical structures, mana behavior, enchantments, supernatural phenomena, arcane history, magical doctrine, and hidden principles such as the difference between public elemental theory and deeper magical truth.

mana control.name: mana control
mana control.description: The bodily and mental discipline of channeling, regulating, sustaining, and surviving mana flow. Used for resisting mana strain, stabilizing difficult spell-shapes, managing overdraw risk, controlling charged blood mana, and maintaining complex magic under pressure.

Elven Precision.name: Elven Precision
Elven Precision.description: Focuses your senses to track targets through dense foliage or over long distances. This ability significantly increases your accuracy with ranged weapons.

Shadow Melding.name: Shadow Melding
Shadow Melding.description: Allows the user to vanish into light shadows even while being watched. It provides a massive temporary boost to stealth maneuvers. Costs 0 mana and no tracked Stamina unless the character also has a Stamina pool and deliberately overstrains.

Iron Resolve.name: Iron Resolve
Iron Resolve.description: Utilizes your physical conditioning to ignore pain and fight through debilitating injuries. This grants a temporary defensive bonus against incoming strikes. Costs 0 mana and no tracked Stamina unless the character also has a Stamina pool and deliberately overstrains.

Silver-Tongued Retort.name: Silver-Tongued Retort
Silver-Tongued Retort.description: Turns a failing negotiation around with a perfectly timed, witty remark. It overwhelms opponents, forcing them to reconsider their position during heated arguments.

Dragon's Roar.name: Dragon's Roar
Dragon's Roar.description: Spend draconic breath to unleash a terrifying roar that disrupts enemy formations and reduces their combat effectiveness. Costs 1 Breathing and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Ballistic Maintenance.name: Ballistic Maintenance
Ballistic Maintenance.description: Rapidly clears jams and optimizes firing mechanisms on black powder weaponry during the heat of combat. This ensures your rifle fire remains consistent and deadly.

Draconic Flight.name: Draconic Flight
Draconic Flight.description: Unlocks the latent draconic wings of your lineage to gain temporary aerial mobility. This allows you to bypass difficult terrain and strike from above.

Shadow Step.name: Shadow Step
Shadow Step.description: Instantly phase through darkness to reappear behind an unsuspecting foe. It grants a significant mobility advantage and initiates combat with a surprise attack. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Precision Shot.name: Precision Shot
Precision Shot.description: Focuses your aim to strike multiple targets in a single volley. This skill turns your ranged weapon into a crowd-control tool by hitting secondary foes with ricochets.

Calculated Volley.name: Calculated Volley
Calculated Volley.description: Uses advanced ballistic calculations to punch through heavy armor with musket fire. This ability effectively neutralizes armored opponents who would otherwise be resistant to your shots.

Masterful Reach.name: Masterful Reach
Masterful Reach.description: Utilizes the length of your polearm to parry incoming attacks before they land. It provides a defensive barrier that keeps multiple enemies at bay while maintaining your positioning.

Inspiring Performance.name: Inspiring Performance
Inspiring Performance.description: Uses captivating oratory or music to bolster the morale of your allies during the heat of battle. It restores focus and increases the combat effectiveness of nearby companions.

Moment of Perfect Clarity.name: Moment of Perfect Clarity
Moment of Perfect Clarity.description: Once per combat, automatically succeed on one mental save, skill check, or concentration roll. Grants +10 to the next action taken this turn.

Shadow Jaunt.name: Shadow Jaunt
Shadow Jaunt.description: Teleport up to 100 ft through shadows or darkness you can see. You appear behind a target or in a hidden position. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Divine Surge.name: Divine Surge
Divine Surge.description: As a bonus action, heal yourself or one ally for a significant amount and remove one negative condition.

Stone Bones.name: Stone Bones
Stone Bones.description: Gain massive temporary damage reduction and resistance to physical damage for 2 turns. You cannot be knocked down or moved. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Iron Heart Surge.name: Iron Heart Surge
Iron Heart Surge.description: For one full turn, ignore all negative conditions, pain, fear, or wounds. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Desert Wind Blast.name: Desert Wind Blast
Desert Wind Blast.description: Unleash a fiery whirlwind through your weapon in a short cone or line, dealing fire damage and knocking enemies back. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Tiger's Fury.name: Tiger's Fury
Tiger's Fury.description: Make two extra attacks this turn. Each successful hit applies a bleeding effect. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Setting Sun Counter.name: Setting Sun Counter
Setting Sun Counter.description: When an enemy attacks you or an ally within reach, immediately counter and redirect part of the attack back at the attacker. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

White Raven Tactics.name: White Raven Tactics
White Raven Tactics.description: Grant one ally an immediate extra action (they can move and act again this turn). Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Scale-Piercing Strike.name: Scale-Piercing Strike
Scale-Piercing Strike.description: A trained anti-dragon strike aimed at wing joints, scale seams, throat plates, eyes, claws, or other anatomical weak points. Against true dragons, false-wyrms, and dragon-scale creatures, this attack partially bypasses natural armor and deals significantly increased damage. It does not make dragons easy to kill; it gives a trained fighter a credible way to wound something far stronger than them.

Material Appraisal.name: Material Appraisal
Material Appraisal.description: Study a material's weight, grain, mana response, fracture, smell under heat, harvest marks, and origin to identify its likely material bonus, drawback, and best crafting use before committing it to a project.

Masterwork Integration.name: Masterwork Integration
Masterwork Integration.description: During crafting, integrate one primary material so its bonus transfers cleanly into the finished item. On strong success, suppress one minor drawback. On critical success, turn one drawback into a controlled feature.

Gentle Hand.name: Gentle Hand
Gentle Hand.description: Calm a frightened, defensive, wounded, or recently trapped tameable creature through posture, voice, food, scent control, timing, and nonthreatening approach.
Gentle Hand.usageInstructions: Use for first contact, calming, approach, de-escalation, and preventing a tameable creature from panicking or attacking. This is trained handling, not mind control.

Command Voice.name: Command Voice
Command Voice.description: Give a clear trained command to an obedient, companion, or loyal creature during stress, danger, or confusion.
Command Voice.usageInstructions: Use with creatures that have already been trained, calmed, bonded, purchased as trained animals, or reached a relationship tier where commands are plausible. It does not force obedience from strangers or sentient beings.

Bonded Recall.name: Bonded Recall
Bonded Recall.description: Use a Companion Call Item with practiced timing. Improves the chance that a Companion or Loyal creature answers when nearby and physically able.

Bladesingers Rhythm (Pyro).name: Bladesingers Rhythm (Pyro)
Bladesingers Rhythm (Pyro).description: Enter the Fire Bladesinger rhythm. The Bladesinger guides ambient airborne fire mana-flow through blade path, footwork, breath, and timing rather than casting from internal mana. The rhythm is freeform: it lets the Bladesinger coax nearby mana into movement like a pied piper for mana, shaping attacks and maneuvers through the dance instead of using a fixed spell list. Fire Bladesinging through this rhythm costs 0 mana, has 0 cooldown, and has 10 power. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.
Bladesingers Rhythm (Pyro).usageInstructions: Use as the core engine for freeform Fire Bladesinging. This is not ordinary spellcasting and does not spend mana. Resolve attacks through hit checks before applying damage.

Bladesingers Rhythm (Aqua).name: Bladesingers Rhythm (Aqua)
Bladesingers Rhythm (Aqua).description: Enter the Water Bladesinger rhythm. The Bladesinger guides ambient airborne water mana-flow through blade path, footwork, breath, and timing rather than casting from internal mana. The rhythm is freeform: it lets the Bladesinger coax nearby mana into movement like a pied piper for mana, shaping attacks and maneuvers through the dance instead of using a fixed spell list. Water Bladesinging through this rhythm costs 0 mana, has 0 cooldown, and has 10 power. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.
Bladesingers Rhythm (Aqua).usageInstructions: Use as the core engine for freeform Water Bladesinging. This is not ordinary spellcasting and does not spend mana. Resolve attacks through hit checks before applying damage.

Bladesingers Rhythm (Ful).name: Bladesingers Rhythm (Ful)
Bladesingers Rhythm (Ful).description: Enter the Lightning Bladesinger rhythm. The Bladesinger guides ambient airborne lightning mana-flow through blade path, footwork, breath, and timing rather than casting from internal mana. The rhythm is freeform: it lets the Bladesinger coax nearby mana into movement like a pied piper for mana, shaping attacks and maneuvers through the dance instead of using a fixed spell list. Lightning Bladesinging through this rhythm costs 0 mana, has 0 cooldown, and has 10 power. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.
Bladesingers Rhythm (Ful).usageInstructions: Use as the core engine for freeform Lightning Bladesinging. This is not ordinary spellcasting and does not spend mana. Resolve attacks through hit checks before applying damage.

Bladesingers Rhythm (Aero).name: Bladesingers Rhythm (Aero)
Bladesingers Rhythm (Aero).description: Enter the Wind Bladesinger rhythm. The Bladesinger guides ambient airborne wind mana-flow through blade path, footwork, breath, and timing rather than casting from internal mana. The rhythm is freeform: it lets the Bladesinger coax nearby mana into movement like a pied piper for mana, shaping attacks and maneuvers through the dance instead of using a fixed spell list. Wind Bladesinging through this rhythm costs 0 mana, has 0 cooldown, and has 10 power. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.
Bladesingers Rhythm (Aero).usageInstructions: Use as the core engine for freeform Wind Bladesinging. This is not ordinary spellcasting and does not spend mana. Resolve attacks through hit checks before applying damage.

Diamond Defense.name: Diamond Defense
Diamond Defense.description: Diamond Mind technique. When activated before or during a roll, Silas centers his mind into perfect stillness and reinforces the action with sublime discipline. This guarantees at minimum a basic success on the roll unless the action is truly impossible or blocked by overwhelming narrative conditions. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Martial Spirit.name: Martial Spirit
Martial Spirit.description: Devoted Spirit technique. When activated while Silas makes an attack, his conviction turns the momentum of battle into restorative force, healing the party when the attack is resolved. The healing should be narrated as morale, breath, divine-like resolve, and battle focus returning to Silas and his allies. Costs 10 stamina and 0 mana unless a specific scene rule overrides it.

Ravager's Will.name: Ravager's Will
Ravager's Will.description: You suck air deep into your lungs and force stamina through your body. Your muscles strain, your grip tightens, and for a short duration your body surges with brutal strength. Best used before a charge, heavy weapon strike, grapple, guard break, or overpowering maneuver.
Ravager's Will.usageInstructions: For 3 rounds, improve Strength-based attacks, heavy weapon damage, grapples, charges, shoves, guard breaks, and forceful Ravager maneuvers.

Battlecry.name: Battlecry
Battlecry.description: You unleash a roar that sunders enemy nerve. This is not ordinary shouting; it throws breath, posture, killing intent, and Sublime force into a concussive challenge that shakes wavering opponents and disrupts formations.
Battlecry.usageInstructions: Use Intimidation and Sublime Arts. Nearby enemies who can hear the cry may suffer shaken morale, hesitation, formation disruption, or penalties to resist pressure; stronger enemies may resist but should still feel the force.

Shield of Dominion.name: Shield of Dominion
Shield of Dominion.description: Invoke Dominus through a light-forged defensive ward around yourself or one ally. For a short duration, the shield absorbs or negates incoming damage before it reaches the target.
Shield of Dominion.usageInstructions: Defensive priest spell. Use to protect one target for 3 rounds. It can absorb, negate, or reduce incoming damage before wounds are applied, but it is not permanent invulnerability and can be overwhelmed by extreme force.

Blessing of Will.name: Blessing of Will
Blessing of Will.description: Invoke Elyra to strengthen courage, focus, and resistance against fear, despair, intimidation, domination, and corruptive influence.
Blessing of Will.usageInstructions: Ally support priest spell. For 5 rounds, improve allied resolve, morale, concentration, and resistance to fear, despair, coercion, domination, and corruptive pressure.

Seal Wound.name: Seal Wound
Seal Wound.description: Invoke Liriel's life-preserving grace to close an open wound, stop bleeding, and soothe immediate pain. This stabilizes and preserves life, but does not repair broken bones, regrow lost tissue, restore organs, or fully reverse severe internal damage.
Seal Wound.usageInstructions: Emergency priest healing. Use to close wounds, stop bleeding, reduce shock or pain, and prevent immediate death. Closed wounds do not always mean the body is fully healed; medicine, rest, alchemy, or Dracomantic regeneration may still be required.

Arcane Rebuke.name: Arcane Rebuke
Arcane Rebuke.description: Invoke Drakath's discipline to strike, disrupt, or suppress hostile magic, dark mana, curses, unstable spellwork, or corrupting supernatural effects with controlled sacred light.
Arcane Rebuke.usageInstructions: Anti-magic and anti-corruption priest spell. Use against hostile spellwork, curses, dark mana, unstable rituals, supernatural corruption, or profane effects. May deal light damage when used offensively.

Consecration.name: Consecration
Consecration.description: Lay hands and recite the prayers of the pantheon, calling sacred light across the surrounding area to purify, heal, and repel that which would bring harm to your flock.
Consecration.usageInstructions: Major priest ability.
