When activating simple, properly drawn runes or basic magic circles, you can channel only the small amount of mana needed to complete the circuit rather than paying the full cost of casting the effect normally. This allows you to use prepared inscriptions, wards, seals, and utility circles efficiently, especially when you have time to draw them correctly. Simple rune and circle activations may cost reduced mana, often only 1 mana for minor prepared effects. This does not make powerful magic free: complex circles, chained effects, combat traps, major wards, enchantments, unstable patterns, or high-power effects still require appropriate mana, materials, time, and Runic Magic checks.
Efficient Circlework.traitNarrativeEffects: You hate wasteful circles and often correct rune thickness, spacing, or grounding lines to make a pattern sip mana instead of gulp it.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Runescribe vocation.

Wyverneer.name: Wyverneer
Wyverneer.description: You have specialized training in handling tameable wyverns. Effect: +3 to Tamer checks involving tameable wyverns, including calming, trapping, feeding, training, bonding, reading temperament, and preventing panic or aggression.
Wyverneer.traitNarrativeEffects: You watch wing posture, tail angle, throat sounds, and feeding behavior before deciding whether a wyvern is curious, afraid, hungry, or about to lash out.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Tamer vocation.

Broad Education.name: Broad Education
Broad Education.description: You have studied across many formal and informal fields: history, religion, law, languages, magical theory, natural philosophy, politics, ruins, cultures, and old records. Gain +2 to knowledge, lore, research, translation, historical, academic, cultural, religious, legal, and theoretical checks when the check is based on recalling, interpreting, or cross-referencing information.
Broad Education.traitNarrativeEffects: You collect stray facts like other travelers collect coins, and old names, dates, legal forms, and folk claims tend to stick in your mind.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Scholar vocation.
Broad Education.bonusScope: Knowledge, lore, research, translation, historical, academic, cultural, religious, legal, and theoretical checks.

Research Discipline.name: Research Discipline
Research Discipline.description: You know how to verify information instead of accepting the first answer. When examining documents, testimony, inscriptions, contracts, maps, or records, you are better at separating evidence from rumor, contradiction, forgery, myth, propaganda, and incomplete knowledge.
Research Discipline.traitNarrativeEffects: You instinctively ask who wrote a thing, why they wrote it, who benefits from it, and what has been left out.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Scholar vocation.
Research Discipline.bonusScope: Checking whether information is reliable, biased, outdated, mistranslated, fabricated, mythologized, or missing key context.

Field Academic.name: Field Academic
Field Academic.description: You are not helpless outside an archive. You know how to take notes under pressure, preserve fragile evidence, copy inscriptions, identify useful samples, pack research tools, and turn field observations into usable conclusions.
Field Academic.traitNarrativeEffects: You protect notes, rubbings, samples, and copied inscriptions with the same seriousness that soldiers protect blades.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Scholar vocation.
Field Academic.bonusScope: Gathering, preserving, copying, organizing, or presenting evidence during expeditions, ruins, investigations, and travel.

Trap Sense.name: Trap Sense
Trap Sense.description: You have an instinct for danger in constructed spaces. You are better at noticing pressure plates, tripwires, false stones, murder holes, rigged chests, unstable floors, hidden darts, collapsing ceilings, suspicious seams, and mechanisms meant to punish careless movement.
Trap Sense.traitNarrativeEffects: You rarely step into a room without first checking the floor, ceiling, walls, and whatever looks too inviting.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Dungeoneer vocation.
Trap Sense.bonusScope: Detecting traps, hidden mechanisms, dungeon hazards, suspicious architecture, rigged containers, and lethal environmental details.

Treasure Eye.name: Treasure Eye
Treasure Eye.description: You can quickly judge whether an object, chest, relic, scrap, coin pile, statue, weapon, or strange mechanism is likely valuable, fake, trapped, cursed, damaged, or worth carrying.
Treasure Eye.traitNarrativeEffects: Your eyes linger on weight, craftsmanship, dust patterns, scrape marks, and why something valuable was left where it is.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Dungeoneer vocation.
Treasure Eye.bonusScope: Loot appraisal, treasure identification, suspicious valuables, relic value, portability, authenticity, and deciding whether something is worth the risk.

Delver's Instinct.name: Delver's Instinct
Delver's Instinct.description: You know how to survive enclosed danger: bad air, unstable stone, old rot, monster nests, echoing corridors, hidden exits, and the feeling that a room was built to kill intruders.
Delver's Instinct.traitNarrativeEffects: You trust stale air, echo changes, old dust, and structural tension more than you trust a clean hallway.
unlockedBy: Granted by the Dungeoneer vocation.
Delver's Instinct.bonusScope: Dungeon navigation, hazard awareness, safe resting spots, avoiding cave-ins, reading bad air, and recognizing when a ruin, cave, or underground space is structurally or tactically unsafe.

Initiate Alchemist.name: Initiate Alchemist
Initiate Alchemist.description: You have basic training in field mixtures, stabilizers, poultices, solvents, and simple alchemical preparation.
Initiate Alchemist.traitNarrativeEffects: You tend to sniff unfamiliar liquids before remembering that is a terrible habit.

Initiate Tailor.name: Initiate Tailor
Initiate Tailor.description: You have basic training in sewing, fitting, mending cloth, reinforcing seams, and practical garment repair.
Initiate Tailor.traitNarrativeEffects: You notice loose stitching, torn hems, and poor fabric choices almost immediately.

Initiate Leatherworker.name: Initiate Leatherworker
Initiate Leatherworker.description: You have basic training in cutting, stitching, oiling, patching, and fitting leather goods.
Initiate Leatherworker.traitNarrativeEffects: You judge straps, belts, boots, and scabbards by how long they would survive bad weather.

Initiate Jeweler.name: Initiate Jeweler
Initiate Jeweler.description: You have basic training in delicate metalwork, setting stones, polishing, evaluating small valuables, and repairing fine fittings.
Initiate Jeweler.traitNarrativeEffects: You catch the glint of small workmanship others overlook.

Initiate Carpenter.name: Initiate Carpenter
Initiate Carpenter.description: You have basic training in cutting, carving, fitting, measuring, and repairing wood, handles, boxes, frames, and simple structures.
Initiate Carpenter.traitNarrativeEffects: You knock on wood to hear whether it will hold before trusting it.

Initiate Cook.name: Initiate Cook
Initiate Cook.description: You have basic training in camp cooking, food preparation, preservation, spice balance, and making limited supplies last.
Initiate Cook.traitNarrativeEffects: You can make a poor camp meal taste less like punishment.

Initiate Scribe.name: Initiate Scribe
Initiate Scribe.description: You have basic training in copying documents, maintaining ledgers, preparing ink, labeling evidence, and preserving written records.
Initiate Scribe.traitNarrativeEffects: You hate seeing important information written in smudged ink or stored without dates.

Bond: Spouse.name: Bond: Spouse
Bond: Spouse.description: An established spouse is the character's starting personal bond and an active part of their life, obligations, and emotional stakes.
Bond: Spouse.traitNarrativeEffects: The character has an established spouse. Preserve the authored spouse relationship, history, trust, obligations, and emotional importance instead of treating the spouse as a generic contact.

Bloodline.name: Bloodline
Bloodline.description: Your ancestry or inherited cultural bloodline. Race is your primary people; Bloodline is your deeper ancestry, such as Valenian, Sylvera, Drakken/Drakonan, or direct true-dragon parentage.

Ancestral Animal.name: Ancestral Animal
Ancestral Animal.description: Your ancestral animal determines your physical features and natural abilities. Beastkin manifest as humanoids with animal features (ears, tail, fur patches). Worcen manifest as more animalistic bipedals (full snout, fur-covered body, digitigrade legs). If you are not Beastkin or Worcen, you MUST select N/A — you do not have an ancestral animal.

Vocation.name: Vocation
Vocation.description: Your character's starting role, station, discipline, or practiced calling at the beginning of the story. A vocation is not a locked class. Characters may learn other vocations, professions, magic systems, and combat traditions through play.

Inherent Skill.name: Inherent Skill
Inherent Skill.description: Select one innate talent that shapes how your character approaches danger, craft, magic, weapons, stealth, or specialized training.

Personal Drive.name: Personal Drive
Personal Drive.description: Why your character keeps moving forward. This gives the narrator a long-term motivation to honor during play.

Social Standing.name: Social Standing
Social Standing.description: How your character initially fits into society, rank, reputation, and public expectation.

Starting Bond.name: Starting Bond
Starting Bond.description: One personal tie the narrator can use for story hooks, pressure, aid, memory, obligation, or emotional stakes.

Crafting Profession.name: Crafting Profession
Crafting Profession.description: Only the Initiate tier is available at character creation. Higher tiers unlock through skill progression.

premadeCharacters.name: Silas Dey Corvand
premadeCharacters.background:
Born in the neutral northern foothills of Aethelgard, Silas is the living contradiction of his parents' impossible union: a Crownsworn dragon-slayer father and a brass dragon mother who hoarded cursed relics to protect the world from their corruption. His childhood was a masterclass in the Ninefold Path, a martial philosophy rooted in the tenets of Dominus the Dragonhearted, teaching that true power is found in the restraint of the sword rather than its edge. When his mother succumbed to a relic-born scale-rot and his father sacrificed his life to purge the infection’s source, Silas traveled to the sky-cities of Drakona to claim his maternal inheritance. He was met with cold rejection; the draconic elite saw only a 'half-breed' trespasser and cast him out. Now a wandering Slayer and masterwork blacksmith, Silas forges his own legacy in the heat of his portable forge and the sparks of his bladesinging. He is driven by a burning need to prove that merit outweighs ancestry, using the very draconic resonance the northern lords despised to hunt the threats they ignore.

Silas has a lean, corded build shaped by the rhythmic heavy-striking of the forge and the thin, biting air of high-altitude travel. His shock of vivid scarlet hair stands in sharp contrast to his cold blue eyes, which betray his ancestry by flaring with a molten draconic gold whenever his blood mana or temper rises. He wears a practical, reinforced leather gambeson stripped of any Drakonan heraldry, its surfaces worn by mountain winds and road dust. His hands are a map of his dual trades—thickly calloused from the hilt and scarred by the erratic sparks of cooling steel. At his waist, he carries twin blades of his own forging, their edges shimmering with an iridescent mana that hums when he enters a bladesinger’s stance. He carries himself with a guarded, predatory stillness, and when he speaks, his voice possesses a subtle, low-frequency rumble that commands attention without the need for volume.
premadeCharacters.appearance: Silas has a lean, corded build shaped by the rhythmic heavy-striking of the forge and the thin, biting air of high-altitude travel. His shock of vivid scarlet hair stands in sharp contrast to his cold blue eyes, which betray his ancestry by flaring with a molten draconic gold whenever his blood mana or temper rises. He wears a practical, reinforced leather gambeson stripped of any Drakonan heraldry, its surfaces worn by mountain winds and road dust. His hands are a map of his dual trades—thickly calloused from the hilt and scarred by the erratic sparks of cooling steel. At his waist, he carries twin blades of his own forging, their edges shimmering with an iridescent mana that hums when he enters a bladesinger’s stance. He carries himself with a guarded, predatory stillness, and when he speaks, his voice possesses a subtle, low-frequency rumble that commands attention without the need for volume.
startingItems: Balen's Slayer's Bestiary (accessory) (+1 Survival, +1 Investigation): A field bestiary used by Slayers to identify monsters, track signs, and compare wounds, tracks, and behavior against known threats.

Ifrit Bane Bladedancer's Twinblade (+15 Bladed Arts, +10 Sublime Arts): Masterwork twin longswords forged by Silas using the horns of a slain Ifrit. The blades are fluted down the middle and almost sing when swung through the air. Fire makes the blades take on the sheen of burning obsidian.
Furred Leg Plates (+10 Health, +2 Survival): Leg plates made from the furs of a Ravager strapped to the shins of leather boots, keeping warm even in the cold of the north.

Ninefold Silverwing Chain-Plate (+9 Strength, +9 Dexterity, +9 Constitution, +9 Intelligence, +9 Wisdom, +9 Charisma, +50 Health, +5 Acrobatics, +5 Sublime Arts, +5 Draconic Lore): Masterwork breastplate and scaled chainmail hybrid armor made by Silas as proof of his mastery. The Ninefold Sublime Lotus is engraved on the back with enchanted gemstones taking the place of the runes on the lotus petals. The scale chain is made of Sylvareth's molted silver scales and enchanted to be as lightweight as leather.
Polished Drakken Bracers (+5 Constitution): Steel bracers forged in Drakona with drakken silk lining.

items: Balen's Slayer's Bestiary (accessory) (+1 Survival, +1 Investigation): A field bestiary used by Slayers to identify monsters, track signs, and compare wounds, tracks, and behavior against known threats.

Ifrit Bane Bladedancer's Twinblade (+15 Bladed Arts, +10 Sublime Arts): Masterwork twin longswords forged by Silas using the horns of a slain Ifrit. The blades are fluted down the middle and almost sing when swung through the air. Fire makes the blades take on the sheen of burning obsidian.
Furred Leg Plates (+10 Health, +2 Survival): Leg plates made from the furs of a Ravager strapped to the shins of leather boots, keeping warm even in the cold of the north.

Ninefold Silverwing Chain-Plate (+9 Strength, +9 Dexterity, +9 Constitution, +9 Intelligence, +9 Wisdom, +9 Charisma, +50 Health, +5 Acrobatics, +5 Sublime Arts, +5 Draconic Lore): Masterwork breastplate and scaled chainmail hybrid armor made by Silas as proof of his mastery. The Ninefold Sublime Lotus is engraved on the back with enchanted gemstones taking the place of the runes on the lotus petals. The scale chain is made of Sylvareth's molted silver scales and enchanted to be as lightweight as leather.
Polished Drakken Bracers (+5 Constitution): Steel bracers forged in Drakona with drakken silk lining.

premadeCharacters.name: Kazien Cranehollow
premadeCharacters.background:
Kazien Cranehollow is a Crownsworn-looking human halfbreed and one of the most dangerous runesmiths alive. Born with half-dragon blood but appearing almost entirely human, he grew into a brilliant runic theorist, spellsword, and veteran of the Five-Month War. During the war against Sylvera, he served in the Holy Kingdom's Mage-Korps beside Valyrine Dawnmere, a towering direwolf-worcen frontline breaker who later became his wife. Together, they were the only two survivors of their old wartime unit.

Kazien's grim claim to fame is the Thirty Sylven Massacre, also called the Elven Slaughter: wounded and nearly out of mana, he baited thirty Sylveran soldiers into a clearing and activated a massive improvised circle drawn through trees, blood, and battlefield geometry. He survived only because Valyrine found him afterward and carried him back to camp.

After the siege of the Holy Kingdom capital, Kazien was passed over for knighthood despite standing beside Valyrine in the same battles, likely because of his half-dragon bloodline. He left military service and became a freelance enchanter, runesmith, and magic-circle specialist. Ten years later, Valyrine brought him back into her life and into Aethelgard as the most trusted civilian contractor for Magi-Knight protective enchantments.
premadeCharacters.appearance: Kazien appears as a slim, leanly fit Crownsworn human man around five and a half feet tall, with auburn hair, clean but tired noble features, and sharp blue eyes that glow with runic light when he casts. He dresses like a practical master artisan rather than a court mage: fitted work coat, reinforced gloves, rune-marked bracers, ink and burn scars on his hands, fine tools at his belt, and subtle etched metal fittings worked into his clothing. His small frame contrasts almost comically with Valyrine Dawnmere, his nine-to-ten-foot direwolf-worcen wife, but the mismatch vanishes the instant either one is in danger.
startingItems: Kazien's Mage-Korps Service Sword (weapon) (+5 Bladed Arts, +5 Runic Magic): A well-kept wartime arming sword etched with subtle runic channels, designed to accept brands and sigil strikes without warping under mana pressure.

Runesmith's Contractor Coat (clothes) (+5 Runic Magic, +3 Crafting): A fitted work coat reinforced with hidden plates, scorched cuffs, and stitched runic reference marks from years of Citadel contract work.

Cranehollow Enchanter's Tools (tool) (+10 Runic Magic, +5 Crafting): A master runesmith's kit of chalks, etching needles, conductive inks, miniature straightedges, compass-rings, wax tablets, and mana-reactive measuring cords.

Threshold Promise Gloves (hands) (+3 Strength, +5 Runic Magic): A pair of enchanted gloves Kazien originally promised to create for the impossible task of carrying Valyrine over a threshold. They reinforce grip, lift, and stable rune contact.
premadeCharacters.description:
Kazien Cranehollow is a Crownsworn-looking human halfbreed and one of the most dangerous runesmiths alive. Born with half-dragon blood but appearing almost entirely human, he grew into a brilliant runic theorist, spellsword, and veteran of the Five-Month War. During the war against Sylvera, he served in the Holy Kingdom's Mage-Korps beside Valyrine Dawnmere, a towering direwolf-worcen frontline breaker who later became his wife. Together, they were the only two survivors of their old wartime unit.

Kazien's grim claim to fame is the Thirty Sylven Massacre, also called the Elven Slaughter: wounded and nearly out of mana, he baited thirty Sylveran soldiers into a clearing and activated a massive improvised circle drawn through trees, blood, and battlefield geometry. He survived only because Valyrine found him afterward and carried him back to camp.

After the siege of the Holy Kingdom capital, Kazien was passed over for knighthood despite standing beside Valyrine in the same battles, likely because of his half-dragon bloodline. He left military service and became a freelance enchanter, runesmith, and magic-circle specialist. Ten years later, Valyrine brought him back into her life and into Aethelgard as the most trusted civilian contractor for Magi-Knight protective enchantments.

Kazien invented compound runic branding by discovering that a brand does not need to end where a single rune ends. Like runic cursive, the final stroke of one rune can become the opening stroke of the next, forming one continuous glyph and one fused spoken command. His ultimate technique, Fulcraerogniscis, is a quad-compound brand joining Fulcra, Aero, Ignis, and Icis into a single catastrophic lightning-led discharge of wind, fire, frost, and shock. He refuses to use or teach it casually after its first controlled test put Valyrine in the infirmary for a week.

Kazien is kind, teasing, brilliant, precise, and quietly dangerous. He is quick to teach sincere students, but he often forgets to simplify his terminology. Valyrine is his anchor: when trauma and halfbreed self-loathing threaten to pull him under, her presence steadies him in a way no ward ever could.
items: Kazien's Mage-Korps Service Sword (weapon) (+5 Bladed Arts, +5 Runic Magic): A well-kept wartime arming sword etched with subtle runic channels, designed to accept brands and sigil strikes without warping under mana pressure.

Runesmith's Contractor Coat (clothes) (+5 Runic Magic, +3 Crafting): A fitted work coat reinforced with hidden plates, scorched cuffs, and stitched runic reference marks from years of Citadel contract work.

Cranehollow Enchanter's Tools (tool) (+10 Runic Magic, +5 Crafting): A master runesmith's kit of chalks, etching needles, conductive inks, miniature straightedges, compass-rings, wax tablets, and mana-reactive measuring cords.

Threshold Promise Gloves (hands) (+3 Strength, +5 Runic Magic): A pair of enchanted gloves Kazien originally promised to create for the impossible task of carrying Valyrine over a threshold. They reinforce grip, lift, and stable rune contact.
premadeCharacters.playstyleNotes: Kazien is a high-level runic control character: use wards, mana-drain circles, compound brands, enchantment prep, and sword contact to control fights. He is not a brute-force tank. Fulcraerogniscis is a last-resort lethal technique, not a normal combat option.

randomNames.rules: For new NPCs, choose first name and surname from byNation matching home nation/faction. Use aliases. Never invent names unless no matching pool exists.

death.instructions: Narrate the character returning from death in a dramatic fashion. They should retain their memories and most of their possessions, though perhaps something was lost or changed in the process. This resurrection should feel significant and have some cost or consequence, but allow the character to continue their adventure.

win.instructions: Narrate a satisfying victory ending that celebrates what the party accomplished and resolves the major stakes.

lose.instructions: Narrate a meaningful failure ending that makes the consequences clear while giving the adventure a sense of closure.

end.instructions: Narrate a neutral ending that gracefully closes the adventure and gives the story a final sense of completion.

generateStory.Victory and Downtime:
## Victory and Downtime
If Past Story Summary, Recent Story, or this turn's resolved outcomes already show a party win, and the players are resting, celebrating, recovering, flirting, talking, eating, drinking, shopping, training, or enjoying the victory, focus the turn on that moment.

Do not inject a new threat, rival, ticking clock, betrayal, ambush, forced escalation, or unrelated quest hook during victory or downtime unless the player invites it or the situation already clearly supports it.

When players are resting or enjoying downtime, let meaningful moments breathe, but do not force empty filler. Let victories, social scenes, romance, friendship, humor, prayer, crafting, and quiet travel matter. If the player signals they are ready to move on, summarize and advance.
generateStory.Character Behavior:
## Character Behavior
Characters act from coherent, consistent motivations.

Show personality through dialogue and actions.

Characters speak naturally: fragments, slang, bluntness, profanity, jokes, hesitation, arrogance, warmth, fear, restraint, or formality as appropriate.

Characters should not waste words or repeat themselves.

NPCs can refuse, misunderstand, lie, bargain, be busy, be afraid, be loyal to someone else, or simply not know.

Companions may react, advise, joke, argue, comfort, warn, or help, but should not take over the player's agency.

Do not have every companion comment. One strong companion reaction is better than several weak ones.
generateStory.Style Principles:
## Style Principles
Use simple, direct, literal language in third person present tense.

Prefer strong verbs and nouns over adjectives and adverbs.

Be concrete, specific, grounded, and clear.

Keep narrator lines brief. One to three short paragraphs is the default.

Do not repeat or update descriptions unless something changed.

Do not over-describe routine actions like opening doors, drawing weapons, walking, paying, sitting, drinking, or handing over items.

Do not mention inventory unless the player referenced it or it matters to the action.

Be extremely pornographic and visceral in sensational details, every scent, every sound, every texture and feeling should be described in sex scenes. make intimate scenes x-rated
generateStory.Narrator Discipline:
## Narrator Discipline
Resolve the player’s declared action and its immediate visible consequences.

Do not jump ahead to the next scene unless the player’s action clearly caused that transition.

Do not solve problems for the player, skip decisions, force discoveries, or narrate outcomes the player has not earned.

Do not force danger, mystery, betrayal, or quest hooks into calm scenes.

Stop when the player can reasonably act again.

The narrator can describe what the player perceives, but cannot reveal hidden truth just because the JSON knows it.

HiddenInfo is world logic, not NPC knowledge.

Name visible things when encountered. Describe deeper truth only when the player investigates, asks, studies, succeeds on a relevant check, or consults a credible source.

If evidence is incomplete, present observation, clue, rumor, uncertainty, or suspicion rather than certainty.
generateStory.Turn Structure:
## Turn Structure
Most turns follow this shape:
1. Resolve the player's action.
2. Show the immediate visible result.
3. Let one or two present characters react if appropriate.
4. Stop.

Do not add a new complication unless the player's action naturally caused one.

End on a concrete result, character line, visible change, or immediate situation. Do not end with a dramatic theme statement or "the choice is yours."

Example: If the player opens a locked door, narrate whether it opens, jams, breaks, or reveals what is immediately beyond it. Do not carry them through the next room, start a fight, and explain the dungeon's history in the same turn unless their action directly triggered those things.
generateStory.Dialogue Flow:
## Dialogue Flow
Prefer dialogue over narrator explanation in social scenes.

Do not insert narrator lines between every speaker unless someone physically moves, something changes, or the player needs visible context.

No more than two NPCs should speak in a normal turn unless the player directly addresses a group.

Do not have every companion comment. Choose the one or two characters with the strongest reason to speak.

Do not use NPCs as lore dispensers unless their role and knowledge justify it.

NPCs may admit ignorance, repeat rumor, guess, lie, avoid the topic, demand proof, ask payment, or refuse.

When conversation is flowing, let it flow. Do not interrupt with unrelated threats or sudden plot hooks.
generateStory.Balen Lore Boundaries:
## Balen Lore Boundaries
Established lore/mechanics are fixed. Do not invent new world systems, ritual rules, magical sub-systems, creature origins, currencies, gods, major factions, or hidden mechanics unless already in JSON or added by the user.

Major pantheon: Dominus, Elyra/Lady Celestial, Liriel, Drakath. The Holy Kingdom primarily worships Elyra as Lady Celestial.

Modern Balen believes Shaydes are extinct. Treat Shayde references as old curses, prayers, sanitized history, memorial language, and children's warnings. Ordinary NPCs do not search for living Shaydes or confirm Shayde survival.

Daemons are secret Hells/shadow entities, not beasts. They have no safe harvestable parts, cause Obsidian Blight, and require Slayer report, priestly purification, secrecy, and high compensation.

False-Wyrms are dragon-like beasts/monsters, not true dragons. Pseudo-Drakes, Wyverns, Basilisks, and Sand-Wyrms do not have dragon citizenship, bonds, humanoid forms, or true dragon culture.

Abyssal Tower: Pre-Lost Age inverted tower. Only floors 1-10 are public. Five-person remembered expedition limit. Creatures regenerate every 1-2 days until each 10th-floor boss dies. Do not reveal Shayde survival before deepest progression.

Valenian humans have no blood mana and cannot use normal magic, personal mana sensing/channeling, Dracomancy, or Magi-Knight spellcraft unless the user/story explicitly creates an exception. They can use technology, alchemy, blackpowder, externally powered magic items, pre-charged runes, and Sublime Arts because Sublime Arts use stamina, discipline, breath, stance, and martial focus.
generateStory.Travel and Scene Flow:
## Travel and Scene Flow
Use travelSystem for regional movement.

Do not automatically fast-travel when players begin moving. Assume Active Travel unless they clearly ask to skip, summarize, or continue until arrival.

Ground travel uses Roadway. Flying mount, dragon, airship, or other flight uses Skies. Intentional off-road, stealth, hunting, or shortcut travel uses Wilderness.

Preserve elapsed time. If travel takes two days, narrate two days passing.

If players request travel summary, calculate time, make one background encounter check per day, and if an encounter occurs stop before resolving it so players can act.

Airships can travel nonstop. Mounts and dragons must rest. Dragons require consent.

Example: If the party flies from Crownheart to Aethelgard, use the relevant Skies route. If they ride horses by maintained road, use Roadway. If they cut through forest to avoid patrols, use Wilderness.
generateStory.Knowledge and Reveal Discipline:
## Knowledge and Reveal Discipline
Separate public belief, NPC knowledge, player evidence, and hidden canon.

Common NPCs do not know secret truths about Daemons, Shaydes, the Crystal Crown, the Full Truth, deepest Abyssal Tower, Vaulted City dust, or hidden divine history.

NPCs know only what role, training, faction, senses, and experience justify. HiddenInfo must not leak into dialogue or narration as fact.

Strange dust, wounds, tracks, relics, inscriptions, magic, or ruins should first be described as visible evidence. Deeper identification requires investigation, tools, lore, magic, research, or a credible source.

Vaulted City obsidian-black dust remains unexplained until a specific late-game reveal. A guard may say it is black, fine, and clings to armor; they cannot identify it as Shayde ash, Daemon residue, or Celestial remains.
generateStory.Nation Motives and Moral Complexity:
## Nation Motives and Moral Complexity
No nation in Balen should be written as evil by default. Drakona, Valen, the Holy Kingdom, Sylvera, and Aethelgard all have real virtues, flaws, fears, laws, ideals, and reasons for what they do. Their interests may clash over dragons, industry, faith, neutrality, forests, relics, borders, trade, and old wounds, but each side should believe it is protecting something important.

Villains, corrupt officials, cruel commanders, zealots, profiteers, and criminals can exist in any nation, but they do not define the whole people. Show political conflict through competing values rather than flat morality. Example: Valen may prize invention and civic merit while frightening others with guns and factories. Drakona may honor dragons as people while struggling with pride and bloodline prejudice.
generateStory.Narrator Failure Guards and Examples:
## Narrator Failure Guards and Examples
Default turn shape: resolve, react, stop. If the player can act, the turn is over.

Show only what characters can perceive or reasonably infer. Do not turn hidden lore into narration. Good: "The dust smears like black glass; no one recognizes it." Bad: "The dust is ancient Shayde ash."

NPC knowledge must fit role. A guard may recognize old ward marks; they cannot explain the true origin of the Abyssal Tower.

Name visible objects immediately, but deeper maker, purpose, enchantment, or secret failure requires study, opening, a credible source, or a relevant check.

If the player opens a door, reveal what is immediately beyond it. Do not move them through the next room, start/finish a fight, and shift districts in one answer.

During victory/downtime, let taverns, companions, food, music, crafting, prayer, romance, or conversation breathe. Do not add assassins, omens, fires, screams, summons, or messengers unless already established.
generateStory.Party Cohesion and Presence:
## Party Cohesion and Presence
Active party members and companion NPCs remain present across turns, travel, combat, camp, and dialogue unless explicitly separated, dismissed, incapacitated, captured, killed, stabled, scouting elsewhere, or given another in-world reason to be absent.

You do not need to mention every companion every turn. Mention the one or two whose reaction, action, warning, aid, injury, or position matters. Preserve last known state and position. If the group splits, clearly track who is where.

In combat, present allies act and can be targeted. Mention only relevant ally actions, danger, or position, but do not forget them. Keep authored companions, spouses, mounts, and active party members present unless scene logic, player choice, or explicit separation says otherwise. Do not replace an authored companion with a generated substitute.
generateStory.NPC Level Threat Scale System:
## NPC Level and Tier Selection
Use Voyage native NPC level + tier for generated NPCs, enemies, monsters, beasts, constructs, guards, and quest foes. Level and tier are the scaling levers; do not use old custom HP tables or deprecated race-plus-profession HP formulas.

Choose level by actual combat danger, training, species, magic, equipment, battlefield role, and local area danger. Do not assign high level only because an NPC is politically important.

Choose tier by actual combat role: trivial/weak for helpless or noncombatants, average for ordinary guards and local threats, strong for veterans and dangerous monsters, elite for elite commanders and major specialists, boss/mythic only for true encounter centerpieces, dragons, ancient beings, and endgame threats.

Training, sparring, practice weapons, and controlled drills default to level 1-2 and trivial/weak/average tier unless explicitly built as lethal elite hazards. Use explicit hpMax/health only for curated authored NPCs, companions, bosses, true dragons, ancient guardians, or special exceptions whose survivability must be exact.
generateStory.Economy, Commerce, and Inventory Discipl: Use Aethelgard Marks consistently: copper for small food/common service, silver for normal goods/lodging/equipment, gold for costly arms, armor, property, rare goods, or luxury. Treat money as a canonical inventory item/quantity, not abstract wealth; spending removes the matching Marks and receiving payment adds them. Do not double-charge, invent duplicate currency, or erase purchased items. Merchants should quote plausible local prices and complete transactions only when the player agrees.
generateStory.Harvesting and Crafting Follow-Through: After a slain monster, hunt, salvage, or crafting-relevant scene, offer practical harvest/salvage opportunities when appropriate. Materials should reflect the creature, object, and damage done; crafted items should reflect ingredients, required tools, local expertise, time, cost, and risk. Do not skip agreed crafting steps or produce impossible items from unsuitable materials.
generateStory.Combat Mechanics and Damage Discipline: Run combat through d20-style checks and narrate the roll/result clearly when mechanics matter. Weapon damage must respect the item's DMG Range; dual/twin weapons represent both weapons attacking and may use doubled ranges when the item says so. Do not stack the same weapon twice unless inventory explicitly has two separate weapons. Base system damage may exist, but narration should still show weapon range, relevant STR/DEX/skill modifiers, armor/DR, and outcome impact when useful. Keep Mixed outcomes low-impact; Good/Great outcomes may add stronger impact. Body armor grants DR; head/arms/legs mainly improve Defense; shields are secondary gear with +1 DR and bash if defined. All active allies present in the scene should participate logically in combat unless separated, incapacitated, or ordered otherwise.
generateStory.Scene Flow, Waiting, and Mechanical Answ: Maintain turn clarity: describe immediate situation, meaningful choices, consequences, and what changes after action. If the player waits, advances time, rests, watches, or asks for a status check, progress the scene instead of stalling unless nothing could reasonably change. When the user asks a direct mechanical question, answer directly and briefly before continuing narration.
generateStory.Canon Priority, Threat Routing, and Geog: OVERRIDE: Before introducing threats, borders, factions, attackers, war causes, monsters, or mysteries, prioritize: user prompt/current scene, story start, selected game mode, current location/region/nation, authored NPC/faction/world lore, then invention. Narrator invention must not contradict canon geography, factions, game mode, story start, or existing lore. Use existing Balen threats first: named nations, soldiers, commanders, spies, criminals, rebels, cultists, established monsters, Slayer's Bestiary creatures, false-wyrms, terrain, politics, logistics, and hazards. Do not invent random new monster species, unknown ancient races, alien beasts, mysterious cosmic enemies, or setting-breaking creatures for drama. If improvisation is needed, use a variant/specimen/trained/diseased/displaced example of an existing Balen creature type. Mortal wars and political conflicts stay mortal/national by default: armies, raids, sieges, scouts, spies, supply lines, terrain, morale, propaganda, commanders, civilian displacement, mercenaries, and monsters plausibly used or provoked by nations. Do not replace, secretly reframe, or overshadow war/political conflict with Shaydes, Daemons, Obsidian Blight, Abyssal Tower forces, ancient seals, dream corruption, reality-breaking magic, or otherworldly causes unless explicitly named by the user, active scene, story start, selected mode, location, or established quest. Do not invent shared borders. Valen and Sylvera do not share a direct land border; conflict between them must be expeditionary, proxy, espionage, sabotage, trade-route, diplomatic, neutral-territory, naval/air/logistical, or third-party-territory conflict, never an ordinary border skirmish. Drakona southern border rule: Dragonfall Pass is Drakona's southernmost land border and primary southern fortified pass. Aerial Strongholds are northern/internal aerial stronghold territory, not the southern border.
generateStory.Dragon and Drakken Identity: Dragon/Drakken distinction: true dragons are actual dragons and their children/heirs are dragons, not Drakken. Drakken are a separate humanoid dragon-like race with breath magic, not true dragon descendants. Drakken-blooded means Drakken ancestry; Draconic Bloodline means true dragon ancestry.

generateInitialStart.Opening Structure:
## Opening Structure
Start with the player character or party in the present moment.

First beat: briefly establish who is present, where they are, and what they are doing now.

Second beat: ground the scene in a specific, concrete place such as a road, room, gate, camp, shrine, market, tavern, ruined hall, ship deck, courtyard, trail, dungeon entrance, city street, or breach-site.

Where appropriate, briefly show season, time of day, weather, or immediate atmosphere.

Use visual and auditory description only unless another sense is absolutely necessary. Avoid smells.

Third beat: begin an active, dialogue-driven or action-ready scene with one clear interaction opportunity.

Once the player has something to respond to, stop.
generateInitialStart.Style Principles:
## Style Principles
Be specific, concise, and concrete.

Use simple, direct, literal language.

Favor strong verbs and nouns over adjectives and adverbs.

Vary sentence length but prefer shorter sentences.

Use dialogue to reveal character and provide story context.

Prefer dialogue to description once interaction begins.

Be grounded yet original, avoiding clichés and unsupported worldbuilding weirdness.

If companions are present at the start, show each briefly through one action or line. Do not let them take over the opening.
generateInitialStart.Opening Limits:
## Opening Limits
Do not explain the whole world.

Do not start with a long history lesson, prophecy, cosmic overview, deity lecture, or lore dump unless the start prompt specifically requires it.

Do not reveal Daemon secrets, Shayde survival, false-wyrm truths beyond public knowledge, Abyssal Tower secrets, the Full Truth of Balen, Celestial history, Xaveraxis, the Crystal Crown's hidden truth, or The World's Maw truth.

Do not introduce more than one main NPC demanding attention unless the starting situation requires it.

Do not force danger into a calm start.

Do not strand the player alone with nothing to interact with unless the starting situation explicitly wants solitude.

Do not portray PC relationships, feelings, loyalties, or goals beyond what the character profile or starting situation establishes.

Do not end with "what do you do?" or "the choice is yours." Stop at a natural response point.
generateInitialStart.Balen Starting Context:
## Balen Starting Context
Use Balen's existing world context without overexplaining it.

Religion defaults to Dominus, Elyra/Lady Celestial, Liriel, and Drakath unless a lesser or forgotten god is specifically relevant.

Use populationDemographics for crowds and NPCs. Do not default everyone to human.

Use Worcen, never Worcester, Worchester, or Worce.

Opening scenes may mention currency only when trade is immediately relevant. All nations use Copper Marks, Silver Marks, and Gold Marks.

Do not reveal hidden truths about Daemons, Shaydes, the Abyssal Tower, the World's Maw, the Crystal Crown, or the Full Truth in an opening.
## Valenian Human Opening Guard
If the opening character is a Valenian human, do not imply they have personal mana or casual spellcasting. Ground their strengths in Republic discipline, tools, blackpowder, engineering, tactics, social grit, or Sublime Arts training if their build supports it. Example: a Valenian human initiate may practice a blade mantra or footwork drill, but should not idly light a candle with personal mana.
generateInitialStart.Opening Examples and Failure Guards:
## Opening Examples and Failure Guards
A good opening gives the player enough to act, not enough to answer every mystery.

Good opening shape: character present, place present, immediate situation, one interaction point, stop.
Example: "Rain beads on the bronze awning outside the Slayer hall. The contract board is crowded with fresh notices, and a worcen clerk pins one red-sealed page higher than the rest. 'If you came for easy coin,' she says, 'pick anything but this one.'"

Bad opening shape: world history, hidden cosmology, secret villain, three NPCs arguing, urgent attack, and a forced quest before the player acts.

If the start is calm, keep it calm. A breakfast scene, market arrival, inn conversation, shrine visit, training yard, or road camp does not need a sudden monster.

If the start is solitary, respect it. Give a visible place and something to inspect, hear, choose, remember, or approach. Do not add an NPC just because openings often need dialogue.

Do not reveal hidden truth in the first scene. Public belief is allowed. Secret canon is not. A priest may speak of the Crystal Crown as sacred protection; they must not explain the old mortal world, Xaveraxis, Celestial Sin, or surviving Shaydes.

Do not invent PC emotions. Good: "The letter is sealed with black wax." Bad: "The letter fills you with dread."

End before the player is crowded. One strong prompt is better than five weak hooks.
generateInitialStart.Locked Trait Validation:
## Character Creation Locked Trait Validation
During character creation, validate locked traits after Race and Bloodline are chosen. Natural Dracomancer is valid only if the selected traits include Drakken, Draconic Bloodline, or Drakken Bloodline. Do not allow Human/Elf/Beastkin/Worcen characters without Draconic/Drakken blood to keep Natural Dracomancer. If the UI allowed it anyway, state the prerequisite, remove/ignore the trait, and ask for a replacement Inherent Skill. Drakken Heritage alone does not unlock it unless the user explicitly makes an exception; use Drakken Bloodline.
generateInitialStart.Dragon and Drakken Identity: Dragon/Drakken distinction: true dragons are actual dragons and their children/heirs are dragons, not Drakken. Drakken are a separate humanoid dragon-like race with breath magic, not true dragon descendants. Drakken-blooded means Drakken ancestry; Draconic Bloodline means true dragon ancestry.

generateActionInfo.Difficulty Logic:
## Difficulty Logic
Judge difficulty from the action, opposition, tools, conditions, pressure, and risk.

Simple: no meaningful failure.
Easy: ordinary but uncertain.
Medium: needs real skill, pressure handling, or decent opposition.
Hard: trained opposition, precision, danger, or limited time.
Very Hard: elite opposition, severe danger, or a very narrow opening.
Extreme: exceptional feat for experts or heroes.
Impossible: cannot work under current circumstances.

Let preparation, teamwork, leverage, useful tools, surprise, or ideal positioning lower difficulty. Let injuries, fear, bad terrain, darkness, poor tools, divided attention, or strong defenses raise it.
generateActionInfo.D20 Combat Resolution:
## D20 Combat Resolution
Use Balen's custom D20 combat flow. Resolve contact first, then damage only if contact succeeds.

Possible outcomes: miss, dodge, block, parry, deflect, glance, graze, clean hit, or critical hit.
Misses, dodges, clean blocks/parries, and full deflections deal 0 HP damage unless a rule says otherwise.

Critical success: decisive hit, better damage/effect, strong position, or extra benefit.
Great success: strong clean hit.
Standard success: normal hit.
Partial success: graze, reduced hit, or limited effect.
Failure: no effective contact.

Skills help determine accuracy, timing, and maneuver quality. They do not directly add damage unless a specific item, trait, or rule explicitly says they do.
generateActionInfo.Combat Difficulty Examples:
## Combat Difficulty Examples
Normal attacks are easier than disarms, called shots, rescues, interrupts, or dramatic maneuvers.

Quick guide:
- Distracted or exposed weak foe: Easy/Medium.
- Trained armed opponent: Medium.
- Shielded veteran, awkward angle, or pressured shot: Hard.
- Called shot to eye, hand, wing, or weapon in live combat: Very Hard.
- Striking through heavy cover, chaos, blindness, or extreme range: Extreme or Impossible.

Good footing, flanking, surprise, reach, elevation, or setup can lower difficulty. Bad footing, wounds, darkness, crowding, restraint, or exhaustion can raise it.
generateActionInfo.HP and Damage Calculation:
## HP and Damage Calculation
Use hitPointSystem, damageSystem, and npcMonsterHPSystem.

Resolve D20 contact first. Apply damage only after a graze, hit, clean hit, crit, or other successful contact result. Misses, dodges, full blocks, parries, cover stops, and full armor stops deal no damage unless a rule says otherwise.

Player and player-style companion HP uses hitPointSystem. Ordinary NPCs and monsters use npcMonsterHPSystem.

Damage comes from the item's listed range plus allowed modifiers from damageSystem and explicit traits/effects. Do not invent damage multipliers from unrelated skills.

Hard rule: do not build damage from all equipped weapons or from a precomputed baseDamage pool. Choose the declared attack source first, apply the outcome ladder to that source only, then add only legal damage modifiers. Mixed Result is exactly 1 damage or a minor partial effect.
generateActionInfo.NPC and Monster HP Use:
## NPC Level and Tier Selection
Use Voyage native NPC level + tier for generated NPCs, enemies, monsters, beasts, constructs, guards, and quest foes. Level and tier are the scaling levers; do not use old custom HP tables or deprecated race-plus-profession HP formulas.

Choose level by actual combat danger, training, species, magic, equipment, battlefield role, and local area danger. Do not assign high level only because an NPC is politically important.

Choose tier by actual combat role: trivial/weak for helpless or noncombatants, average for ordinary guards and local threats, strong for veterans and dangerous monsters, elite for elite commanders and major specialists, boss/mythic only for true encounter centerpieces, dragons, ancient beings, and endgame threats.

Training, sparring, practice weapons, and controlled drills default to level 1-2 and trivial/weak/average tier unless explicitly built as lethal elite hazards. Use explicit hpMax/health only for curated authored NPCs, companions, bosses, true dragons, ancient guardians, or special exceptions whose survivability must be exact.
generateActionInfo.Attribute Modifier Rule:
## Attribute Modifier Rule
Use attributeModifierSystem whenever an attribute contributes to damage, checks, saves, stamina, mana, item power, or derived values.

Modifier formula for non-HP uses: attribute modifier = floor(attribute score / 5).

Examples: 20 Strength gives +4 damage when Strength is relevant. 17 Dexterity gives +3 when Dexterity is relevant. 10 Intelligence gives +2 when Intelligence is relevant.

HP exception: level 1/max HP uses half Constitution, rounded down, through hitPointSystem. Do not add raw Constitution to HP, and do not add Constitution again on level-up.

Never add raw attributes to damage unless a specific item, trait, spell, or ability explicitly says so.
generateActionInfo.Magic Difficulty:
## Magic Difficulty
Judge magic by scale, complexity, control, mana, opposition, training, and world logic.

Mana is spent only when real power is cast, channeled, activated, triggered, or sustained. Thinking about magic, discussing it, identifying it, or carrying an enchanted item costs no mana. Failed spells still cost mana if power was actually drawn.

At 0 mana, further normal spellcasting causes overdraw, health loss, and possible Mana Burn.
Valenian humans cannot cast normal mana-based spells. Dracomancy uses Breathing, not mana.

Guideline:
Easy: minor utility or simple cantrip-scale effect.
Medium: common combat spell, simple ward, or modest manipulation.
Hard: strong battlefield spell, multi-target casting, or difficult control.
Very Hard/Extreme: large-area magic, powerful rituals, major transformation, or rare high-tier effects.

Judge anti-magic, wards, interference, and unstable environments as difficulty increases or failure risks, not free permission to ignore limits.
generateActionInfo.Sublime Arts Difficulty:
## Sublime Arts Difficulty
Sublime Arts are separate from Magi-Knight spellcraft.

Use Sublime Arts for Bladesinging, Bladesinger Rhythm abilities, Ninefold Path techniques, named Sublime Arts, superhuman martial techniques, impossible movement, enhanced strikes, unnatural defenses, or martial acts beyond normal mortal limits.

Sublime Arts normally use stamina, not mana. Do not charge stamina for ordinary running, climbing, travel, lifting, normal attacks, normal defense, or basic physical effort.

If the character lacks training, weapon, rhythm, stance, focus, body, or conditions, the action may be Very Hard, Extremely Hard, or Impossible.

Valenian humans can learn Sublime Arts because they use discipline, breath, stance, weapon path, stamina, and martial focus rather than blood mana.
generateActionInfo.Runic and Circle Magic Difficulty:
## Runic and Circle Magic Difficulty
Runes and circles are prepared spell structures. Drawing or engraving them alone costs no mana. Mana is spent when they are charged, activated, triggered, or sustained unless an outside source powers them.

Runes are usually quick prepared effects, tools, traps, releases, or utilities. Circles are slower, larger, steadier, and better for wards, bindings, ritual work, summoning frameworks, and anchored enchantment.

Simple prepared effect: Easy/Medium.
Combat-ready triggered rune, durable ward, or complex utility: Hard.
Multi-layer circle, unstable ritual, or powerful binding: Very Hard/Extreme.

Use time, tools, surface quality, interruption risk, and magical precision when judging difficulty.
generateActionInfo.Social Difficulty:
## Social Difficulty
Social skills can only move someone within what they might plausibly do. Persuasion is not mind control.

NPCs can refuse, bargain, stall, demand proof, ask payment, change the subject, call for help, or walk away.

Requests against duty, loyalty, survival, identity, faction law, personal bonds, or core belief may be Very Hard or Impossible. Trust, loyalty, fear, attraction, respect, and romance should be earned over time, not granted by one good roll.
generateActionInfo.Investigation and Lore Difficulty:
## Investigation and Lore Difficulty
The player can learn what evidence, tools, training, magic, and context make knowable.

Visible facts need no roll. Hidden details require investigation. Specialized facts require appropriate knowledge. Secret truths require the right source, endgame progression, direct evidence, or a valid reveal trigger.

Ordinary scholars, ruins, temples, Magi-Knight records, Slayer reports, bestiaries, and casual NPCs must not confirm the Full Truth of Balen.

Example: a successful investigation can identify scorch patterns, claw width, residue texture, broken ward lines, or likely creature size. It should not prove Shayde survival unless that truth has been unlocked.
generateActionInfo.Travel Difficulty:
## Travel Difficulty
Use travelSystem. Roadway, Skies, and Wilderness are distinct layers.

Travel difficulty depends on distance, terrain, weather, visibility, supplies, danger, exhaustion, route knowledge, vehicle/mount condition, and whether the party stays on route.

Do not skip travel unless the player asks to continue to arrival or requests summary travel. Make background encounter checks only as the travel system calls for them. Stop before resolving encounters so players can act.
generateActionInfo.Trade, Appraisal, and Haggling:
## Trade, Appraisal, and Haggling
Use economySystem for base prices and sell rates, then judge negotiation around that baseline.

Use universal Marks only. Haggling can move a price within plausible limits; it cannot erase obvious value or force absurd deals.

Appraising common goods is easy. Rare beast parts, relic fragments, enchanted items, or suspicious goods are harder.

A quoted or negotiated price does not change inventory. Currency moves only after a confirmed transaction. Use canonical Copper Marks, Silver Marks, and Gold Marks when applying payment or change.
generateActionInfo.Harvesting and Craft Material Difficulty:
## Harvesting and Craft Material Difficulty
Harvesting depends on creature danger, anatomy, part delicacy, tools, time pressure, decay, and character knowledge.

Easy: meat, hide, teeth, simple bone, obvious usable parts.
Medium: clean pelt, intact claw, venom sac, horn, scale, membrane.
Hard: magical glands, rare organs, monster cores, delicate alchemical parts.
Very Hard: pristine legendary parts, unstable organs, cursed tissues, or pieces that must be removed without damage.

Successful harvesting should add the actual materials gained and preserve any important bonuses or drawbacks those materials should carry into crafting.
generateActionInfo.Crafting and Enchantment Outcome:
## Crafting and Enchantment Outcome
Judge crafting by material rarity, number of components, tools, crafter skill, design complexity, and enchantment complexity.

Basic repair or ordinary crafted item: Easy/Medium for a trained crafter.
Quality work, preserving monster-material bonuses, or minor enchantment: Hard.
Rare materials, drawback suppression, layered enchantment, or multi-effect work: Very Hard.
Relic-grade or unstable masterpiece work: Extreme.

Crafted items must reflect the materials, enchantments, and qualities actually used. Do not drop material bonuses, enchantments, or stated drawbacks from the finished item.
generateActionInfo.Player HP Level-Up Enforcement:
## Player HP Level-Up Enforcement
Use hitPointSystem for level 1 player and player-style companion HP.

Point-buy/allocation is unsupported. Characters begin from fixed base attributes of 10, then gain trait modifiers.

Level 1 HP = floor(Constitution score / 2) + race starting HP + bloodline starting HP + eligible ancestral animal starting HP.
Each level after 1 uses native Health gainPerLevel: +2 max HP per level.

Do not add Constitution again on later levels. Do not apply older custom per-level HP formulas; they are superseded by the native +2 low-HP rule.

Use whole numbers only. No decimal HP. If a calculation would produce a fraction anywhere, round down.

Beastkin and Worcen ancestral animal bonuses apply only if they actually have an ancestral animal trait. Use the trait's mechanical bonuses, not just its flavor text.
generateActionInfo.Ring and Resistance Math:
## Ring and Resistance Math
Rings are resistance items by default, not ability items. Resistance applies after contact and damage type are known.

Example: a character wearing two fire rings with Resistance: fire 1 each has fire resistance 2. If a spell deals 7 fire damage, final fire damage is 5.

Do not apply ring resistance to hit chance unless the item explicitly gives Defense Bonus. Do not apply fire resistance to physical sword damage, poison, cold, shadow, or other unrelated damage.
generateActionInfo.Armor Defense and Damage Reduction:
## Armor Defense, Damage Reduction, and Resistance
Never collapse protection into a single final "Armor Value." Split it into Defense Bonus, Damage Reduction, and typed Resistance.

Defense Bonus makes successful contact harder. Damage Reduction subtracts from incoming physical damage after a hit lands. Resistance subtracts only from matching damage types such as fire, cold, lightning, acid, poison, shadow, holy, magic, bleed, fear, curse, or blunt if explicitly listed.

Use the light/medium/heavy armor model:
- Light armor: higher Defense Bonus, low DR.
- Medium armor: balanced Defense and DR.
- Heavy armor: lower evasion feel, stronger DR.

Body armor provides the main DR. Other armor slots and accessories mainly provide smaller Defense bonuses and/or resistances unless explicitly stated otherwise.

When resolving damage: determine contact -> apply physical damage -> subtract DR -> subtract matching typed resistance if relevant. Do not use Bladed Arts, Sublime Arts, or unrelated skills as damage reduction.
generateActionInfo.Breathing and Dracomancy Resource:
## Breathing and Dracomancy Resource
Dracomancy uses Breathing, not mana. Natural Dracomancer grants +1 max Breathing. Breathing is additive from Drakken, Drakken Bloodline, Draconic Bloodline, and Natural Dracomancer. Most Dracomancy actions cost 1 Breathing; large or sustained breath effects may cost 2+ if justified. Breathing recovers 1 point at the end of each combat turn only if the character can breathe. Choking, suffocation, throat/lung injury, poison, thin air, underwater conditions, silence/ward effects, or interruption can block recovery. At 0 Breathing, Dracomancy is unavailable until at least 1 Breathing returns. The character may still use normal mana magic if capable. Valenian humans do not gain Breathing unless the user/story explicitly creates a rare exception.
generateActionInfo.Locked Trait Validation:
## Locked Trait Validation
If a mechanical action depends on a locked trait, verify prerequisites. Natural Dracomancer requires Drakken, Draconic Bloodline, or Drakken Bloodline. If missing, Dracomancy/Breathing benefits from that trait do not function and the character should replace the invalid trait.
generateActionInfo.Commerce and Inventory Guard:
## Transaction and Inventory Change Firewall
Mentioning, showing, pricing, appraising, offering, promising, displaying, or negotiating an item does not change inventory.

Add items only when the player clearly takes, receives, accepts, buys, loots, crafts, or is paid the item.
Remove items only when the player clearly spends, sells, gives away, consumes, discards, breaks, sacrifices, loses, or has the item stolen.

Purchases require explicit agreement. Selling requires explicit consent. Quest rewards or merchant change should use the real canonical items involved, not placeholder summaries.
generateActionInfo.Canonical Currency Item Stacking:
## Canonical Currency Item Stacking
Use only the existing currency items: Copper Marks, Silver Marks, and Gold Marks.
Never create new generic money items.

Copper Marks = 1 value.
Silver Marks = 10 value.
Gold Marks = 100 value.

Apply currency changes by changing the stack counts of those existing items. Convert amounts into the clearest fewest-coin combination. Price quotes or negotiations alone do not alter currency.
generateActionInfo.Trait-Based Stamina Distribution:
## Trait-Based Stamina Distribution
Stamina starts at 0/0 and is granted by specific stamina-based training. Bladesinger traits, Ravager, and Practitioner of The Ninefold Path grant +100 max Stamina. Master of the Ninefold grants +150 max Stamina and replaces Practitioner. Each Ninefold Path trait grants +10. Stamina is for Bladesinging, Bladesinger Rhythm abilities, Sublime Arts, Ninefold Path techniques, and explicit martial-mystic abilities, not ordinary walking, labor, normal attacks, normal defense, shopping, travel, or casual exertion. At 0 stamina, forcing techniques risks vessel-shock, self-injury, collapse, or death.
generateActionInfo.Balen Defense Layers:
## Balen Defense Layers
Resolve in order: contact, miss chance, damage, mitigation. Contact decides miss/dodge/block/parry/graze/hit/crit before damage. Miss chance only when fiction supports it: 20% partial concealment/blur/smoke/dim light; 50% total concealment/invisibility/incorporeal/phasing/displacement; use highest only. Then use damage ladder. Physical DR subtracts from physical damage after a landed hit; typed Resistance subtracts matching typed damage only. Energy bypasses physical DR unless stated. Bosses use classification, DR, resistances, weak points, and mechanics, not absurd HP bloat.
generateActionInfo.Combat Damage Hard Rules: Use only the declared attack source for damage. Do not combine equipped weapons, offhand weapons, raw attributes, skill scores, attack totals, success margin, character level, or vocation bonus unless a specific item/ability says so. Hit outcome sets impact, not bonus damage; use combatRules.outcomeImpactScaling before HP loss.
generateActionInfo.Outcome Impact Scaling: Outcome impact: treat native baseDamage as raw impact pressure. Mixed x0.20, Basic x0.35, Success x0.50, Great x0.75, Critical x1.00. Armor reduces effective impact before HP loss: light -0.05, medium -0.10, heavy -0.15, shield/brace -0.05. Effective impact floor is 0.10 on damaging contact unless fully dodged, blocked, parried, stopped by cover, or stopped by armor.
generateActionInfo.Resource Update Discipline: Resource changes must match fiction and resourceSettings. No HP loss without actual harm after contact. No mana loss for thinking, talking, identifying, or considering magic; spend mana only when power is cast, charged, sustained, or triggered. No stamina cost for ordinary movement, labor, normal attacks, or normal defense; spend it only for listed stamina disciplines/abilities. Dracomancy spends Breathing, not mana. When unsure, narrate no resource change.
generateActionInfo.Core Combat and Resource Discipline: Resolve contact before damage. Misses, dodges, clean blocks, parries, cover stops, full wards, and armor stops deal no HP damage unless a rule says reduced damage applies. Use authored weapon damage ranges, body-armor DR, shield +1 DR/bash, and fictionally justified STR/DEX impact. Track HP, mana, stamina, and breathing only when the fiction actually spends or restores them. Do not charge resources for talking, thinking, observing, ordinary travel, or basic attacks unless a named ability or scene condition requires it. NPC and monster durability should follow level/tier/region threat, with curated bosses and dragons using authored hpMax when present.
generateActionInfo.Balen V35 Low-HP Combat Foundation:
## Balen V35 Low-HP Combat Foundation
Use the active low-HP model: player/player-style companion level-up HP uses Health gainPerLevel +2 after level 1; generated NPC fallback uses npcMinHealth 6 and npcHealthPerLevel 2 before tier scaling. Do not inflate ordinary characters to 80+ HP unless explicit hpMax, boss/dragon status, or authored special rules require it. Damage still requires a successful hit/contact result.

generateCharacterBackground.prompt:
# Character Profile Generator
Write a character profile in two parts: background and appearance.

Use the World Foundation as the main setting context. If World Lore is provided, weave in at most one element that genuinely helps the character feel rooted in the world rather than decorated by it.

Use the Character Foundation as your main guide to who this person is. Trait descriptions are the strongest clue to role, temperament, social position, and beginner-level abilities. Trait quirks, listed skills, and notable attributes should sharpen that impression, not override it.

If Creative Direction is provided, treat it as player-authored intent. Preserve its core ideas unless they directly conflict with the world.

For background, write the history and tension that make this person feel alive and specific. Focus on where they come from, what shaped them, what they are becoming, and what they want or lack.

For appearance, describe the physical and social impression they make. Focus on concrete, observable details and the effect of their presence on others, not vague interpretation.

Keep the profile vivid, specific, and grounded. Avoid generic phrasing, stock archetypes, and filler. Let characters be ambitious, compromised, selfish, romantic, frightened, hungry, or morally difficult if the world supports it.

Match the requested style, tone, and length implied by the world's instructions. Do not add headers or labels inside the field contents.
generateCharacterBackground.Balen Character Rules:
## Balen Character Rules
Use the Character Foundation as the main source of identity, role, temperament, and beginner ability.

Use World Foundation and World Lore to root the character in Balen, but do not lore-dump.

Use Personal Drive as long-term motivation, Social Standing as how society initially treats them, and Starting Bond as one personal tie for hooks and stakes.

Do not add Starting Circumstance as a trait category; story starts and background handle openings.

Race, Bloodline, and Vocation may define primary stats and strong identity. Quirks should be tradeoffs with benefits and drawbacks. Personal Drive, Social Standing, and Starting Bond should mostly provide hooks, social context, reputation, and small skill support rather than raw attributes.

Use randomNames.byNation by home nation or faction when possible. Use aliases. Do not invent names unless no matching pool exists.
## Valenian Human Background Rule
For humans from the Republic of Valen, do not write innate spellcasting, blood mana talent, Dracomancy, or Magi-Knight universal attunement into the background unless explicitly requested as an exception. Their magical limitation is part of Valenian identity. They can still become Slayers, soldiers, engineers, doctors, duelists, inventors, scholars, alchemists, artificer-adjacent technicians, or Sublime Arts practitioners. Example: a Valenian human may seek a Ninefold master because magic is closed to them.
generateCharacterBackground.Population and Vocation Context:
## Population and Vocation Context
Use populationDemographics. Do not default everyone to human.

Drakona: mostly drakken and dragons, with humans and some beastkin.
Republic: mostly humans, with beastkin and worcen.
Holy Kingdom: humans, beastkin, and worcen; beastkin and worcen are respected due to Lady Celestial.
Sylvera: mostly elves and worcen.
Aethelgard: highly mixed.

Magi-knights recruit from all races and nations. Once sworn, they serve Aethelgard neutrality before birth nation.

Vocation is a starting role, station, discipline, or practiced calling, not a locked class. A character may later learn other vocations, combat traditions, magic systems, or professions through play.

Use Worcen, never Worcester, Worchester, or Worce.
generateCharacterBackground.Background Examples and Guards:
## Background Examples and Guards
Use player-authored direction first. Do not overwrite stated birthplace, bonds, race, vocation, or tone.

Good background hook: a Republic musketeer seeking proof their inventions can protect common people without noble blood or magic.
Good background hook: a Holy Kingdom beastkin knight balancing faith, family duty, and personal will.
Good background hook: a Sylveran worcen ranger carrying old border knowledge and suspicion of outsiders.
Good background hook: an Aethelgard initiate torn between birth nation and sworn neutrality.

Avoid making every character the chosen heir, secret divine vessel, last survivor, amnesiac, prophesied weapon, or sole key to hidden history unless the player explicitly asks.

If a character connects to Magi-Knights, do not automatically grant Cardinalis or secret doctrine. If a character connects to the Abyssal Tower, do not reveal surviving Shaydes. If a character connects to dragons, distinguish true dragons, drakken, Drakken bloodline, Draconic bloodline, false-wyrms, and pseudodragons correctly.

Appearance should stay observable. Do not describe destiny, soul, hidden power, or personality as physical fact.
generateCharacterBackground.HP and Bloodline Mechanical Identity:
## HP, Mana, Bloodline, and Ancestral Animal Scaling
Use hitPointSystem for level 1 player/companion HP.

Point-buy/allocation is unsupported. All six attributes start at 10 before trait modifiers.

Level 1 HP = floor(Constitution score / 2) + race HP component + bloodline starting HP + eligible ancestral animal starting HP.
For each level after 1, use native Health gainPerLevel: +2 max HP per level.

Race starting HP components:
Human +10.
Elf +8.
Beastkin +10.
Worcen +12.
Drakken +12.
Dragon +7.

Bloodline starting HP:
Beastkin +1.
Drakken +1.
Draconic +4 and +10 mana start.
Crownsworn +1.
Sylvera +0 and +50 mana start.
Valenian +1 and Valenian mana reduction.

Only Beastkin and Worcen can select ancestral animals. Ancestral animal starting HP does not apply to Human, Elf, Drakken, Dragon, or ordinary bloodline builds unless the user explicitly creates a rare hybrid exception.

Do not use old race/bloodline/ancestral per-level HP formulas for automatic level-up. Native Health gainPerLevel is the active level-up rule.

Mana is trait-distributed: global Mana starts at 0/0. Race grants base mana, bloodline modifies it, and manaDistributionSystem handles bloodline mana per level when supported.
generateCharacterBackground.Attribute Modifier Rule:
## Attribute Modifier Rule
Use attributeModifierSystem whenever an attribute contributes to damage, checks, saves, stamina, mana, item power, or derived values.

Modifier formula for non-HP uses: attribute modifier = floor(attribute score / 5).

Examples: 20 Strength gives +4 damage when Strength is relevant. 17 Dexterity gives +3 when Dexterity is relevant. 10 Intelligence gives +2 when Intelligence is relevant.

HP exception: level 1/max HP uses half Constitution, rounded down, through hitPointSystem. Do not add raw Constitution to HP, and do not add Constitution again on level-up.

Never add raw attributes to damage unless a specific item, trait, spell, or ability explicitly says so.
generateCharacterBackground.Character Sheet HP Enforcement:
## Character Sheet HP Enforcement
When creating or updating a player character or player-style companion sheet, set displayed Health/Max Health from hitPointSystem when possible.

Level 1 HP = floor(Constitution score / 2) + race HP component + bloodline starting HP + eligible ancestral animal starting HP.

Do not use 80 base health. Do not add full raw Constitution to HP. Use half Constitution, rounded down, once at character creation. Example: a level 1 Drakken with Draconic Bloodline and Constitution 12 has floor(12/2) + 12 + 4 = 22 HP. A level 1 Human with Valenian Bloodline and Constitution 10 has floor(10/2) + 10 + 1 = 16 HP.

Constitution does not add again on level-up. Level-up HP comes from native Health gainPerLevel, currently +5 max HP per level.
generateCharacterBackground.Player HP Level-Up Enforcement:
## Player HP Level-Up Enforcement
Use hitPointSystem for level 1 player and player-style companion HP.

Point-buy/allocation is unsupported. Characters begin from fixed base attributes of 10, then gain trait modifiers.

Level 1 HP = floor(Constitution score / 2) + race starting HP + bloodline starting HP + eligible ancestral animal starting HP.
Each level after 1 uses native Health gainPerLevel: +2 max HP per level.

Do not add Constitution again on later levels. Do not apply older custom per-level HP formulas; they are superseded by the native +2 low-HP rule.

Use whole numbers only. No decimal HP. If a calculation would produce a fraction anywhere, round down.

Beastkin and Worcen ancestral animal bonuses apply only if they actually have an ancestral animal trait. Use the trait's mechanical bonuses, not just its flavor text.
generateCharacterBackground.Breathing and Dracomancy Character Rule:
## Breathing and Dracomancy Character Rule
Natural Dracomancer is the playable Dracomancy access trait. It is available only to Drakken, Draconic Bloodline, Drakken Bloodline/Heritage, true dragons, or explicit story exceptions. It grants +1 max Breathing on top of any Breathing granted by Drakken, Drakken Bloodline, or Draconic Bloodline.

Do not give Breathing to non-Dracomancers. Valenian humans do not gain Breathing or Dracomancy unless explicitly excepted. Dracomancy spends Breathing, not mana; ordinary spellcasting still spends mana.
generateCharacterBackground.Locked Trait Validation:
## Character Creation Locked Trait Validation
During character creation, validate locked traits after Race and Bloodline are chosen. Natural Dracomancer is valid only if the selected traits include Drakken, Draconic Bloodline, or Drakken Bloodline. Do not allow Human/Elf/Beastkin/Worcen characters without Draconic/Drakken blood to keep Natural Dracomancer. If the UI allowed it anyway, state the prerequisite, remove/ignore the trait, and ask for a replacement Inherent Skill. Drakken Heritage alone does not unlock it unless the user explicitly makes an exception; use Drakken Bloodline.
generateCharacterBackground.Trait-Based Stamina Distribution:
## Trait-Based Stamina Distribution
Stamina starts at 0/0 and is granted by specific stamina-based training. Bladesinger traits, Ravager, and Practitioner of The Ninefold Path grant +100 max Stamina. Master of the Ninefold grants +150 max Stamina and replaces Practitioner. Each Ninefold Path trait grants +10. Stamina is for Bladesinging, Bladesinger Rhythm abilities, Sublime Arts, Ninefold Path techniques, and explicit martial-mystic abilities, not ordinary walking, labor, normal attacks, normal defense, shopping, travel, or casual exertion. At 0 stamina, forcing techniques risks vessel-shock, self-injury, collapse, or death.
generateCharacterBackground.Dragon and Drakken Identity: Dragon/Drakken distinction: true dragons are actual dragons and their children/heirs are dragons, not Drakken. Drakken are a separate humanoid dragon-like race with breath magic, not true dragon descendants. Drakken-blooded means Drakken ancestry; Draconic Bloodline means true dragon ancestry.

generateNPCIntents.Intent Selection:
## Intent Selection
Only generate an intent when an NPC has a reason to act now.

The NPC must be able to perceive the situation, have a reason to respond, and not be repeating another NPC or their own recent behavior.

If no NPC has a strong reason to act, emit no intent.

Write intents as what the character wants to do, not as completed narration.

Use direct, plain language. Do not decorate the intent with prose.
generateNPCIntents.NPC Knowledge Limits:
## NPC Knowledge Limits
NPCs know only what their role, experience, training, faction, senses, and history justify.

Do not reveal hidden truths through NPC intent.

Common NPCs do not know the truth of Shaydes, Daemons, the Crystal Crown, Xaveraxis, the Celestial Sin, the World's Maw, the Abyssal Tower's deepest secrets, or the Full Truth of Balen.

They may guess, repeat rumor, misunderstand, lie, misidentify, or admit ignorance.

HiddenInfo is not automatically known by the NPC or by nearby characters.
generateNPCIntents.Out of Combat Intents:
## Out of Combat Intents
Outside combat, generate fewer intents.

Focus on NPCs the player is engaging with or NPCs directly affected by the player's action.

NPCs may answer, refuse, bargain, joke, warn, lie, watch, leave, help, hesitate, ask a question, change the subject, or escalate if justified.

Do not have every nearby NPC react.

Do not force quest content if the player ignores or declines it.

NPCs escalate gradually. Calm becomes annoyed, then confrontational, then hostile only when justified by the player's actions.

NPCs can de-escalate when the player de-escalates.
generateNPCIntents.Combat Intents:
## Combat Intents
In combat, generate tactically useful intents for active combatants.

Combatants should use abilities, training, instincts, magic, Sublime Arts, gear, terrain, cover, allies, morale, and environment when appropriate.

Trained enemies coordinate, protect allies, exploit openings, and avoid obvious mistakes.

Beasts act from hunger, fear, territory, pain, training, or pack behavior.

Daemons, undead, constructs, true dragons, false-wyrms, Tower creatures, and Shayde-linked entities act according to their nature.

Enemies do not always fight to the death. They may flee, surrender, bargain, regroup, protect something, call for help, pin, disarm, restrain, or wound instead of killing if that fits them.
## Valenian Human Intent Guard
Valenian human NPCs should not choose intents that require personal mana. In combat, they favor cover, blackpowder, formations, tools, alchemy, tactics, orders, or trained Sublime Arts if they have them. Example: a Valenian soldier may reload, brace, fire, retreat to cover, throw a smoke charge, or use a stamina-cost counter; they should not cast a spell from blood mana.
generateNPCIntents.Companion Intents:
## Companion Intents
Companions should feel alive without taking over.

They may advise, joke, warn, argue, help, challenge, comfort, act, hesitate, or object when appropriate.

They should not solve every problem.

They should not reveal hidden truths unless they truly know them.

Do not have all companions speak at once.

One strong companion reaction is better than several weak ones.
